I have a custom 2.6.20.11 kernel and have just upgraded from edgy to feisty.

Have a slight problem regarding device-mapper.

I have device mapper support compiled into my kernel so i can use truecrypt (i dont need it to boot).

When booting under feisty i get loads of these messages flooding all consoles which causes the system to hang during boot:

```

May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   21.169000] device-mapper: ioctl: 4.11.0-ioctl (2006-10-12) initialised: dm-devel@redhat.com
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: table: 254:0: linear: dm-linear: Device lookup failed
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: ioctl: error adding target to table
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: table: 254:0: linear: dm-linear: Device lookup failed
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: ioctl: error adding target to table
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: table: 254:0: linear: dm-linear: Device lookup failed
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.725000] device-mapper: ioctl: error adding target to table

May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.726000] device-mapper: ioctl: error adding target to table
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.726000] device-mapper: table: 254:0: linear: dm-linear: Device lookup failed
May 18 16:41:57 localhost kernel: [   27.726000] device-mapper: ioctl: error adding target to table
.
.
.
.

```

### Post by r_mano on 2007-05-23
Before all: thanks for the thread! The mehod using ```
make-kpkg
``` works ok.

BUT:

the problem with this method is that everytime you rebuild a kernel, you have to do a  ```
make-kpkg clean
```, and that signify that you have to recompile all. If you use a generic .config, this could be 4 hours on my poor laptop. Now, if you have made little changes, it should be possible to recompile the kernel in a much more rapid fahion, with the 
```
make ; make modules_install
``` method, and then manually install the new kernel.

My problems come at the moment of creating the initrd. Suppose I have compiled the kernel version 2.6.21.1-myk; after the abovementioned step I do

```
mkinitramfs 2.6.21.1-myk -o initrd.img
```

I then move al to the /boot director, run ```
/sbin/update-grub
```, and...

...the new kernel boots ok, but then the boot process stops with a BusyBox in the initramfs.

Is there anyone that can help me to create, manually, the correct initrd to boot the system? 


Thanks

### Post by GaryH on 2007-05-23
Hi Everybody,

Well my wireless is working just fine now; new kernel and all  :guitar:

I haven't gotten around to my modem yet. That's next.

So here's the skinny. When we change the kernel, we need to rebuild our restricted modules. Not all of them. Just the ones we need. I my case that was wireless and modem. Now to rebuild restricted modules we need their source, makefile, and lots and lots of other files too. Give yourself plenty of time to do this step. It is no small potato to find source, supporting files, and good instructions. Ubuntu is no help here. We're on our own. I found all the files and excellent instructions for my wireless at madwifi.org and for my modem at linmodems.org.  I extracted the files from both tarballs into /usr/src. It wasn't difficult. From the instructions I copied, pasted, and executed three lines of code. Wireless just came up and started running right off the bat!

Hope this helps the community and if anybody wants more details on building the Atheros wireless module let me know and I'll write a detailed howto.

Now off to get the old modem working again. That's going to be a bear.  I've still got more research to do. There's a brand new package out there for my modem called martian. The good news is it's kernel version independent. The bad news is it's still in the development phase. I'm tempted to try it anyway. Anybody out there know anything about martian?

Peace,
GaryH

### Post by GaryH on 2007-05-24
Hi darkjedi664,

I read somewhere in Ubuntu cyberspace a warning stating that rebuilding kernels, in almost all cases, should be left to kernel developers. I chose to disobey that warning because I'm a software engineer who enjoys tinkering with software and who enjoys web surfing for hours for answers to Linux questions. 

The downside of customizing your kernel is complete destruction of your system and the upside is little to no speed improvement and disk savings which on a modern hard drive are insignificant.

I noticed no speed improvement with my custom Feisty kernel as compared to the generic Feisty kernel when running glxgears.

That's just MHO.

Peace,
GaryH

---

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-05-24
> **darkjedi664 said:**
> I'm pretty new to Linux and after some reading, I can tell the advantages of compiling a new kernel, but what about the disadvantages?  Also, I tried compiling with k7smp (for my dual core Opteron), and it seemed to compile without incident.  Is that a correct revision?  I don't want to install it if it's not right.  I mean, I didn't have any issues with compiling.  Just a little scared to screw things up :P

if you would have read the guide you would have saw that the revision has nothing to do with the kernel at all. It is merely a naming thing. You don't even have to add a revision but then you might not know which kernel is the one you just compiled. Also, as long you have another kernel option to boot to in your menu.lst, you can always boot to that so it wouldn't screw up your system, it would merely just maybe not boot using your newly compiled kernel. GO FOR IT!

I changed the capital letters because I realized that this is construed as yelling and not just trying to point something out. so if you're offended I aologize and in know way meant for that to be read as me yelling that. It's just that many people have asked this same question.

### Post by glennric on 2007-05-26
> **GaryH said:**
> Hi darkjedi664,

I read somewhere in Ubuntu cyberspace a warning stating that rebuilding kernels, in almost all cases, should be left to kernel developers. I chose to disobey that warning because I'm a software engineer who enjoys tinkering with software and who enjoys web surfing for hours for answers to Linux questions. 

The downside of customizing your kernel is complete destruction of your system and the upside is little to no speed improvement and disk savings which on a modern hard drive are insignificant.

I noticed no speed improvement with my custom Feisty kernel as compared to the generic Feisty kernel when running glxgears.

That's just MHO.

Peace,
GaryH

You really have to do something wrong to completely destroy your system.  If you properly customize your kernel there are speed improvements.  Are you are basing the speed of your system purely on the output of glxgears?  That is a very incomplete benchmark of your system.  In addition there is hardware that will not work properly with the default Feisty kernel (e.g. many usb scanners due to a bug the usb suspend feature, and certain intel motherboards due to a bug in ACPI).

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-05-29
I would say that until you can solve the issue, just boot to the older kernel. The file you want to change is 
/boot/grub/menu.lst
you can use kate in kde and gedit in gnome. you'll have to use sudo to edit this file
once you're in it, you can simply change the default kernel that get's booted, this way you can leave the kernel installed until there is a fix for ya and also, you won't keep getting asked to update your kernel although there is a way to "hold" a package so it doesn't keep trying to upgrade itself. So once you're in the file, you want to change the default number (normally 0) to the number of the kernel you want booted. I am going to guess that will be number 2 since grub starts at 0 and normally also auotmiagically installs a recovery option of the newly installed kernel just like the older kernel, so the kernel 0 is the one that you can't boot, then kernel 1 is the recovery option for that one (it just boots to single user mode) so that would make the .15 kernel number 2. That should be it, then save the file and next time you boot up, your computer will automatically boot to the kernel that works. Also, while you're in there, you can always change whether or not the grub menu appears and for how long until the default kernel you chose is booted. This would allow to keep trying the new kernel once in a while. Just read thru the menu.lst file and you'll see where I am talking about, it saya something about grub menu display, and I htink it's either set at 0 or false or something liek that whcih means that the grub menu won't show up upon bootup but you may want it to show, then the other settings to look for is the amount of time that the default kernel boots, I think the default is 3 seconds, change that to 15 or even 30. THewn after making all changes, save the file and tehn next time you boot, you'll see you're changes in affect. Good luck.

### Post by GaryH on 2007-05-31
Hi Everybody,

Thanks to the MarvS and Jacques at linmodems.org my modem is now working with the custom kernel and life is beautiful. :guitar:

I decided to use the newer modem driver package, martian. If anybody is interested in details please see my post on [linmodems.org](linmodems.org).

What I learned:
[LIST=1]
[*]Restricted modules quit working after the module is customized.
[*]Ubuntu doesn't support  restricted-modules
[*]Agere DSP modems are supported at linmodems.org
[*]Wireless devices using the Atheos chip set are supported at madwifi.org
[*]It takes a long time to find everything necessary to rebuild and reinstall restricted-modules
[/LIST]

Peace,
GaryH

---

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-05-31
> **GaryH said:**
> Hi Everybody,

Thanks to the MarvS and Jacques at linmodems.org my modem is now working with the custom kernel and life is beautiful. :guitar:

I decided to use the newer modem driver package, martian. If anybody is interested in details please see my post on [email]discuss@linmodems.org[/email].

What I learned:
[LIST=1]
[*]Restricted modules quit working after the module is customized.
[*]Ubuntu doesn't support  restricted-modules
[*]Agere DSP modems are supported at linmodems.org
[*]Wireless devices using the Atheos chip set are supported at madwifi.org
[*]It takes a long time to find everything necessary to rebuild and reinstall restricted-modules
[/LIST]

Peace,
GaryH
1. The default restricted modules won't work once you try to use the source and compile your own module against the new custom kernel
2. Ubuntu does support restricted modules, I use both the Nvidia and the Atheros restricted modules within Feisty.
3. thanks for the tip
4. Atheros chips are supported out of the  box using the Restricted Modules that Ubuntu Provides. If you compile your own kernel that you'll need to compile this against the new kernel (please correct me if I am wrong)
5. I can imagine.

### Post by hyperair on 2007-06-01
My initrd is only 7.4 MB compared to the standard one (386) which is 8.1MB. Kernel I'm using is the most recent from feisty-security I reckon. I don't know why yours is so huge. To tell you the truth, that was my first time compiling a kernel, but I removed a lot of stuff in the xconfig. Maybe that's why? Anyway my kernel modules are pretty much the same size as the normal kernel. Perhaps it was a different configuration? What happens when you just use the original configuration of the standard kernel and compile? Do you still have the huge initrd problem?

By the way, the code I used is:
```

make oldconfig
make xconfig
sudo make-kpkg clean
sudo make-kpkg --initrd kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

```

Then I just install whatever debs come from it.

### Post by antiplex on 2007-06-01
thank you very much for your feedback, hyperair!

i didn't change much in the config compared to the original config, only disabled paravirtualization support and removed some intel-cpu specific options but its a nice idea to try to compile with the exactly original configuration, will do so as soon as i don't need any cpu power for other things.

i used almost the same commands as you did, except that 
- i manually copied the original configuration by 'sudo cp /boot/config-2.6.20-16-generic /usr/src/linux/.config' because i wanted to make sure the lastest config is used since i have these strange version-problems i described earlier, but as far as i understood, 'make oldconfig' would do pretty much the same if compile using the 2.6.20-16 source which is the case here.
- then i did ```
sudo -s
make clean
make-kpkg clean
make menuconfig
make-kpkg -initrd --revision=*<mytag>* kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

``` and finally as well installed the outcoming .deb's
nothing wrong so far, right?

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-06-01
> **GaryH said:**
> Hi dannyboy79,


Thanks for responding to my last. I'm glad to hear that Ubuntu does indeed support getting restricted-modules to work with custom kernels. Point me to the procedures, instructions, etc. and I'll be happy to test them out on my system with my modem and wireless and happily report my findings.

Peace,
GaryH
Gary, you've added words onto what I stated so I will make it clear what I said AND OR what I meant to say if you interpreted something else.

In my number 1 statement, I have already stated that the default restricted modules DO NOT work with a custom kernel. (UNLESS you follow this thread, [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=441013&page=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=441013&page=2))

My number 2 statement, I stated that Ubuntu supports restricted modules and that is all. (NOTE: all this is saying is that Ubuntu by default comes with restricted modules and they DO work. This mentions nothing about using a custom kernel.)

I stated that Ubuntu supports the Atheros Chips using restricted modules and that is all. (NOTE: again, refer back to the above note and you'll see what this is saying)

IF you compiled your own kernel, than you need to compile any restricted modules that you were using against the new kernel just as it states within the guide regarding the Nvidia restricted module. It doesn't state that regarding all restricted module BUT does state that you'll have to recompile the Nvidia Driver if you're using it. I know this can't be assumed for beginners and maybe we should have the author here add a statement about the restricted modules after a custom kernel install and maybe he can add the link I posted so people can try it out and see if that works instead of going thru all the trouble of compiling the Atheros chipset drivers from madwifi source if they don't need to and any other restricted modules for that matter.

Sorry if there was any confussion by my statements.

### Post by antiplex on 2007-06-01
ok now, i've compiled my kernel with exactly the original ubuntu-kernel's config found in /boot/config-2.6.20-16-generic but still everything was huge as described.

but luckily, one of the people who used to have similar issues and whose [thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=2761971") i just linked to replied pointing out what caused the whole thing and now it worked fine! everything is now about 10-20% smaller than the standard ubuntu ones... all running fine and i'm feeling good now :D

it further dawned on me that the sub-sub-revision of 3 in 2.6.20.3 might be completely correct and that i was probably just confused by the restricted modules packages which use a different revision number and were listed close to the other kernel stuff...

### Post by SirShaggy on 2007-06-07
***SOLVED***

Well, I went for it after reading about the kernel options online. I removed several things I knew I didn't need, added some stuff I need and went for it. From step one of the HOWTO to reboot took 43 minutes total! Thant was the easy part. I did indeed have lots of issues with NVIDIA drivers not working. I am currently in X using "nv" drivers. I downloaded the actual Nvidia 9755 drivers off the website into my home folder and installed Envy before rebooting just in case. I have tried both, they error no matter what I do. I have so many xorg.conf backups it's not funny! Please take a quick look at this, do you see any issues?

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 72.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "nVidia Corporation G71 [GeForce 7900 GS]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "nVidia Corporation G71 [GeForce 7900 GS]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "MultiGPU" "on"
    Option         "SLI" "on"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       15
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
    Option         "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection

That is after unpacking the driver from the website that will not boot.
Here is what I am using right now that does work:

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"nVidia Corporation G71 [GeForce 7900 GS]"
	Driver		"nv"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Generic Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	28-72
	VertRefresh	43-60
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"nVidia Corporation G71 [GeForce 7900 GS]"
	Monitor		"Generic Monitor"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1440x900" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

BusID is the only thing that pops out to me between the two.......I tried to just add it to my first attemp, it didn't work.
Everything else works, very well I might add and it's crisp. I didn't think it would be faster but it is. I want to keep it, I just want the nvidia drivers and SLI back!

SirShaggy

### Post by SirShaggy on 2007-06-07
For whatever reson, when I installed the nvidia driver, it did not remove/comment out "dri" in the modules. It took me a bit to find it, comment it out and try it. Since then, I have multi gpu and sli working with accelerated graphics on my new kernel! It is much faster at some things, the same on others. All in all, very worth it.

SirShaggy

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-06-15
> **xyz said:**
> Hi - I tried this but:


...and when trying to install:

What else can I try? Thnx for your help.

well you could use xconfig like the guide suggests here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=84174&highlight=compile+kernels](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=84174&highlight=compile+kernels)

or you could try to install the packages, it's telling you which ones it's missing. when you do a search and those exact criteria return results, you should always remove a little and try search again, when I do 
sudo aptitude search libglade, I get all this:p   libglade-cil                    - CLI binding for the Glade libraries
v   libglade-dev                    -
v   libglade-gnome-dev              -
p   libglade-gnome0                 - library to load .glade files at runtime (G
p   libglade-gnome0-dev             - development files for libglade (Gnome widg
p   libglade-java                   - LIBGLADE bindings for Java
p   libglade-java-doc               - LIBGLADE bindings for Java (API documentat
p   libglade-java-gcj               - LIBGLADE bindings for Java (native code fo
p   libglade-perl                   - Glade-perl runtime modules
p   libglade0                       - library to load .glade files at runtime
p   libglade0-dev                   - development files for libglade
i   libglade2-0                     - library to load .glade files at runtime
p   libglade2-dev                   - development files for libglade
p   libglade2-ruby                  - Libglade 2 bindings for the Ruby language
i   libglade2.0-cil                 - CLI binding for the Glade libraries 2.6
p   libglademm-2.4-1c2a             - C++ wrappers for libglade2 (shared library
p   libglademm-2.4-dev              - C++ wrappers for libglade2 (development fi
p   libgladeui-1-5                  - GTK+ User Interface Build core library
p   libgladeui-1-common             - GTK+ User Interface Build core library
p   libgladeui-1-dev                - GTK+ User Interface Build core library (de
p   libgladexml-perl                - Perl module for the libglade library

so maybe try to install libglade2-0? then try again. just do the same for each thing. it's not like installing this stuff will take up tons of room.you can also write down what you install in case you want to remove it if it didn't help, you can always run a simulation also before you do the final final install of a package, to make sure it's not going to bring down tons of dependencies, so that command is
sudo aptitude -s install libglade2-0
it will go  thru it's normal thing BUT not install it, just tell you what it will install if you don't use the -s (simulate) option. Good luck

### Post by matt221984 on 2007-06-16
It might be useful to note in the first post that the Nvidia driver wont compile on the 2.6.21 kernel if you have Paravirtualization Support enabled in the kernel.

I didnt read this entire thread to see if it had been mentioned or not, but you can read up on the bug in this thread:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=2306826&highlight=GPL-incompatible+module+nvidia.ko+uses+GPL-only+symbol#post2306826]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=2306826&highlight=GPL-incompatible+module+nvidia.ko+uses+GPL-only+symbol#post2306826")

If you uncheck Paravirtualization Support under Processor type and features when configuring the kernel you will be able to compile the nvidia driver when you boot that custom kernel.

I was effected by this bug and it seems to only exist in the .21 kernel.

---

### Post by edmondt on 2007-06-18
I too had the same problem with Nvidia Drivers using envy. The steps from brent.stephens worked great for me:

1. Go to your source location and run make menuconfig
2. Under "Processor type and features" turn "Paravirtualization Support" OFF
3. Exit and save the config.
4. Run make prepare
5. Re-run the NVIDIA Installer
6. Profit.

---

### Post by disturbedsaint on 2007-06-22
> **edmondt said:**
> I too had the same problem with Nvidia Drivers using envy. The steps from brent.stephens worked great for me:

1. Go to your source location and run make menuconfig
2. Under "Processor type and features" turn "Paravirtualization Support" OFF
3. Exit and save the config.
4. Run make prepare
5. Re-run the NVIDIA Installer
6. Profit.
Or see this thread:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=292971](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=292971)

In short:
Use the patch found here: [http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=85037](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=85037)

Does this mean ALL the deselected packages compile as modules?  If so whats the point?  Do modules mean that it doesnt load it into the kernel, but only calls it from an external file?

### Post by hyperair on 2007-06-25
In the xconfig, a blank checkbox means that it isn't compiled, a circle in the checkbox means that it's compiled as a module, and a checked checkbox means that it is compiled into the kernel.

Compiling as a module means that it will not run unless requred, whereas compiling straight into the kernel will make it run regardless of whether it is required or not.

---

### Post by gala.martin on 2007-06-26
a few tips for the author. of course, you are free to accept/reject them

-almost eveybody does not need "bin86" for any of the operation listed above
-"wget" and "libncurses5" are installed by default on ubuntu.

-replace "sudo -s -H" with "sudo -s". The -H option is not needed here, even not suggested. Furthermore, I would put this command after 2., and then delete all the "sudo"s in the following commands (at present, there is sudo on some of them, and it there is not on others).

-"make oldconfig" is completely unnecessary in both the lines in 6. oldconfig, menuconfig, xconfig are three alternative ways to configure the kernel. Oldconfig is not recommended at all; menuconfig is the standard tool (not just for servers), xconfig is my favourite and easier to use, since it tells you a little about the various options. Before running the config command, I would also recall that configuring is the main reason beacuse one compiles the kernel, and I would link the tip page again here.

-In general, I would avoid to write many commands in a single line, as in "sudo cp /boot/config-2.6.20-16-generic .config && sudo make oldconfig && sudo make xconfig". This can be hostile to the newbies.

Cheers

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-06-26
> **gala.martin said:**
> a few tips for the author. of course, you are free to accept/reject them

-almost eveybody does not need "bin86" for any of the operation listed above
-"wget" and "libncurses5" are installed by default on ubuntu.

-replace "sudo -s -H" with "sudo -s". The -H option is not needed here, even not suggested. Furthermore, I would put this command after 2., and then delete all the "sudo"s in the following commands (at present, there is sudo on some of them, and it there is not on others).

-"make oldconfig" is completely unnecessary in both the lines in 6. oldconfig, menuconfig, xconfig are three alternative ways to configure the kernel. Oldconfig is not recommended at all; menuconfig is the standard tool (not just for servers), xconfig is my favourite and easier to use, since it tells you a little about the various options. Before running the config command, I would also recall that configuring is the main reason beacuse one compiles the kernel, and I would link the tip page again here.

-In general, I would avoid to write many commands in a single line, as in "sudo cp /boot/config-2.6.20-16-generic .config && sudo make oldconfig && sudo make xconfig". This can be hostile to the newbies.

Cheers

Thank you for your suggestions, I will change my thread to meet the demands of some of these suggestions.

1.  I will not be removing any packages from the list because this thread is not meant only for Ubuntu users, but for those using other linux distros also, distros which may not have these packages installed by default.
2. I will be changing sudo -s -H to sudo -s. Thank you for this suggestion.
3. The reason I added "make oldconfig" is that "make oldconfig" keeps the current configuration and builds on it. This will keep any dependencies/hardware support enabled when "make xconfig" is invoked.
4. I will be separating all the commands, but will eventually add a link to a script that will run these commands automatically.

Thank you!

### Post by stepan2 on 2007-07-11
I managed to solve it ! the reason was because i did acpi=off . i searche darround and found out that i had to do pci=nommconfig

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-16
For example I need UDF 2.50 functionality form SourceForge UDF 2.50 patch. But it isn't added to any kernel right now so I must go and compile it themselves.

PS: I have solution for black TTY console. I've not tested it thought - need to compile new kernel:
```

echo vesafb | sudo tee -a /etc/initramfs-tools/modules
echo fbcon | sudo tee -a /etc/initramfs-tools/modules

```
It must be done before **DEB** installation.

...
...
...

OK. I've tested it.
IT"S WORKING!!!

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-17
If it's still this same architecture like your laptop (eg. both, laptop and desktop have i386 compatibile processors) you just edit ".configure" file with "menuconfig" (under shell/console) or "xconfig" (under X server) and chose appropriate CPU optimization.
I think it should work. I've baked yesterday 2.6.20  32bit kernel with K8 optimization under AMD64.

---

With a little stretch of imagination, one could conclude that the same goes for the processor*.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-17
During building you could also pass architecture and processor to compiler:
```

fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image --arch=i386 --subarch=k6

```

### Post by praxis22 on 2007-07-17
Hi,

It's probably just me but strange things happen when I rebuild the kernel :)

First time I rebuilt the kernel: **2.6.20.3**
It built and installed fine but when I rebooted it threw me into the ash shell in initramfs, this turned out to be because it had decided to rename the device tree from /dev/hdb to /dev/sdb, etc. So upon removing the UUID strings and replacing them with /dev/sdb1 ro it booted normally. so I edited /etc/fstab and though nothing of it.

Now upon rebuilding again: **2.6.22.1**
The kernel again refuses to load, I boot single user and it hangs upon probing the DVD, however it got far enough to tell me that the new build had reverted to using /dev/hdb, etc. so I edit menu.lst and reboot, where it again throws me into the initramfs shell. So I boot back into 2.6.20.3 and reset the /dev/hdb entry to use the UUID again.

This works, but I check df -k just out of curiosity, this is what I found:
```

desktop:~$ df -k
Filesystem           1K-blocks Used Available Use% Mounted on
/dev/sdb1             30961696  15633128  13755808  54% /
varrun                  514160        76    514084   1% /var/run
varlock                 514160         0    514160   0% /var/lock
procbususb              514160        96    514064   1% /proc/bus/usb
udev                    514160        96    514064   1% /dev
devshm                  514160         0    514160   0% /dev/shm
/dev/hdc2             18867136  11150144   7716992  60% /fat
desktop:~$ ls /dev/hd*
/dev/hdb   /dev/hdb2  /dev/hdc   /dev/hdc2  /dev/hdd
/dev/hdb1  /dev/hdb3  /dev/hdc1  /dev/hdc3
jdesktop:~$ ls /dev/sd*
ls: /dev/sd*: No such file or directory

```

Welcome to the twilight zone, kernel style :)

**EDIT:**  Uname output: Linux desktop 2.6.22.1 #1 SMP Tue Jul 17 19:39:02 BST 2007 x86_64 GNU/Linux

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-18
change fstab and menu.lst from /dev/hdxX to UUID.
With this, no mater what's new will developers invent with device name it will boot-up all the time.
To check UUID specified device issue:
```

blkid /dev/hda1 # for /dev/hda1 drive and so on...

```

---

### Post by praxis22 on 2007-07-18
> **dk75 said:**
> change fstab and menu.lst from /dev/hdxX to UUID.
With this, no mater what's new will developers invent with device name it will boot-up all the time.


No, not all the time, most of the time. I had problems when I first rebuilt that the kernel could no longer translate between UUID's and devices.

---

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-18
If you do not mismatch UUID and /dev/ then it shouldn't be any problem.
I've never had fsatb and menu.lst with /dev, I've always had UUID and when people reported that after upgrading kernel (it was with 7.04 beta test period) their devices suddenly changed name from hda to sda and they have problem with boot I've hadn't had any problem ever, even after installation custom compiled kernel.
And you couldn't translate between /dev and UUID. You must chose what to use, but only one method at the time.

make menuconfig -> device drivers -> ATA/ATAPI/MFM/RLL support -> 
    -> ATA/ATAPI/MFM/RLL support
    -> enhanced IDE/MFM/RLL disk/cdrom/tape/floppy support
    -> include IDE/ATA-2 Disk support
    -> generic/default IDE chipset support
    -> PCI IDE support
    -> Intel PIIXn chipset support (may be for ICH chipset??)

and tried recompiling, rebooting it detected the hard drive and root partition.
someone might be able to give good explanation what difference this options made. I enabled them without completely understanding them.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-20
> **phpathak said:**
> success in the 4th compilation ....:)

I looked into the output of dmesg and it looked like this ...

.
[   11.126276] Uniform Multi-Platform E-IDE driver Revision: 7.00alpha2
[   11.126287] ide: Assuming 33MHz system bus speed for PIO modes; override with idebus=xx
[   11.126361] ICH: IDE controller at PCI slot 0000:00:1f.1


Yes, it's normal. In most cases PCI works as bus line between North and South bridge. It could be modified and could have fancy name but it's still PCI.

> **phpathak said:**
> 
    -> ATA/ATAPI/MFM/RLL support
    -> enhanced IDE/MFM/RLL disk/cdrom/tape/floppy support
    -> include IDE/ATA-2 Disk support
    -> generic/default IDE chipset support
    -> PCI IDE support
    -> Intel PIIXn chipset support (may be for ICH chipset??)

Yes, it's for some ICH chips
didn't you make "cp /boot/config-`uname -r` .config && make oldconfig"??? It copying last working config for you so it's good base for modyfication.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-07-21
You can't compile git sources with OldDebianWay (eg. make-mpkg). It's incompatible.
You must use NewUbuntuWay form here:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile")
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelCustomBuild]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelCustomBuild")

### Post by wnelson on 2007-07-22
Two things come to mind. First, dual channel memory. If your memory is supposed to be dual channel try moving the sticks of memory to on bus. Second, IDE cable. It may be a bad IDE cable. Try another maybe. But, first run memtest+ to see if any errors occur.

Walt
Tacoma Wa

### Post by dk75 on 2007-08-07
> **caterpillar33 said:**
> After I run step 8, I get asked a (very long) series of questions. My impression is that my configuration was not copied properly. Could someone tell me how to copy them properly?


You've copied it properly, but it was config from 2.6.20 and you added it to 2.6.22
Kernel 2.6.22 have few new functions which must be configured either "on" or "off" so "make oldconfig" check's configuration file and fix it - that's why you have to answer few question. Just hit "Enter" all the time and make proper configuration with "make menuconfig"/"make xconfig" later.

---

### Post by cacycleworks on 2007-08-08
> **Tiftof said:**
> 
2) I would like to disable some more options in the kernel to see the effect. But what if I screw up, could I just use the deb's from a previous compile or does the src folder has to be intact (the state it was when I compiled the first deb's)?


Yes! Better still, you have the start up image in /boot ... when the computer starts up, Grub lets you pick the kernel to boot to. you can edit /boot/grub/menu.list to make the count down slower. I also turned off the splash and quiet options from the boot line... seems faster without having to turn the screen on and off all the time. And I like seeing the log scroll by. :)

Though... I would LOVE to change the default font shown. When I first got into linux (over 10 years ago) I had a nice font by default and loved that...

:D

### Post by cacycleworks on 2007-08-17
Modules can be loaded via two files: 
/etc/modules 
/etc/initramfs-tools/modules

If you edit initramfs-tools, you must run update-initramfs.

Look around the google a little to read up on initramfs before doing it. I think you just put the module name in there. (that's what I do to get piix loaded right away for DMA)

### Post by reacocard on 2007-08-27
> **master_kernel said:**
> Just to put it out there: What would you like in [KernelCheck]("http://kcheck.sourceforge.net")?

The ability to add patches. For example, many people use the suspend2 patches, others may need a patch to get their particular hardware to work, and so on. Users should be able to choose from a list of common patches, or supply their own from a file. Multiple patches should also be allowed, with checks to see if there is a conflict of course.

Another nice thing would be a simple kernel configurator to enable/disable certain common features without having to dig through the hideous mess of options that is kconfig. Things like debugging support, USB suspend, etc.

Also perhaps ability to choose a different kernel version?

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-08-31
> **DnasTheGreat said:**
> Has anyone else been experiencing much bigger modules than before? Like... on the order of 5-10 times the original size?

Do you have any of the debug options on? That could certainly do it. Also, you can always do away with your initial ramdisk, just make sure that you include your root filesystem and disk controller into the kernel and not as modules.
cheers,

### Post by RonB123123 on 2007-09-02
I installed the kernel and everything seems to be working fine. When I choose Ubuntu from the grub menu, I see an error message real quick that says something like this.

MCFG something something is not reserved.

Is there anyway to avoid this error? or a way to fix it?

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-02
> **RonB123123 said:**
> I installed the kernel and everything seems to be working fine. When I choose Ubuntu from the grub menu, I see an error message real quick that says something like this.

MCFG something something is not reserved.

Is there anyway to avoid this error? or a way to fix it?

Is this your error message?
> BIOS Bug: MCFG area at e0000000 is not E820-reserved

If so...
**This is a BIOS Bug, not a kernel bug.** If your computer boots normally otherwise then just ignore this error. This bug is reported at Launchpad [here]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-source-2.6.20/+bug/91674"). Somebody suggested using the following boot option:
> pci=nommconf
This may or may not work, but it doesn't hurt to try. Just add it to the kernel options in your /boot/grub/menu.lst.

Keep in touch!

master_kernel

---

### Post by Tiftof on 2007-09-03
> **RonB123123 said:**
> Thank you master_kernel, but where do I put **pci=nommconf** ?

This is my menu.lst file after I put the above in. Is this correct?

Is the above correct? Thank you.

~Ron

No that is not correct. Add pci=nommconf add the end of the line that says kernel (in the section of the kernel you want to boot with that option.
From recent experience: don't change the recovery mode kernel line unless you're sure that it will work. Although you can still edit the options from the grub boot menu.
[B]
styrofoam cup[/B]: m stands for compiling as a module and not statically in the kernel. I think it won't be loaded if not necessary.

### Post by Sekuhara on 2007-09-04
I just had a terrible experience of compiling kernel on Ubuntu and hope for some help from you.

I have to recompile the kernel because I needed 2 minor changes in the code without changing the kernel much, so I wanted to unpack the source suitable for the current stock kernel (2.6.20-16), apply my changes, recompile it using exactly the same configuration and install it.

What I did:
1. Updated the stock kernel to 2.6.20-16 from 2.6.20-15 that came on the CD., rebooted with no problems
2. Installed build-essential etc.
3. installed linux-source-2.6.20 , unpacked it in /usr/src , made a symlink 
4. make-kpgk clean ; make-kpkg --append-to-version=-mychanges --initrd kernel-image modules-image
5. 3 hours compile time later... resulted in a 24MB-large package in /usr/src
6. dpkg -i linux....deb

Now it looks like the kernel is being installed, it even has exactly the same size as the stock vmlinuz-2.6.20-16-generic, but then it turns out that initrd being written is so huge I run out of free space on /boot, and the installation aborts with an error.

apt-get remove linux....-mychanges reports that installed size is around 580MB! How is this possible, when the deb package is just 24? Why is my initrd so large?

Right now I'm trying to install the kernel by hand and not use initrd by compiling in support for filesystems, so the kernel can mount root partition directly. I noticing however that if I make another make without clean, I get a lot of warnings during compile, something like "symbol xxx is now in incorrect position" (sorry can't post the exact warning right now), and I wonder what difficulties will I encounter next...

Update: didn't work - I get kernel panic with "please append correct root= option". So far I tried root=UUID=XXXXXXXX, as the normal options use, root=/dev/sda3 (why is even IDE drive now sda ?)  and root=/dev/hda3, all yield same message. In my experience, grub entry as simple as

```

title=Some Title
root (hd0,0)
kernel /kernel-2.6.XX-version root=/dev/hda3

```

always worked perfectly for kernels compiled without using initrd.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-09-05
[QUOTE=Sekuhara;3311461
What I did:
1. Updated the stock kernel to 2.6.20-16 from 2.6.20-15 that came on the CD., rebooted with no problems
2. Installed build-essential etc.
3. installed linux-source-2.6.20 , unpacked it in /usr/src , made a symlink 
4. make-kpgk clean ; make-kpkg --append-to-version=-mychanges --initrd kernel-image modules-image
5. 3 hours compile time later... resulted in a 24MB-large package in /usr/src
6. dpkg -i linux....deb
[/QUOTE]
point 4 is your problem
It should be:
```
4. cp /boot/config-`uname -r` .config && make oldconfig && make xconfig (or "make menuconfig" if in text terminal - <ctrl><alt><F1>)
4.1. make-kpkg clean
4.2. make-kpkg --append-to-version=-mychanges --initrd kernel-image modules-image
```
You've had ommited most importand step - configuration.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-08
[CENTER]**There is a bug in the "Check for Updates" button in KernelCheck 1.0.3 Revision 1. Anybody using any version below this one is advised to upgrade to 1.0.4 immediately.**[/CENTER]

There are two ways to fix this bug:
[LIST=1]
[*]Manually download and install KernelCheck 1.0.4
[*]Edit the KernelCheck file (ONLY WORKS IF KERNELCHECK IS STILL INSTALLED)
[/LIST]

Visit [this site]("http://kcheck.sourceforge.net") to download and install KernelCheck manually.

Editing the KernelCheck file the easy way:

[LIST=1]
[*]Open a terminal
[*]Download the patched file
```
wget http://kcheck.sourceforge.net/pool/patches/kernelcheck
```
[*]Make it executable
```
chmod +x kernelcheck
```
[*]Move the patched file to /usr/bin
```
sudo mv kernelcheck /usr/bin
```
[*]You're done!
[/LIST]

Editing the KernelCheck file the hard way (This has not been tested):
[LIST=1]
[*]Open a terminal
[*]Download the patch
```
wget http://kcheck.sourceforge.net/pool/patches/patch09082007.patch
```
[*]Patch the file
```
sudo patch /usr/bin/kernelcheck -i patch09082007.patch
```
[*]You're done!
[/LIST]

We apologize for overlooking this error.

---

### Post by kevdog on 2007-09-12
How do I add the bcm43xx kernel module into the vanilla kernel??  Do I have to recompile the kernel?

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-12
> **kevdog said:**
> How do I add the bcm43xx kernel module into the vanilla kernel??  Do I have to recompile the kernel?

No. You can, but it will not do anything. You can load this module by entering the following command(s) into the terminal (I just tested this under a fresh Ubuntu Feisty):
```
sudo modprobe bcm43xx
```
And, if you want it loading every time you boot:
```
echo "bcm43xx" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```

Cheers!
master_kernel

---

I tried following this exelent guide ( [link]("http://www.howtoforge.com/kernel_compilation_ubuntu")) and obviosly fail to load the .config with errors. So i read a litle and run:

```
make menuconfig -ARCH=i386

```
and i manage to pass this stage without problems. Once i saved the kernel configuration is when i need a litle help.

```

```
wont run.
```

wont run either.. so i guess i need some special version of kernel libs or perhaps gcc?

I'm not entirely sure if this may help, but[ I wrote a guide a while ago]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=1343290") under the name linuxnewbie946. Try using these commands after configuring the kernel.

> Now time to build the kernel: Make sure that you are in /usr/src/linux. This will build a debian file that you can install.

```
fakeroot make-kpkg clean
```

```
fakeroot make-kpkg -initrd --revision=386 binary-arch modules_image
```
Edit: If the above **command**(singular) doesn't work, try this:
```
make-kpkg --rootcmd fakeroot --initrd --revision=386 binary-arch
```

### Post by dk75 on 2007-09-12
> 2. Specifically what folder is not automatically created?
```
/lib/firmware/2.6.23-rc5-custom
```
for example, but reason for that is that make-kpkg don't make it itself so KernelCheck don't make it too.
Of course it's easy to do by
```

mkdir /lib/firmware/`uname -r`
cp -r `ls -d /lib/firmware/2.6* | tail -1`/* /lib/firmware/`uname -r`/

```

### Post by dk75 on 2007-09-15
You could compile bcm43xx module and modprobe it but... source for that can't be taken from kernel source. That source could be used during kernel compilation only.
If you want to compile bcm43xx module separately you must serach for it standalone sources elsewhere.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-15
> **kevdog said:**
> How come the bcm43xx and other drivers (like ipw) didnt get compiled when the kernel was compiled??   I dont remember an option during the config process that allowed me to deselect compilation of net drivers.  This is what Im most confused about!

I'm not sure. One SUSE thread for compiling kernels had this module already compiled. Ironically, I did [find something on the net]("http://digilander.libero.it/hba/articles/Ubuntu-7.04-ASUS-A6000.html") though while I was trying to fix my 64 bit Flash:
> 
The 64-bit Windows driver can be downloaded from [here]("ftp://ftp.support.acer-euro.com/notebook/aspire_3020_5020/driver/winxp64bit/80211g.zip"). Unzip it and install it as described below:

    * ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf (Installing Windows INF driver in ndiswrapper)
    * ndiswrapper -l (See Driver installed)
    * ndiswrapper -m (Write configuration for modprobe)
    * change the wlan0 aliais to eth1 in /etc/modprobe.d/ndiswrapper
    * add ndiswrapper to the file /etc/modules to load it at boot time.
    * modprobe ndiswrapper (Load driver)
    * dmesg | tail -n 6 (gives a description of your wireless lan interface)

A good instrument to administrate wireless networks is wlassistant, you should be able to find and install it with Synaptic. You could also use the commands iwlist eth1 scan to scan the aria for networks and the iwconfig command together with ifconfig, to setup the interface manually.

If that doesn't work, download the motherboard specific driver for your BroadCom card and try again.

Not sure if this will work, but try it.

### Post by kevdog on 2007-09-15
Found this information specifically for bcm43xx

```
Use the native Linux Broadcom bcm43xx drivers

I've tested these (briefly) and they work with the HP nx6125, which has a Broadcom BCM4318 [AirForce One 54g] (in my case). I have used them successfully with wpa_supplicant, using the setup described in section on WPA Supplicant below. However, I have not done exhaustive tests. Most of the information below comes from a useful gentoo source which can be found here.

To use the bcm43xx kernel module you'll need a 2.6.17 or newer kernel. I am using vanilla kernel 2.6.18.2. In your kernel configuration enable the following. In the Networking --> section enable

Networking -->
	<M>   Generic IEEE 802.11 Networking Stack                           
	[*]     Enable full debugging output                                 
	---     IEEE 802.11 WEP encryption (802.1x)                          
	<M>     IEEE 802.11i CCMP support                                    
	<M>     IEEE 802.11i TKIP encryption                                 
	<M>     Software MAC add-on to the IEEE 802.11 networking stack      
	[*]       Enable full debugging output                               

In the Device drivers --> Network device support --> Wireless LAN (non-hamradio) --> section enable

Device drivers--> Network device support --> Wireless LAN (non-hamradio) -->
	<M>   Broadcom BCM43xx wireless support
	[*]     Broadcom BCM43xx debugging (RECOMMENDED)

Now build the kernel in the normal way.
```

---

**Program name: KernelCheck**

Visit [http://kcheck.sourceforge.net](http://kcheck.sourceforge.net)

KernelCheck is a project that is designed to easily build the latest kernel for your distribution using the instructions provided in the Master Kernel Thread. This automated process is a fork of AutoKernel by Robert Wolterman (xtacocorex), Timothy Janssen (mentok), and Kristof Verbeken (PingunZ). KernelCheck is currently under the GNU Public License.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-16
**Program name: fglrx_install.sh**

The program attached automatically downloads and installs the proprietary fglrx driver from the ATi site.

To run the script, download it and then:
```
chmod +x fglrx_install.sh
```
```
./fglrx_install.sh
```

### Post by paul_manning22 on 2007-09-16
Hi all.  This tute has helped me successfully compile several new kernels but I have the same problem with each new one.  It seems I experience problems with certain USB devices on compiled kernels.  For example, my external hdd will no longer auto mount.  If I unload ehci_hcd and reload it, or cycle the power of the external drive, it then works.  It still wont automount, but at least a proper dev id is assigned so I can manually mount it via fstab.  The other issue I experience is my USB IR receiver, command ir, doesn't seem to like ehci_hcd loaded at all.  A lsusb detects the device briefly, and then it disappears.  If I blacklist ehci_hcd all together, the device works fine. Problem for me is, I need ehci_hcd loaded in order for my Hauppage Nova T 500 to function correctly. I have several regular installs of Ubuntu on other machines an I can verify the IR reciever and external HDD works as expected with EHCI_HCD loaded at boot.

My other question is regarding housekeeping.  With all the 'other' kernel updates ubuntu seems to do via the update manager, I'm looking at tidying up all the redundant files I no longer need for old kernels.  I have found these in;
/usr/src/{kernel version}
/lib/modules/{kernel verison}
/lib/firmware/{kernel version}
and all the other entries in grub etc
Is it safe to delete all these without any damaging effect to the compiled kernel?  Is there a basic tidy tute someone can assist me with?  

Cheers.

The other issue I experience is my USB IR receiver, command ir, doesn't seem to like ehci_hcd loaded at all.  A lsusb detects the device briefly, and then it disappears.  If I blacklist ehci_hcd all together, the device works fine. Problem for me is, I need ehci_hcd loaded in order for my Hauppage Nova T 500 to function correctly. I have several regular installs of Ubuntu on other machines an I can verify the IR reciever and external HDD works as expected with EHCI_HCD loaded at boot.

My other question is regarding housekeeping.  With all the 'other' kernel updates ubuntu seems to do via the update manager, I'm looking at tidying up all the redundant files I no longer need for old kernels.  I have found these in;
/usr/src/{kernel version}
/lib/modules/{kernel verison}

Is it safe to delete all these without any damaging effect to the compiled kernel?  Is there a basic tidy tute someone can assist me with?  

Cheers.

As for your first question, it sounds like some options aren't being enabled in the kernel. Try upgrading to Gutsy's developing kernel using the [script found here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3376098") and then try compiling a new kernel again. This should enable any options the Ubuntu team has enabled for better hardware support.

As far as your second question goes, based on the folders you have listed above, you can try this as long as **you are not running the kernel being removed.** Replace 2.6.22.6 with the kernel being removed.

```
sudo apt-get remove 2.6.22.6*
sudo apt-get autoremove
sudo apt-get check
sudo rm -rf /usr/src/2.6.22.6 /lib/modules/2.6.22.6 /lib/firmware/2.6.22.6
sudo update-grub
sudo updatedb
```

### Post by quark_77 on 2007-09-18
Hi cebe11,

You've got the right of it so far. You need to download and unpack the kernel sources, which I'd do in /usr/src/linux-(kernel version).


Applying patches depends on how the patch was made. Both of these commands will apply the patch
```
cat patchname | patch
```
or:
```
patch < patchname
```

Patches can be reversed using -R and "tested" using --dry-run.

The only question is where to put the patch before applying it. Some patches will patch from the top of the tree i.e. you'll put the patch in /usr/src and run the command from there. Others, like yours perhaps, will be applied in the same folder as the file in question. If you look in the first few lines of the patch you'll see your answer in the filenames (is it looking for isl6421.c or linux/drivers/media/dvb/frontends/isl6421.c.

Hope this helps, if anyone else more au fait with patching please feel free to add to that. _EDIT: Just seen master_kernels post, looks like he'll be more help than I am!_

With respect to the wireless problems people have been having, the kernel config has changed. My understanding is a new wireless subsystem has been pulled (mac80211) from the wireless-dev git tree. As such, WLAN_80211 has replaced NET_RADIO. Thus when you run make oldconfig you might be missing things. Again please can someone correct me if I'm wrong!

I picked that up trying to compile IPW3945 with kernel 2.6.22.5 as the patch I previously posted doesn't work on newer kernels.

Hope this helps,

Quark_77

---

I have downloaded and unpacked the sources for linux-2.6.17.12 into /usr/src/linux-2.6.17.12.

The patch of interest is located here if this helps: [http://www.linuxtv.org/pipermail/linux-dvb/2006-November/014567.html](http://www.linuxtv.org/pipermail/linux-dvb/2006-November/014567.html)

So far so good.  I cut and pasted the patch into a .diff file.

BUT when I go to /usr/src/linux-2.6.17.12/drivers/media/dvb/frontends I can't find this file.  There are other source files present, just not this one.  If I google the file it seems to be common and is listed all over the place showing it in this location.   Here is an example: [http://www.koders.com/c/fid73B8FF7EA9D4EFC4AB73B7DB75DDEE849027E288.aspx](http://www.koders.com/c/fid73B8FF7EA9D4EFC4AB73B7DB75DDEE849027E288.aspx). I could download it and put it in this directory?  But then will it be used properly?
Could this be in a module instead of the kernel? 
Now I'm stuck, was thinking I was getting somewhere.

Any help is appreciated.


UPDATE:

Just so I don't waste anyones time, I did eventually find the file and applied the patch!  It was part of the linux-dvb module.  I downloaded the source, applied patch successfully ( at least no errors) and compiled and installed OK.  I can't quite yet determine if it was successful but judging by the fact that the new module loads OK I think I'm good.  Thanks for your help, this place is excellent for support.
I'm sure I'll be back as a learn more and more.
cebe11

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-19
> **quark_77 said:**
> Hi cebe11,

You've got the right of it so far. You need to download and unpack the kernel sources, which I'd do in /usr/src/linux-(kernel version).


Applying patches depends on how the patch was made. Both of these commands will apply the patch
```
cat patchname | patch
```
or:
```
patch < patchname
```

Patches can be reversed using -R and "tested" using --dry-run.

The only question is where to put the patch before applying it. Some patches will patch from the top of the tree i.e. you'll put the patch in /usr/src and run the command from there. Others, like yours perhaps, will be applied in the same folder as the file in question. If you look in the first few lines of the patch you'll see your answer in the filenames (is it looking for isl6421.c or linux/drivers/media/dvb/frontends/isl6421.c.

Hope this helps, if anyone else more au fait with patching please feel free to add to that. _EDIT: Just seen master_kernels post, looks like he'll be more help than I am!_

With respect to the wireless problems people have been having, the kernel config has changed. My understanding is a new wireless subsystem has been pulled (mac80211) from the wireless-dev git tree. As such, WLAN_80211 has replaced NET_RADIO. Thus when you run make oldconfig you might be missing things. Again please can someone correct me if I'm wrong!

I picked that up trying to compile IPW3945 with kernel 2.6.22.5 as the patch I previously posted doesn't work on newer kernels.

Hope this helps,

Quark_77
Thank you for this quark, I did not know about this wireless change. This is no doubt why people have been experiencing problems. Because Gutsy now uses the 2.6.22* kernel, once the final release comes out, the make oldconfig will be set correctly again.

Thank you for the update cebe11, keep us posted if you run into any other problems.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-09-20
You means ".config" not "xconfig"? Because "make xconfig" it's only a tool, graphical tool to edit ".config" and "make menuconfig" is textual tool to edit the ".config".

If you means "to use konfig from previous kernel" then yes, you'll do everything as in guide except that you dont make the last kernel edition by "make menuconfig/make xconfig" but you must to do "make oldconfig" and "cp" which is before.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-20
> **milkstarbanana said:**

I think you mean the .config file. This is the file containing the previous kernel's configuration. If this is indeed to what you are referring, the answer is yes. I include this step in the tutorial. This step contains the code 'make oldconfig'.

A question from a newbie to compiling kernels for ubuntu/debian.

First off, my goal is to create a nearly identical kernel to the feisty generic kernel or 2.6.20-15-generic. I am wanting to compile a single addition kernel module (a kernel module that debian and therefore ubuntu dont compile by default), and get this kernel and module onto a router that doesnt have enough room for me to compile a kernel on it. So I want to create a deb file that I can copy to the router and install the kernel that way.

I am using the directions found here:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Kernel/Compile)

To get the kernel source, I am using the commands to get the Ubuntu kernel source or:

apt-get source linux-image-2.6.20-15-generic
apt-get build-dep linux-image-2.6.20-15-generic

At this point I have a new linux kernel directory (linux-source-2.6.20-2.6.20) with everything unpacked in it and diffs applied. I am compiling for the 386 arch, so I am editing the config file found in debian/config/i386. 

Question here, how do the arch config files found in debian/config/i386 relate to .config found in the kernel source root?  For example, when I read about copying the config file from /boot/config-2.6.20-15-generic to [sourceroot]/.config,  how does this .config relate to the configs in i386?

For now I have edited the config in the i386 directory.

I then run

debian/rules updateconfigs

One complaint here, the above command fails, unless I go into debian/bin and make the scripts there executable. Why arent these made executable when the source is installed?

After running the above command, I then run:

AUTOBUILD=1 fakeroot debian/rules binary-debs

Upon completion of this step, I would assume that I have deb files for each of the flavors in the 386 arch.. However I am having a hard time getting the compile to go to completion. 

My compile is failing some time after the modules are made into kernel objects and at

====== making target build [new prereqs: stamp-build]======
make[1]: Leaving directory `/usr/src/linux-source-2.6.20-2.6.20/debian/build/build-386'
Checking module listings...
Modules have gone missing:
dccp_probe

Will not continue!
make: *** [build] Error 1

I have no clue how a module would go missing, AND the last compile I even tried compiling dccp-probe into the kernel and not as a module, and I still get the same error.

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-09-23
> **yther said:**
> 
What do we need to do in order to get all of the Ubuntu customizations (patches?) so that our starting config will be the same as the kernels they ship?

Thanks for any ideas!

Best bet would be to just use the Ubuntu kernel sources, and change your config from there IMHO.
The issue is that Ubuntu heavily patches their kernel source and to do the equivalent would require a lot of manual edits on your part (creating and modifying makefiles, grabbing all the sources, etc...)

If you'd rather not use the Ubuntu kernel sources to roll your own custom kernel, you could 1)patch the kernel with any desired upstream patches and 2) compile required modules without their own patches outside of the kernel tree (much easier than incorporation).

Anyone have any other thoughts on the issue?

cheers,

---

> **atlfalcons866 said:**

Post 600!

Now down to business. Many people have asked how to get the restricted modules, and the down and dirty answer is that you have to compile them yourself. You have to download your drivers yourself (most people need nVidia or ATi drivers) and compile them. You can also use Envy to install these video drivers.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-29
> **atlfalcons866 said:**
> i mean for stuff like ndiswrapper
Did some Google-ing, found this (only works in Feisty, Gutsy repos don't have ndiswrapper-source).
**EDIT:** Oddly enough, Linux Apps shows that the Gutsy repos do have ndiswrapper-source in the repos, but it isn't listed on the Ubuntu Package Search. You can get ndiswrapper-source for Gutsy [here]("http://linuxappfinder.com/package/ndiswrapper-source"). (It says there isn't one for 64-bit Gutsy, but the 32-bit one works b/c it is for all architectures)


**First**, follow my guide up **to** step 10.
```
sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-utils ndiswrapper-source
```
```
cd /usr/src
```
```
sudo tar --bzip2 -xvf ndiswrapper-source.tar.bz2
```

That should extract the needed modules to the modules folder in /usr/src. Now, follow the remainder of my guide starting at step 10. After the kernel compiles, there should be a file in /usr/src called ndiswrapper-modules-something.deb. Install this file BEFORE rebooting using the code below.
```
dpkg -i ndiswrapper-modules*
```

Hope this helps!
master_kernel

---

### Post by danzk on 2007-09-30
I have managed to compile the latest kernel and it works but during step 9 i couldn't find the module for my D-link  dwl-g510 rev c so i have no wireless. Can someone please tell me what i need to enable and is there any way to add it post compile it would be much appreciated

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-09-30
> **danzk said:**
> I have managed to compile the latest kernel and it works but during step 9 i couldn't find the module for my D-link  dwl-g510 rev c so i have no wireless. Can someone please tell me what i need to enable and is there any way to add it post compile it would be much appreciated
I did some searching, and it seems like you will need to install ndiswrapper to enable your card. See [http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/showthread.php?t=214888](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/showthread.php?t=214888) for this solution, however, the starter of this thread was using Mandrake.

I found a better solution involving ndiswrapper at the Ubuntu Community Wiki site. You can see the guide here: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WikiGuide/DWL-G510](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WikiGuide/DWL-G510). I can sum this guide up pretty easily below.

First, install ndiswrapper. Then follow the steps below.
```
sudo ndiswrapper -m
```
```
cd ~
```
```
wget ftp://ftp1.planet.com.tw/Wireless_Lan/WL-3563/DR-WL3563v230.zip
```
```
unzip DR-WL3563v230.zip
```
```
ndiswrapper -i Win98/mrv8k51.inf
```
Check if it is installed:
```
ndiswrapper -l
```
If so, add it to your /etc/modules:
```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```
```
echo "ndiswrapper" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```
Then report back here if this works or doesn't work.

Good Luck!
master_kernel

1. It does run make oldconfig, but it does not automatically choose the default options. You can choose the default options by holding down ENTER when make oldconfig is run.

### Post by ilikenwf on 2007-10-02
I will post this separately somewhere too, but I wanted to share a few kernel patches....They should apply cleanly against Ubuntu kernel source, and will probably do the same for vanilla as well. Try both and let me know which is faster for you. 

First off, I have all of these as a personal patchset, which is included if you want it. The series file pretty much tells you the patch order, and that sort of thing.

I also wanted to highlight a couple: the hz-864 and hz-432 patches are really good. I wrote the 432 patch. Use the 864 patch for single core processors and the 432 for dual core processors. Make sure to select the appropriate option in the processor menu before compiling.

Also included is the cfs scheduler patches.

Skip any you don't want or need, but I highly suggest using CFS and one of the hz patches.

Link: [http://www.megaupload.com/?d=PGB5RWDL](http://www.megaupload.com/?d=PGB5RWDL)

I narrowed it down to those two with a compilation issue I had...adaptive ondemand readahead is OK, if I remember correctly..

sata_nv-sw-ncq-support-2.6.22.patch is for nforce boards I believe...if your ata works right now, just use adaptive ondemand readahead...will put that in the next release.

### Post by quark_77 on 2007-10-02
Hi All,

A few questions for you all

I'm back to the pain of /dev/sd(a) versus /dev/sd(b) with kernel 2.6.22.9. Can anybody explain why this has happened in this kernel version?

On edgy's kernel < 2.6.20 I was running with /dev/hda disks. Upgrading to 2.6.20 then 2.6.21 has resulted in /dev/sda disks and I'm now experimenting with 2.6.22.9 which is looking for /dev/hda again (IDE disks). I'm aware of this because it can't find the root partition as it is set in /etc/crypttab (/dev/sda) hence I'm not prompted for the LUKS passphrase.

Specifically, how do I find out if my hd is SCSI or IDE based independant of the kernel that has loaded it? Knowing this, what drivers do I need to enable and what can I safely disable? I'm currently enabling IDE-2 drivers and SCSI support, I'm unsure what happens if I pull one or the other i.e. could I connect to an external scsi/ide device in the future?

Thanks for all your help,

Quark_77

### Post by dk75 on 2007-10-05
@quark_77:
Try to disable "kernel configuration - Device Drivers - ATA/ATAPI/MFM/RLL support - SCSI emulation support"
But I'm not sure since I've never used this emulation and I don't have IDE disks at all so I've never had this type of trouble.


Other things that you can try is to edit "/etc/fstab" and "/boot/grub/menu.lst" and change "root=/dev/hdaX" to "root=UUID=very_long_uuid_number".
"very_long_uuid_number" you can obtain by:
```
sudo blkid
```
After that you won't have any /dev/hda-/dev/sda trouble.

---

Regarding my problems with hda/sda, different versions of the kernel represent my hdisk different ways... no idea why. Either way, I've added UUIDs to crypttab and fstab, backed up my 2.6.21.3 initramfsdisk and am going to reboot and see whether the new initramfs can find my hdisk...

Quark_77

### Post by loko on 2007-10-11
hello,

i need help to set-up the kernel 2.6.23 correctly.

i have built it according to the instructions on page one. it starts fine, but doesn't ask me about the passphrase of my encrypted home-partition.

if i switch to tty after the xserver is up, and enter (as root) : "/etc/init.d/cryptdisks restart" i am not asked for the passphrase.

therefor mount /home gives this error: "special device /dev/mapper/cryptohome does not exist"


can somebody help me to set this up correctly?


EDIT: i found the reason. The kernel was set up correct, but the partitions are not named /dev/sdaX. They are named /dev/hdaX so the cryptsetup-script could not find the right device.

---

### Post by quark_77 on 2007-10-12
> **master_kernel said:**
> make defconfig does work, but it does not entirely do what I want it to do. What I really want is for make defconfig to "filter" through make oldconfig, only selecting the defaults for modules make oldconfig does not have. I have heard of qconfig, but I have no idea how to use it. Any help is appreciated.

master_kernel

Hi master_kernel

Ok what I know about first;

gconfig is the same thing as xconfig. Both of these tools are GUI kernel config editors; they are built when the command is issued on the host system, as is the config utility which I think provides menuconfig, oldconfig and just plain ask for questions. Being built at the time, make menuconfig will always work, as will oldconfig, but xconfig and gconfig are dependant on the qt and gtk libraries respectively. Thus if you install the -dev versions of these libraries they will be buildable and usable assuming you are using a desktop environment.

If you try the commands, it'll tell you what libs it wants installing.

Make defconfig just copies /arch/$arch/defconfig to .config in / of the linux k source, so it isn't much use I'm afraid, sorry about that I thought it might respect the old config file.

And what I'm able to make out second:

make silentoldconfig will only ask about NEW modules i.e. ones not in the current kernel configuration, is this what you're after? It defaults over anything not NEW, but doesn't modify what is already there. I think it's been mentioned before and isn't what you're after but it comes closest.

Finally, /dev/sda vs /dev/hda:

I have my BIOS set up so AHCI is enabled in compatibility mode (because Windows can't cope with pure AHCI mode on setup). Thus, I think a PATA driver is taking over and labelling the disks /dev/h i.e. emulating IDE. I'm fixing windows so I should be able to use pure AHCI under linux. Just to be sure I'm removing the driver I think is causing the problem along with anything including PATA in the name. I'll let you know what happens.

Sorry I can't be any more help master_kernel.

Quark_77

---

  linux-restricted-modules-2.6.23

for this program to work."

I tried apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-2.6.23 but it said Package not found. I tried apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-generic, but it said that this package was up to date.

What am I doing wrong? :confused:
You can't have restricted modules in the new kernel unless you compile them yourself. The easiest way to install your nVidia drivers is to reinstall the current one so it detects your kernel. In the future, if you want to compile your nVidia driver into the kernel, install nvidia-kernel-source and extract it in /usr/src.

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-10-14
**Regarding the questions of the_mouse and NotRoot:**

You can delete /usr/src/linux-2.6.23* and it will spare you about 2.5 GB. You can also delete any downloaded files.

The linux kernel will use /usr/src/linux/.config for the configuration file for the kernel. Don't click Save As in xconfig, just save.

                             bzImage

  CHK     include/linux/version.h

grep: write error: No space left on device

Your hard drive is too small to compile the kernel. You need about 2.5 gigabytes to do it. About 2.2 GB can be deleted afterwards.

### Post by tormod on 2007-10-15
Thanks for a nice how-to. But please avoid root shells when possible. There is no reason to run anything as root, except the installation of the packages. Install the fakeroot package and prepend the make-kpkg commands with "fakeroot". This way all the compilation and packaging can be done running as your normal user.

Please also make a note/warning that almost 2.5 GB of disk space is needed. [edit: make that 3 GB]

Finally, I ended up with a kernel package monster of 200 MB, instead of the usual < 20 MB. It seems that the modules have not been stripped. Running strip --strip-debug on them gives them the ordinary size. I don't know if I missed a make-kpkg option?

Oh and by the way, the guide says **make-kpkg -initrd** where it should be **make-kpkg --initrd** (double dash).

---

### Post by tormod on 2007-10-16
> It seems that the modules have not been stripped.

I sorted out this using:
```
INSTALL_MOD_STRIP=1 fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --revision=generic.tv1 kernel_image kernel_headers
```

Note also that I only need to specify kernel_image and kernel_headers. I don't know why the guide uses module_image as well.

---

### Post by Björn on 2007-10-20
A tip for nvidia proprietary drivers, just inactivate them (with the system -> administration -> manager for proprietary drivers-utility) before you reboot into the new kernel and activate them afterwards.
(or else you will get an x-server error)

/Björn

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-10-27
> **wrishibothra said:**
> 
I am not able to find the package in any repositories either

The ipw3945 module needs to be compiled against a specific kernel. You'll need to build your own to get it to work with your custom kernel.
Sources and instructions can be found at [http://ipw3945.sourceforge.net/](http://ipw3945.sourceforge.net/). Of course you'll also need the microcode and regulatory daemon in addition to the driver.
Alternatively, a new driver without the regulatory daemon is also available:[ http://intellinuxwireless.org/?p=iwlwifi]("http://intellinuxwireless.org/?p=iwlwifi")

cheers,

---

### Post by kevdog on 2007-10-27
Just an FYI - when you upgrade kernels you basically lose all the restricted drivers contained in the ubuntu kernel (since the packages were compiled against ubuntu's kernel package version).  In many cases you also lose the wireless drivers. You also lose any other custom kernel modules you may have installed in the previous version, such as ndiswrapper.  And not to be repetitive, any compiz/fusion you had working in gusty, will not be contained in the new kernel, and you will have to reinstall from package or source.

Again, the purpose of this thread is to instruct how to compile a vanilla kernel.  The kernels contained in the Ubuntu versions are all modifications of the vanilla kernel with added packages and kernel modules.

---

### Post by mbeierl on 2007-10-27
The number of jobs started by default in the kernel make is controlled by /etc/kernel-pkg.conf.  Just add the following to that file:
```

CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3

```
General rule of thumb is to have either the same number as CPUs, or one higher.  In my case I've got core 2 dual, so I set the concurrency to 3.

Hope this helps.

Btw, any one else concerned about executing the full compilation as root?  I prefer to work as my user account and use fakeroot for the make-kpkg instead.

And, lastly, if anyone knows of a howto for "official" ubuntu kernels (say, like I'd like to build the same kernel as the current Gutsy release, with a patch or two of my own) please, let me know :)

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-10-27
> **mbeierl said:**
> 

Btw, any one else concerned about executing the full compilation as root?  I prefer to work as my user account and use fakeroot for the make-kpkg instead.


It's pretty much the primary no-no. There was a bug sometime ago in which some entries in /dev were deleted if the kernel was compiled with root privs. Better to play it safe IMHO and compile as a normal user and preferably not in /usr/src (for other reasons).
cheers,

---

### Post by Etlaesium on 2007-10-29
I was having a problem with my IDE drives not showing up in 2.6.22:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3646593](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3646593)

This tutorial along with modifying the following resolved all of my issues:

```

CONFIG_IDE_MAX_HWIFS=4

```
to
```

CONFIG_IDE_MAX_HWIFS=10

```
though a value of 8 probably would have sufficed.

Thank you, Master_Kernel, for a well-written tutorial.

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-10-29
> **firecat53 said:**
> Yes, the linux-source package from the repos has the Ubuntu patches.

There is a guide [here]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Suspend2Kernel") for compiling the Ubuntu kernel with the Suspend2 (Tuxonice) patch. Just substitute your patches...works great!  (Except for the problems I mentioned above....also no sound afterwards)

Scott
For your sound problem:

Is your sound HD (Azalia)? If so or if you're unsure make sure the Azalia option is enabled in the kernel (Ctrl+F for Azalia).

Are you using a laptop? I know that some people have got it to work again in this forum by reinstalling ALSA.

Also: Suspend and hibernate don't work on some systems because of the change to the SLUB allocator. Just change it back to SLAB and it should work.

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-10-29
Finally. I have found several patches for fglrx 8.40.4 that help this driver compile with the 2.6.23 kernel. They are available in the KernelCheck pool repository under patches/fglrx.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-10-30
step 8. - what "many options"? You have no option here, just agree to all.
After that in step 9 you will have better configuration tool with help for any option.
Step 8 is only for "integrity checking" of configure file and modify it for this new kernel but not for configuration (you could use it for configuration of new options only available with this new kernel but it is redundant with step 9 - but you can't ommit step 8 = trouble).
