### Post by revenant_org on 2007-11-04
Hi All, 

After a long night of recompiling, finally I figured out how to compile 2.6.24rc1-git12 kernel.

Known issues: 
 - Alsa-driver won't compile. It complains about ( O=..  ) src directory, you need to get the daily snapshot of alsa-driver to compile it.

 - Nvidia-new-kernel is not listed for module-assistant. You need to link nvidia-new-kernel to packages default script.
```

cd /usr/share/modass/overrides/
ln -s ../packages/default.sh nvidia-new-kernel-source

```

- Nvidia module won't load because ubuntu uses lrm-video to load it which doesn't work for custom nvidia compiles. You need to comment the following line in /etc/modprobe.d/lrm-video :

```

#install nvidia /sbin/lrm-video nvidia $CMDLINE_OPTS

```

- Initrd.img is huge. You need to disable kernel debug listend under Kernel Hacks in xconfig.

Note, My laptop with kernel 2.6.24rc1 is very responsive and fast specially if you enabled CFQ, Tickless Timer and changed Timer Frequency to 1000Hz. 

It was worth it :guitar:.   & I hope it will help you also.


Enjoy,:popcorn:

---

### Post by xzero1 on 2007-11-05
Got the new kernel working. Compiz was a bit more difficult though. It seems xorg was configured incorrectly when I chose generic lcd panel. The default selected boot resolution was not supported by my lcd panel. Though I configured the correct resolution, apparently the timings were incorrect and it would fall back to an unsupported one.  Restoring part of my old xorg allowed compiz to boot correctly.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-11-09
> **jpittack said:**
> I am using the 23.1 kernel.  I have lost sound after trying out this new kernel.  All I can tell from Dell's website is that I have HD integrated audio.  Have I lost sound because of something I did in compiling the kernel or is this not my fault?

In addition, I decided to use this kernel because I recalled that someone said that 64-bit systems would now be able to have a tickless kernel with this version.  My projected battery life has dropped now.  Is tickless not enabled by default or did my memory serve me poorly and this kernel is not tickless at all?

Thanks for the how to.  I always like fiddling with computers and this will end up being my favorite way.
I had the same error as you for HD audio. I posted the fix for this under the troubleshooting section on the first post. You have to enable the Intel HD Audio module in xconfig under Sound > Advanced Linux Sound Architecture > PCI Devices > Intel HD Audio. I posted a screenshot below.

The tickless timer can be added by putting a checkmark for Tickless System (Dynamic Ticks) under Processor type and features. I posted a screenshot for this as well.

master_kernel

---

### Post by revenant_org on 2007-11-16
> **Hairy_Palms said:**
> im not using the one from the repos, im using the 100.14.19 nvnidia installer

Maybe it is related to the same issue I had, try to see what does modprobe do to install the module using the following:

```

modprobe -v nvidia

```

If you see "lrm-video" thing there, then you will need to comment the nvidia line from the file /etc/modprobe.d/lrm-video.

Hope it helps ;)

modprobe: fatal: couldn't load /lib/modules/2.6.23.1/modules.dep, no such file
It(s very fast and I don't know how to log it but everything works fine after that.
I read stuff about problems with initrd, depmod, etc... but don't know
lsmod show me that modules are loaded, don't know if those modules  are loaded after boot, but can't explain.
I tried without modifying .config but same problem.
Have you any idea ?
Thanx for your help and sorry for my english ;b

Christophe
France, Toulouse

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-11-17
> **Tirook said:**
> Hello,

modprobe: fatal: couldn't load /lib/modules/2.6.23.1/modules.dep, no such file


Yup, depmod creates modules.dep and associated file...though that should have been taken care of automagically. A sudo depmod -a will do the trick if you're booted with the custom kernel.
cheers,

---

### Post by jaybombalous on 2007-11-17
Ok, so I have tried lately to compile a kernel using kernel check. everything seems to go good till reboot. 

Then it try to start gdm, then screen flickers 4 times then it just hangs at the boot up screen and says

'checkin the state of the battery' GDM never loads, wtf is causing this problem?

I will try to compile using oldconfig again, but its just a waste of time to compile all those modules. :(

any ideas? I used kernel check before, configured it from scratch and everything worked fine before, but now I need to redo the kernel cause I need to add support for vfat fs and every time I try and compile its the same thing. 

GDM never loads and it just hangs as described above.

---

### Post by jaybombalous on 2007-11-17
I answered my own question. Once the computer hanged I pressed a few buttons and I finally ended up at a login prompt, command line. So I tried to startx and I got an error, something about nvidia. So I reset the xorg.conf to load vesa and tried to start x again. 

This time it got me to the desktop, re-installed nvidia drivers and rebooted, now everything works fine.


I always forget to uninstall drivers before I compile kernel.

---

### Post by KarT on 2007-11-17
Here's a small tip :)

Situation:
I recently used your app (fantastic! congratulations!) but made a few changes in the background (like changing the -O2 flag to -O3) which caused a crash in the kernel compile. I had to correct the issue and continue "by hand"

Sugestion:
In case anything goes wrong, instead of stopping the process why not ask the user if he/she wants to quit or to retry that specific operation? (in my case, the compilation itself).

Once again, congratulations ;)

### Post by ilikenwf on 2007-11-26
The 64 Bit Deb Package of _[2.6.24-rc3-zen3-ubuntu]("http://www.mattparnell.com/zen/2.6.24-rc3-zen3/x86_64/")_ is up. I have also upped the _[source code.]("http://www.mattparnell.com/zen/2.6.24-rc3-zen3/")_ _[Get info about it here]("http://www.mattparnell.com/zen-kernel.html")_ You should probably read the info, too. The deb is 18.5megs, and the kernel image itself is only 1.6megs. It boots fast, and runs fast too....not to mention all of the bugfixes, and new hardware support, and new features it has.

### Post by master_kernel on 2007-12-03
> **atlfalcons866 said:**
> how can we add andrew mortons -MM patch? i want to try reiser4

[COLOR="DarkOrange"]**You have to use the latest prepatch in order to use the *latest* MM patch.** I have created this short tutorial to replace steps 6 and 7:[/COLOR]

[COLOR="Green"]Download the latest patches, both devel and mm:[/COLOR]
```
wget http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/testing/patch-2.6.24-rc3.bz2
```
```
wget http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/people/akpm/patches/2.6/2.6.24-rc3/2.6.24-rc3-mm2/2.6.24-rc3-mm2.bz2
```

[COLOR="Green"]Now apply the patches:[/COLOR]
```
bzcat patch-2.6.24-rc3.bz2| patch -p1
```
```
bzcat 2.6.24-rc3-mm2.bz2| patch -p1
```

Continue the howto from step 8.
master_kernel

---

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-04
Okay, so I'm halfway through completing my compiling of the 2.6.23.9 kernel. Now, I noticed that the /usr/src/linux-2.6.23.9 is more than 1 GB now. So I began comparing some of the kernel modules, and got a fright when I noticed that almost all of them are approximately 10x bigger than that of 2.6.22-14-generic. What's going on?!

Just for an example:
/usr/src/linux-2.6.23.9/drivers/acpi/asus_acpi.ko: 130881 bytes
/lib/modules/2.6.22-14-generic/kernel/drivers/acpi/asus_acpi.ko: 23604 bytes

EDIT: I've checked a few pages back, and I realized that the reason is because debugging is enabled. Where do I disable this? Also, the debugging options were enabled in the 2.6.22-14-generic kernel as well! So how come my kernel comes out super-huge and not that?

---

### Post by NotRoot:-) on 2007-12-04
hyperair, there have been a lot of changes and additions  to the kernel.  That's why its bigger.  The detailed change logs can be found at:  [http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/](http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/)   and listed in the ChangeLogs.
One example is [http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/ChangeLog-2.6.23](http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/ChangeLog-2.6.23)

---

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-04
I know there have been many changes! But 10x bigger for EACH module is excessive. Anyway I've disabled every single debug option I could find in xconfig, and am compiling again. Let's see if it reaches as big.

Okay, I found that the sizes in 2.623 aren't 10x of the sizes in 2.6.22 now. Thanks anyway.

### Post by NotRoot:-) on 2007-12-04
Looks like 2.6.24 will be very huge too:

from:  [http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/testing/ChangeLog-2.6.24-rc1](http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/testing/ChangeLog-2.6.24-rc1)

commit c9927c2bf4f45bb85e8b502ab3fb79ad6483c244
Author: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@woody.linux-foundation.org>
Date:   Tue Oct 23 20:50:57 2007 -0700

    Linux 2.6.24-rc1
    
    The patch is big.  Really big.  You just won't believe how vastly hugely
    mindbogglingly big it is.  I mean you may think it's a long way down the
    road to the chemist, but that's just peanuts to how big the patch from
    2.6.23 is.
    
    But it's all good.
    
    Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-04
I'm just wondering, is it possible for me to use the nVidia installer for one kernel and nvidia-glx for another?

---

### Post by glennric on 2007-12-04
> **hyperair said:**
> I'm just wondering, is it possible for me to use the nVidia installer for one kernel and nvidia-glx for another?

I have not heard of a way of doing this.  However,it is possible to install the nvidia drivers downloaded from the nvidia web site for multiple kernels.  Install the nvidia drivers for one of the kernels using the script.  Then boot to the other kernel and compile the kernel module using the command
```
sudo sh  NVIDIA-Linux-x86-100.14.19-pkg1.run -K
```

You may have to adapt the filename for the installer if you are using a different version of the nvidia drivers.

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-06
> **glennric said:**
> I have not heard of a way of doing this.  However,it is possible to install the nvidia drivers downloaded from the nvidia web site for multiple kernels.  Install the nvidia drivers for one of the kernels using the script.  Then boot to the other kernel and compile the kernel module using the command
```
sudo sh  NVIDIA-Linux-x86-100.14.19-pkg1.run -K
```

You may have to adapt the filename for the installer if you are using a different version of the nvidia drivers.

So I take it that the -K option will compile just the kernel module without anything else? If that's the case then I'll give the 2.6.23 kernel I compiled another try.

---

### Post by glennric on 2007-12-07
> **hyperair said:**
> So I take it that the -K option will compile just the kernel module without anything else? If that's the case then I'll give the 2.6.23 kernel I compiled another try.

Yes, that is what the -K option will do.  You can also do
```
sudo sh  NVIDIA-Linux-x86-100.14.19-pkg1.run -K -k 2.6.23-revision 
```
and compile the nvidia kernel module for the specific kernel 2.6.23-revision (or whatever kernel number) even when that kernel is not the one running.

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-10
Just run ```
sudo nvidia-xconfig --add-argb-glx-visuals
``` if you don't have a customized Xorg.conf previously.

Otherwise, poke around your /etc/X11 folder and see if you can find your old xorg.conf file. Then to use it:
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/backupfile /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

---

### Post by phillywize on 2007-12-10
> **hyperair said:**
> Just run ```
sudo nvidia-xconfig --add-argb-glx-visuals
``` if you don't have a customized Xorg.conf previously.

Otherwise, poke around your /etc/X11 folder and see if you can find your old xorg.conf file. Then to use it:
```
sudo cp /etc/X11/backupfile /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

Thanks...back in business for the most part.  Got the nvidia driver back working again.  Tried your suggestions, hyperair,  but no love until I used the generic vesa driver, then used synaptic (no good with apt-get or aptitude) to rip out every shred of restricted nvidia code on my system, and reinstalled it from the bottom up.

Now my compiz is not working, but I can live with that until I figure out what to do.  And should take it to another thread.

In the meantime, is there anything anywhere with a step-by-step way of compiling a custom kernel on a system with an nvidia gpu without screwing it up?  Something tells me that downloading NVIDIA_*.run, and then executing "sudo sh NVIDIA_*.run" as suggested in the howto isn't going to cut it.

EDIT:  And now, between uninstalling/reinstalling restricted stuff, and dusting off some old config files, compiz is back in the saddle again.

### Post by MeathooK427 on 2007-12-12

---

### Post by The Noble on 2007-12-12
Ok, it seems like the "-c" is not suppossed to be there. Remove the -c or use this command below to install what you need. I just finished my compile so nothing seems wrong.

```

sudo apt-get install build-essential bin86 kernel-package libqt3-headers libqt3-mt-dev wget libncurses5 libncurses5-dev

```

On another note: I love core 2 duos. My 1.8 Ghz just compiled the kernel in under an hour! Now to restart.

### Post by atlfalcons866 on 2007-12-12
i compiled another kernel for my sempron processor. But how would i install virtualbox it is complaning that it cant find the modules.

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-12
> **phillywize said:**
> That makes sense, although I still think I did something terribly wrong to screw up my stock kernel nvidia config.  I'll take another run at it once I get over this bad trip.  Like I said; my fault for being a nimrod.  Thanks for your help.

Don't worry about it. Also, if you use the nVidia custom installer make sure you get the correct version.
If you use nvidia-glx, you must use the 96*** driver.
If you use nvidia-glx-legacy you must use the 7*** driver.
If you use nvidia-glx-new you can get the latest driver.

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-12
Looks like you're missing a few required packages to me. Why don't you go to the first page and make sure you have everything that is needed installed?

On a side note I've got a slight problem to do with initramfs-tools. I compiled and installed 2.6.23.9, then was lazy to get nVidia working so I uninstalled it. Then I upgraded to Hardy. All was fine, then today initramfs-tools was updated. For some reason, the post-installation script wanted to create an initrd.img-2.6.23.9, eventhough my kernel is uninstalled! So it couldn't find /lib/modules/2.6.23.9 (or something like that) and then began complaining, and failed miserably. Next thing I know, my dpkg is in non-working order. Can someone help me out here?

My processor is a dual core indeed.

When I installed ubuntu on this laptop it detected my processor as a generic "586/K5/5x86/6x86/6x86MX". In attachment there is a couple of screenshots of my xconfig while reading my running kernel configuration file. Ubuntu installed itself relying on a generic processor instead of choose the correct type "Core 2 /newer Xeon".
I tried at least 20 times  to recompile existing kernel or compile a newer version keeping all the settings of my old config but changing the processor type to "core 2/newer Xeon", but the PC hangs. I always must reboot with the original kernel 2.6.22.14-generic.

Any idea?
Thanks in advance
Claudio

---

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-13
> **hyperair said:**
> On a side note I've got a slight problem to do with initramfs-tools. I compiled and installed 2.6.23.9, then was lazy to get nVidia working so I uninstalled it. Then I upgraded to Hardy. All was fine, then today initramfs-tools was updated. For some reason, the post-installation script wanted to create an initrd.img-2.6.23.9, eventhough my kernel is uninstalled! So it couldn't find /lib/modules/2.6.23.9 (or something like that) and then began complaining, and failed miserably. Next thing I know, my dpkg is in non-working order. Can someone help me out here?

I've solved it! I read the /usr/sbin/update-initramfs script, and found that it looks at the files found at /var/lib/initramfs-tools. All files there will refer to a specific kernel version. In my case removing 2.6.23.9 from that folder worked.

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-12-17
Hi,
> **bracc0 said:**
> 
My processor is a dual core indeed. 
Yup, dual core- but a core duo, not a core 2 duo.
That's a big difference as far as kernel processor optimizations are concerned. 

[quote=bracc0]
I tried at least 20 times  to recompile existing kernel or compile a newer version keeping all the settings of my old config but changing the processor type to "core 2/newer Xeon", but the PC hangs. I always must reboot with the original kernel 2.6.22.14-generic. [/quote]
That would do it .

As gcc recommends -march=prescott for the core duo, it seems to be best to optimize as a P4, but P-M and generic 686 will work fine as well.

cheers,

---

### Post by bracc0 on 2007-12-18
> **5-HT said:**
> 

Yup, dual core- but a core duo, not a core 2 duo.
That's a big difference as far as kernel processor optimizations are concerned. 




As gcc recommends -march=prescott for the core duo, it seems to be best to optimize as a P4, but P-M and generic 686 will work fine as well.

5-HT,
thank you very much for the clarification.

Forgive a newbie: what is "gcc" and "-march=prescott"?

TIA
Claudio

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2007-12-18
> **bracc0 said:**
> 5-HT,
thank you very much for the clarification. 
NP. Kind of a shame there's no Core Duo specific option, but P4 or P-M work fine.
> **bracc0 said:**
> 
Forgive a newbie: what is "gcc" and "-march=prescott"?

[GCC]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_Compiler_Collection") is the GNU C / C++ compiler that will take the kernel source code and magically turn it into a binary executable file.

[CFLAGS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CFLAGS"), such as -march=[prescott]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pentium_4#Prescott") are certain variables that you can pass to the compiler to modify build options.

The gentoo wiki has a great article on[ 'safe' CFLAGS]("http://gentoo-wiki.com/Safe_Cflags") to user for certain processors/desires, and the indicated ones for the core duo are suggestive of optimizing the kernel itself for a P4.


hope that helps,
cheers

### Post by 1900 on 2007-12-19
I have used this thread many times and thank the author and all contributors for it.

I have been compiling 2.6.24 recently and ran into a problem with vsftpd.

To correct the issue, i had to enable the option

Security/
 Enable different security models (SECURITY)
   and
 Default Linux Capabilities (SECURITY_CAPABILITIES)

I think these map to the functionality where I 'used to'

modprobe capability 

(though this 'module' was not built anymore in 2.6.24)

I found this info (again) from an old gentoo forum ([http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-194754.html](http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-194754.html))

---

### Post by ilikenwf on 2007-12-19
> **1900 said:**
> I have used this thread many times and thank the author and all contributors for it.

I have been compiling 2.6.24 recently and ran into a problem with vsftpd.

To correct the issue, i had to enable the option

Security/
 Enable different security models (SECURITY)
   and
 Default Linux Capabilities (SECURITY_CAPABILITIES)

I think these map to the functionality where I 'used to'

modprobe capability 

(though this 'module' was not built anymore in 2.6.24)

I found this info (again) from an old gentoo forum ([http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-194754.html](http://forums.gentoo.org/viewtopic-t-194754.html))

For 2.6.24 some devs (especially those who deal with modules) have to update CFLAG related issues, as the new way is to use KBUILD_CFLAGS...among other changes in the code that has to be brought up to date...heck...look at nVidia's beta driver for crying out loud!

If you get sick of compiling, I've got precompiled debs over in the [Zen Kernel Thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=623874&page=1")....top post is up to date...

For me, Vanilla doesn't quite cut it.

### Post by dk75 on 2007-12-26
When you compile kernel by hand then you can't use any of the repo driver (Synaptic, apt, aptitude).
You must compile it by hand as well and as for nVidia driver download it's binnary pack from nVidia site and simply run it (Envy won't work because RestrictedManager and RestrictedDriver must be compiled by hand as well).

If you should read this thread you could know that already...

### Post by hyperair on 2007-12-31
> **NotRoot:-) said:**
> fullback43, my screen goes black for a short time while loading the new kernel too.  I just wait and it loads fine and gets past that stage.  I seem to recall graphics options in the configuration of the kernel.  When the next kernel release comes out, I will search for them and decrease the settings.  
Does your system load if you give it the time to do so?

Meathook427, 
I also have sound problems with the new kernel.  Now I can only have one
audio stream ( program ) playing.  Previously I could have several.
I think my audio problem and possibly yours is listed here.
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Gutsy_Intel_HD_Audio_Controller](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Gutsy_Intel_HD_Audio_Controller) 
Multiple audio cards use the Intel HD Controller. Asus was one of them.
Actually, your problem is a much more minor one compared to Meathook427. When the snd-hda-intel module isn't loaded, you will not have any sound at all. The fact that you can only have ONE program access the sound at one time means that you're using OSS. You should poke around your configuration elsewhere (not the kernel) and change everything to use ALSA.

### Post by dk75 on 2008-01-01
> **NotRoot:-) said:**
> fullback43, my screen goes black for a short time while loading the new kernel too.  I just wait and it loads fine and gets past that stage.  I seem to recall graphics options in the configuration of the kernel.  When the next kernel release comes out, I will search for them and decrease the settings.  


black screen durring boot-up: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3026772&postcount=442](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=3026772&postcount=442)
edit "/etc/modutils.d/blacklist-framebuffer" also (it's new with u7.10) and hash ("#")  "blacklist vesafb" line to this state:
```
#blackist vesafb
```

it could be done even after installing new kernel - change files as described and then do:
```
sudo update-initramfs -u
```

### Post by NotRoot:-) on 2008-01-02
hyperair, you were correct about fixing my sound problem when you said "change everything to use ALSA."   Under System->Prefernces->Sound , I changed the devices from Autodetect to ALSA. Suprisingly, I found the same setup in several applications too. After I changed all of them from Auto to ALSA, all is now good with my sound.  So thank you for the suggestion.

### Post by 5-HT on 2008-01-09
> **DualC said:**
> Acculy i dont know if it's a explanation how to remove a compiled kernel after it has been compiled and installed ?

I'm is wery intrested how to know how to do it, cose i want to try many kernels, and acculy know if it's possible to do...

So my question, how do i removed a compiled and installed kernel in my system??,  to GET back to my stock installed kernel again and can move on from the beginning. :)

/M

a) If you generated the .debs to install using the the instructions in this thread, it's simply a matter of removing the associated packages.

b) If you installed the kernel and modules manually, or via the install script just delete the custom:

I.  kernel image from /boot
II. System.map from /boot
III. Initial ramdisk from /boot (if applicable)
IV. Associated modules from /lib/modules
V. the source directory in /usr/src

And then update your boot manager accordingly.

If you had previously removed the default kernel, make sure to reinstall before removing the custom one though ;).
cheers,

---

### Post by 5-HT on 2008-01-17
> **jocheem67 said:**
>  But still there's a lot of motherboard chippies that I don't need as well as firewire, agp and other stuff....

Is there a way to really strip down a self-compiled kernel?

One way would be to list all of the modules the default kernel is loading and to only include that hardware support into your config.
[URL="http://www.kroah.com/lkn/"]
Linux kernel in a Nutshell[/URL] has some great information (and some handy scripts) on how to customize your kernel for your hardware.

cheers,

### Post by jocheem67 on 2008-01-30
I recompiled the newest stable kernel and got rid of the mmconfig bios bug...

apparantly the kernel tries to detect the pci-devices in different ways:
through the bios, mmconfig or directly. By setting the way to detect to "direct" , the " not using mmconfig"  message disappeared while booting.

Very nice:guitar:

### Post by jpkotta on 2008-01-31
You don't need to be (and shouldn't be) root to build the kernel.  You can extract it to your $HOME, and give make-kpgk the --rootcmd=fakeroot option.  You must have the fakeroot package installed as well.

### Post by hyperair on 2008-02-04
That kernel is already in the Hardy repository. So I say it's a bit of a gamble, but you could enable Hardy repositories and grab the kernel, linux-restricted-modules and such. Then disable it.

---

### Post by glennric on 2008-02-04
> **hyperair said:**
> That kernel is already in the Hardy repository. So I say it's a bit of a gamble, but you could enable Hardy repositories and grab the kernel, linux-restricted-modules and such. Then disable it.

That is not the solution I am looking for.  I am trying to compile my own kernel and restricted modules.  The point is to tweak the settings for my specific machine for improved performance.  The Ubuntu kernels are designed to run on every machine, and because of that are inherently slower than the kernels I compile when run on my computer.

I figured it out by the way.  It turns out that make-kpkg is not ready for the migration of the i386 architecture to the x86 architecture.  Because of this it doesn't install all of the necessary files into the headers package.  I had to copy the files arch/x86/Makefile_32 and arch/x86/Makefile_32.cpu from the compiled source directory to the headers directory.  I also had to fix a broken link in the include directory, include/asm was pointing to asm-i386, which doesn't exist, and should have been pointing to asm-x86.  With those changes the restricted modules compiled.

---

### Post by mahuyar on 2008-02-13
For those of you trying to run the latest kernel (2.6.24.x) might have a problem with starting avahi-daemon.  To test if yours is running, 
```
sudo /etc/init.d/avahi-daemon restart 
```

You may receive an error of something like:
```
Timeout reached while wating for return value
Could not receive return value from daemon process. 
```

If it runs successfully, please ignore the rest of the post.

If not, you will have to enable the "Default Linux Capabilities" (SECURITY_CAPABILITIES).  It is at Security options > Enable different security models > Default Linux Capabilities.

This option becomes disabled if you follow all the default answers when you issue the command: ```
make oldconfig 
```

Hope this saves some of your googling times.  :)

### Post by tormod on 2008-02-16

initrd-tools has been replaced by initramfs-tools nowadays. You will have to change the dependencies and some postinst scripts in the .deb package. Anyway, why do you  want to downgrade to such an old kernel in 7.10?

### Post by tormod on 2008-02-16
> Perhaps you are right, and it is only a placebo effect.  It does feel faster and smoother.  However, I would be interested in obtaining actual benchmarks for comparison.  I am not sure how to do this.  Let me know of good ways of obtaining such benchmarks and I will provide numbers.

Here is the original thread from when the decision to make "generic" kernels was taken:
[https://lists.ubuntu.com/archives/ubuntu-devel/2006-August/019983.html](https://lists.ubuntu.com/archives/ubuntu-devel/2006-August/019983.html)

It has some benchmarking tips and a lot of technical information. As for the "optimized for my processor" debate, read the explanation how the "generic" kernel chooses optimizations for the processor type at boot-up (run-time) instead of having only optimizations for one kernel type compiled in.

> I'm not sure how you could say it was a Placebo effect, especially when I picked my processor type and turned off system tick or whatever it is called.

Turning off important features like "system tick" might make a difference but in many cases these things can be tuned or disabled by kernel flags and sysctl.conf, and you can avoid the whole compiling story. Secondly, if for instance "system tick" has a measurable negative influence, it would be good to report it and have it fixed for everybody.

> 
However, I noticed ~5 fps increase in playing World of Warcraft through wine (and yes, smoother and more responsive as well).

I don't know what your +5 fps would correspond to in percentage, but it sounds like a clear improvement. If this is a reproducible, statistically valid measure, it would be interesting to track down the reason for it.

PS. See also an old forum discussion on this: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=257544](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=257544)

---

### Post by sylecn on 2008-02-22
I've got two questions before trying this:
If I didn't change options after importing the old config file, will I get better performance?
How much faster is  all-in-one kernel compared to modularized kernel?

### Post by mahuyar on 2008-02-22
> **sylecn said:**
> I've got two questions before trying this:
If I didn't change options after importing the old config file, will I get better performance?
How much faster is  all-in-one kernel compared to modularized kernel?

For your first question, are you saying that going with Ubuntu's kernel configuration would give you better performance?  If that's the case, you won't "feel" any performance gained, since Ubuntu's kernel configuration is to fit everyone's needs.  Note that I have quotations around the word, feel, because it varies among people here.  As for me, personally, yes I do notice that the system has become a bit more responsive after changing some options, e.g. Timer Frequency to 1000 Hz.  But this doesn't mean you'll get everything done faster, since there are always tradeoffs between throughput and being more responsive.

I don't think I've noticed anything faster by compiling modules into the kernel rather than being modular.   That's for your second question, of course.  :)

### Post by nexes on 2008-02-25
Has anyone else come across a problem where instead of compiling the modules, it just prints the script used to do the compilation to the terminal window? I can't figure out what exactly is causing it.

I haven't looked too far into it, as I'm not sure how exactly make-kpkg works, but it's not executing that shell script correctly for whatever reason..

```

# make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 modules_image
exec debian/rules  DEBIAN_REVISION=386  INITRD=YES  modules_image
for module in  ; do                       \
          if test -d  $module; then                                \
            (cd $module;                                          \
              if ./debian/rules KVERS="2.6.24.2" KSRC="/usr/src/linux-2.6.24" \
                             KMAINT="Unknown Kernel Package Maintainer" KEMAIL="unknown@unconfigured.in.etc.kernel-pkg.conf"      \
                             KPKG_DEST_DIR="/usr/src/linux-2.6.24/.."       \
                             KPKG_MAINTAINER="Unknown Kernel Package Maintainer"        \
                             KPKG_EXTRAV_ARG=""        \
                             ARCH="i386"         \
                             KDREV="386" kdist_image; then    \
                  echo "Module $module processed fine";            \
              else                                                  \
                   echo "Module $module failed.";                  \
                   if [ "X" != "X" ]; then      \
                      echo "Perhaps $module does not understand --rootcmd?";  \
                      echo "If you see messages that indicate that it is not"; \
                      echo "in fact being built as root, please file a bug ";  \
                      echo "against $module.";                     \
                   fi;                                              \
                   echo "Hit return to Continue";                   \
                 read ans;                                        \
              fi;                                                   \
             );                                                    \
          else                                                      \
               echo "Module $module does not exist";               \
               echo "Hit return to Continue?";                      \
          fi;                                                       \
        done

```

Any help would be appreciated. :)

*EDIT 2* Ok, apparently this is due to it not finding any modules to process. Notice that in the for loop ( for module in  ; do ) there are no array items to traverse. The question now becomes, should it be finding modules to process?

This is my 1st go at kernel build.

Okay, I had to download kernel from the source tree at kernel.org because I was getting compile errors , about ***-??.s, rules. Anyway, after hours of googliong and ubuntu forum searches I discovered that the linux source packages could be corrupt. Thats why I downloaded external kernel sources.

Anyway, it now builds, no errors and modules have also built and installed, all as kernel 2.6.24.3

I setup lilo and booted. The new 2.6.24.3 option was there but it stops at some point complaining about cannot find ???/2.6.22-14/??? , my old kernel.

I used ```
# mkdir custom
# cd custom
# cp /boot/initrd.img ./initrd.gz
# gunzip initrd
# cat initrd| cpio -i
``` to "unlock" the initrd and create a copy of the initrd filesystem. I found that it is all setup against my old 2.6.22-14 kernel. I'm using this initrd to boot and thus this is why its failing.

How do I create a suitable initrd for my custom kernel.

My searches so far have revealed the deb style package creators with the --initrd flag, but I'm using external sources and not packages.

Also, mkinitrd is not on my system and if it was, I doubt it will create all what is required ?? or will it?

Thanks in advance.

### Post by keratos on 2008-03-17
Compiling a kernel is a piece of cake really, on *buntu

grab linux source...
edit if necessary

make menuconfig 
fakeroot make-kpkg clean
fakeroot make-kpkg --append-to-version=xxx kernel_image --initrd binary
dpkg-reconfigure kernel-image-xxxx


ha, the hard bit is making sure you dont omit anything you really need in the kernel.
!

Do you have ZEN KERNEL source? If so, remove zen virtualization kernel from your .config (in make menuconfig and remove the Zen option)

### Post by psilo on 2008-03-23
> Do you have ZEN KERNEL source? If so, remove zen virtualization kernel from your .config (in make menuconfig and remove the Zen option)

That was the problem.  Thanks.

### Post by gaspard.leon on 2008-04-05
for those nvidia users trying to use a 2.6.25-rc* kernel, and having trouble building the kernel module:

Patches to use drivers with 2.6.25-rc* kernels: **[http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=110088](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=110088)**
(I found it in this discussion on page 6: [http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=107144](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=107144))

**there are patches for 71, 96, 169 series drivers there, and they work.**

[COLOR="Red"]I'm currently using 2.6.25-rc8 w/ the 169.12 driver and it works!![/COLOR]

Steps I used to upgrade (this was in Ubuntu 7.10):
(replace [COLOR="Navy"]NVIDIA-Linux-x86-169.12-pkg1.run[/COLOR] names in navy text with your filenames)


[LIST=1]
[*]build a 2.6.25-rc* kernel (I used kernel check, and spent 2 hours customizing the config...)
[*]download nvidia driver from [http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html](http://www.nvidia.com/object/unix.html) , and patch from link above
[*]patch the driver using instructions from link above (run this in a terminal: [FONT="monospace"][COLOR="DarkSlateGray"]sudo sh [COLOR="Navy"]NVIDIA-Linux-x86-169.12-pkg1.run[/COLOR] --apply-patch ./[COLOR="Navy"]NVIDIA_kernel-169.12-2286310.diff.txt[/COLOR][/COLOR][/FONT]) this step will spit out a new [COLOR="Navy"]NVIDIA-Linux-x86-169.12-pkg1-custom.run[/COLOR] file
[*]reboot into your new kernel (cross your fingers the config was right), failsafe X will probably come up.. use vesa driver for now
[*]jump into tty1 (ctrl-alt-F1)
[*]kill gdm and X: [FONT="monospace"][COLOR="DarkSlateGray"]sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop[/COLOR][/FONT]
[*]run driver install: [FONT="monospace"][COLOR="DarkSlateGray"]sudo sh [COLOR="Navy"]NVIDIA-Linux-x86-169.12-pkg1-custom.run[/COLOR][/COLOR][/FONT]
[*]????
[*]PROFIT!! :)
[/LIST]

### Post by master_kernel on 2008-04-18
> **andrewabc said:**
> I got to this step

Then it started askign a lot of y/n questions. I figured this would not occur until the next step.

why are some of the options
Y/n
N/m/y/?
N/y
and several others? Is the first option the default one used?
What this does is copy your current boot configuration to the new kernel directory so your new kernel will have [almost] the same configuration as your old one. Just hold the ENTER key for the default (the one in CAPS). After this, use the xconfig step to graphically edit the configuration.

master_kernel

**ANNOUNCEMENT:** Kernel 2.6.25 released.

### Post by keratos on 2008-04-24
> **Modplanman said:**
> I keep getting this:
And I really have no idea why.

Your /tmp filesystem is full.

```
df -h
```

delete files in /tmp

```
rm -rf /tmp/*
```

---

### Post by tormod on 2008-04-30
You should use a normal user (and fakeroot when needed) for compiling and building the debs. Then sudo only for installing the final debs. I think I commented on this in this thread a while ago, but it maybe got lost in all the comments.

### Post by Gumper on 2008-04-30
I just finished installing 2.6.25 and I have several problems. The first time I installed it I couldn't get my sound to work but figured out that I didn't have the modules selected for my sound card. Now what I have is I can't access my windows partion. I'm not able to read from it or write to it. I do know that I didn't select the "write to NTFS" in the config, but I thought that feature would have been enabled when I used my old config as a starting point.

The other problem that I'm seeing is when the Kubuntu splash screen comes up during booting, the progress bar gets about a tenth of the way across and then it hangs there for at least 30 seconds or so. It does finally go on but it takes too long. My boot up time has almost doubled with this kernel. This doesn't happen with my other kernels. 

Any suggestions on either of my issues?

Thanks!

---

### Post by ibuclaw on 2008-04-30
> **Gumper said:**
> I just finished installing 2.6.25 and I have several problems. The first time I installed it I couldn't get my sound to work but figured out that I didn't have the modules selected for my sound card. Now what I have is I can't access my windows partion. I'm not able to read from it or write to it. I do know that I didn't select the "write to NTFS" in the config, but I thought that feature would have been enabled when I used my old config as a starting point.

The other problem that I'm seeing is when the Kubuntu splash screen comes up during booting, the progress bar gets about a tenth of the way across and then it hangs there for at least 30 seconds or so. It does finally go on but it takes too long. My boot up time has almost doubled with this kernel. This doesn't happen with my other kernels. 

Any suggestions on either of my issues?

Thanks!

When you next boot up and see the splash screen. Hit **Ctrl+Alt+F1** to see what your PC is doing.
It is most likely just hanging when it tries to load one module. So you should have plenty of time to write down the process it stops on.


_____________________
Questions regarding my attempt at 2.6.25:

Okay, I've been doubly prepared for this.
I've [compiled and installed]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=311158") the [2.6.25 kernel]("ftp://ftp.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/"), I've downloaded the [madwifi 0.9.4 drivers]("http://madwifi.org/") and I've gotten the drivers for my [NVIDIA card]("http://www.nvidia.co.uk/Download/index.aspx?lang=en-uk") and the NVIDIA card [fix for the 2.6.25 kernel.]("http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=110088")

First thing I did was boot into recovery "**root console**", ran the NVIDIA card patch, then installed the madwifi drivers.
Typed **exit** and chose the "**fix X-screen**" option and then continued into "**normal user**" mode.

NVIDIA card works perfect!
Madwifi Drivers are detecting wonderfully too!
But one small snag, I seem to have **lost the ability to scan for other wireless networks.**

Does anyone know what I can do to fix it?

Regards
Iain

### Post by Gumper on 2008-04-30

> When you next boot up and see the splash screen. Hit **Ctrl+Alt+F1** to see what your PC is doing.
It is most likely just hanging when it tries to load one module. So you should have plenty of time to write down the process it stops on.

"Buffer I/O error on device FD0, Sector 0"
"End request : I/O error on FD0"

I assume from the FD0 part that it's referring to an issue with the floppy drive. The problem with that is I don't have one installed on my system. There is an entry for one in FSTAB though. 

Here's that entry:

```
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto user,utf8,atime,noauto,rw,dev,exec,suid 0 0
```
 

Any ideas what this could mean?

### Post by ptero on 2008-05-01

hmmh, that's strange... are you sure that you didn't change anything else as well back then?
however, it shouldn't be a big enough reason to hesitate. just have a good live-cd near you (i prefer gentoo minimal cd ;) ) and change the configuration file back if a problem like that appears.
and before applying **major** changes in configuration, i prefer to make a backup (stage4), because you can always get some minor troubles, which you won't discover instantly.

    DISABLED_MODULES="nv nvidia_new" ?

### Post by unutbu on 2008-05-08
For the hardware detection problem, I don't have much to suggest other than that you should go over your config options and make sure you clicked yes or module for each of your hardware items.

For the power off problem, next time you want to power off, open a terminal and type

```
sudo /sbin/shutdown -h now
```

You'll at least get a more verbose run down of what's happening during the shutdown process and perhaps you'll see where it is hanging. Post here the last few lines before the system hangs, and perhaps one of us will know what to do.

With my custom kernel, I can't mount any blank CDs at all.  CDs with content will not auto-mount, I can only manually mount them.  Ever seen something like this?

---

### Post by tormod on 2008-05-12
Technically, you don't mount blank CDs. You only mount file systems. What you're missing is the drive polling that tell applications (like the file manager) that you have inserted something (blank or not) into the drive.

### Post by unutbu on 2008-05-14
I'm sorry -- I think I was wrong in my last post.
In step 10 I think you want 
```
make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=<unique string> kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
```

See [http://newbiedoc.sourceforge.net/tutorials/kernel-pkg/build-kernel-pkg.html.en](http://newbiedoc.sourceforge.net/tutorials/kernel-pkg/build-kernel-pkg.html.en)

---

### Post by reacocard on 2008-05-14
> **santiagorf said:**
> Thank for the help. My kernel version is: 

sa@sa-desktop:~$ uname -a
Linux sa-desktop 2.6.25 #1 Wed May 14 01:47:25 EDT 2008 i686 GNU/Linux

so if I strictly follow the steps of this tutorial to compile the last kernel I think I'll overwrite it.
I don't want to take the risk since I'm experimenting with different configurations, and so I'm not sure if next time my linux will boot.

Are there any way to change the name of the kernel such as modifiying the .config...?

from step 10 of the guide:
> 
Then this:

Code:

make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

Note: You can replace "386" with anything you want. Like "k7" or "686." The kernel will now compile for 1-3 hours, depending on the speed of your processor. If you have an extremely slow processor, you may have to wait 4 or more hours waiting for the kernel to compile. In the meantime, I would go out to a movie or do something else while it's compiling.


so just give your different configurations different --revision arguments when you build them. Then they'll be named differently and not overwrite each other.

---

### Post by unutbu on 2008-05-14
According to 
[http://newbiedoc.sourceforge.net/tutorials/kernel-pkg/build-kernel-pkg.html.en](http://newbiedoc.sourceforge.net/tutorials/kernel-pkg/build-kernel-pkg.html.en)

> Using different values of --revision will not prevent conflicts between kernels with the same name.

### Post by doktormiod on 2008-05-19
Hi,
I used your program Kernelcheck and everything was ok except 1 little thing. It didn't compile restricted-modules and i don't know wheter it's my fault with cofiguration or it's set not to compile it as default. Anyway, fglrx stopped working and i wonder if there's a way to compile restricted modules itself without repeating the whole procedure?

---

### Post by dk75 on 2008-05-20
> **doktormiod said:**
> Hi,
I used your program Kernelcheck and everything was ok except 1 little thing. It didn't compile restricted-modules and i don't know wheter it's my fault with cofiguration or it's set not to compile it as default.

Restricted-Modules are different external software package thus it can't be compiled together with kernel. You must dowload sources and compile it by hand.
I've tryed once and... couldn't do it.


> **doktormiod said:**
> Anyway, fglrx stopped working and i wonder if there's a way to compile restricted modules itself without repeating the whole procedure?

Sure. It's the only way. You can't compile it at the moment of kernel compilation. You must compile kernel, install it, boot on it and thereafter you could compile Restricted-Modules.

Some user page or two earlier said that he compiled it - so serach and ask him.:twisted:

libc6-dev: /usr/include/sys/types.h

### Post by master_kernel on 2008-06-13
> **dm6257 said:**
> I followed your directions to install 2.6.25.5 on Ubuntu 8.04 on ThinkPad Z61t.  When I rebooted with the new kernel I saw only a blank screen after the initial ubuntu loading screen.  Two questions:
1. Is there a log file that might point to the error?
2. How do I cleanly remove the new kernel.

It is easy to use the old kernel so no real harm has been done.

Thanks,
dm6257
1. See if you can switch to a terminal after Ubuntu loads. If you can, login and type
```
dmesg | tail
```
This should show you the last thing that happened. If you can't do this or you don't feel like fixing the error(s) that you see...

2. Boot into your old kernel and in a terminal type:
```
sudo apt-get remove 2.6.25.5*
```
This will remove the packages from your system and the entries from your grub list. You can also remove the source from /usr/src.

---

### Post by Payteer on 2008-06-20
How do I go back to my old configuration as this is of no use at all apart from a learning process.  I have lost sound on my Lenovo 3000 V100 with built in intel sound card and no wifi anymore either.  I really would like to go back without reloading Harday again and losing all my changes.

---

### Post by gradedcheese on 2008-06-20
> **Payteer said:**
> How do I go back to my old configuration as this is of no use at all apart from a learning process.  I have lost sound on my Lenovo 3000 V100 with built in intel sound card and no wifi anymore either.  I really would like to go back without reloading Harday again and losing all my changes.

Just select the previous kernel from the grub menu (press ESC to access it during boot, right after your BIOS screen).  You can also uninstall the newer kernel:

sudo apt-get remove linux-image-2.6.26* linux-headers-2.6.26*

(replace the names with those from the packages you built or use tab completion).  If you need to, edit /boot/grub/menu.lst to change the default kernel to boot, remove entries, etc.

---

Thanks very much appreciated

### Post by unutbu on 2008-06-22
In your /boot/grub/menu.lst, go to near the bottom where there will be a bunch of stanzas that look something like

```
title		Ubuntu 7.10, kernel 2.6.22-14-generic
root		(hd0,2)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.22-14-generic root=UUID=03a507ee-cdac-4bd9-b438-eccd616b66ed ro **quiet splash**
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.22-14-generic
quiet
```

Does the kernel command end with the words "quiet splash"? If not, add that.

### Post by dk75 on 2008-06-25
You must decide which kernel is your working one and install nVidia driver here. Then make 2 copy of xorg.conf: xorg.nvidia and xorg.vesa. Configure it as it's named. Then when you want to boot to kernel without dirvers copy xorg.vesa to corg.conf and then reboot. When you chose to reboot from krenel with VESA to kernel with nVidia then copy xorg.nvida to xorg.conf and then reboot.
For easier copying install MidnightCommander ```
sudo apt-get install mc
```

---

When you chose to reboot from krenel with VESA to kernel with nVidia then copy xorg.nvida to xorg.conf and then reboot.
For easier copying install MidnightCommander ```

```

### Post by Joker5bb on 2008-06-29
does make-kpkg accept the "-j4" option? 

If it does, then dual/quad-core users can benefit a faster compilation!

i know that make does accept -j4 :popcorn::lolflag:

---

### Post by Joker5bb on 2008-06-29
```
make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image 
```

should be 
```

make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 kernel_image kernel_headers kernel_source modules_image

```
kernel_source is very important!!! must be added :)

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2008-06-30
> **Joker5bb said:**
> does make-kpkg accept the "-j4" option? 

If it does, then dual/quad-core users can benefit a faster compilation!

i know that make does accept -j4 :popcorn::lolflag:
I'm just speculating, but you could probably edit the MAKEFLAGS variable in Makefile to include -j4. Setting CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=4 will also have the same effect though.

### Post by Blinkiz on 2008-07-16
> **Blinkiz said:**
> I would like to know more about how distcc can be used. I have successfully set up distcc so the compile process is balanced across my computers in the network.
So, "make -j 12" works, but not "make-kpkg". Why? I have set up masquerade directory and I have tested that it works with a program I download as source and compiled.

I have followed the guide [here](http://myrddin.org/howto/using-distcc-with-debian/) and [here](http://wiki.vpslink.com/index.php?title=HOWTO:_Install/Configure_Distcc).

I have found out why distcc did not work for me with make-kpkg. It was because make-kpkg is running as root and distcc running as my normal user. distcc refuses to run as root. Anyone have a solution?

make[2]: *** [arch/powerpc/boot/uImage] Error 127

### Post by tormod on 2008-08-01
> **Blinkiz said:**
> I have found out why distcc did not work for me with make-kpkg. It was because make-kpkg is running as root and distcc running as my normal user. distcc refuses to run as root. Anyone have a solution?
You should never compile stuff as root. Compile and make the packages using your normal user account and only do the package installation using "sudo dpkg -i ...". To run make-kpkg (or whatever that claims it needs root - it doesn't) use fakeroot.

---

### Post by mrynit on 2008-08-13
step 8
```
cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config && make oldconfig
```

that did use the config file i just coppied. I get ask hundereds of questions y/n/m. I ctrl+C the questiongs and just did make xconfig and used it to open the config file.

did anyone else the all the questions??

---

### Post by jvincent08 on 2008-08-16
> **mrynit said:**
> step 8
```
cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config && make oldconfig
```

that did use the config file i just coppied. I get ask hundereds of questions y/n/m. I ctrl+C the questiongs and just did make xconfig and used it to open the config file.

did anyone else the all the questions??

Yea, I did. If you don't want the terminal version, only execute "cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config" without the "make oldconfig" part. Then run "make xconfig".


Many, many thanks to Master_Kernel for such a great guide. I was so scared of compiling from source, but everything has been going good so far. I'll post back when it gets done compiling.

Edit: Quick question. In xconfig, when you select an option, is it the equivalent of selecting it as "Y" or "M" in the terminal?

Update: Woo hoo! Everything worked great. I was so scared something was going to mess up or I'd get an error message or something, but nope! Thanks again for taking the time to write this article (and for keeping it up to date with the newest kernel)!

---

Then I get to here and get the followin errors.

```
root@computer:/usr/src# cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config && make oldconfig
make: *** No rule to make target `oldconfig'.  Stop.
root@computer:/usr/src# cd linux
root@computer:/usr/src/linux# cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config && make oldconfig
Makefile:296: /usr/src/linux-2.6.26/scripts/Kbuild.include: No such file or directory
Makefile:431: /usr/src/linux-2.6.26/arch/x86/Makefile: No such file or directory
make: *** No rule to make target `/usr/src/linux-2.6.26/arch/x86/Makefile'.  Stop.

```

```

which does not exist!  Assume -R? [n]

Makefile:296: /usr/src/linux-2.6.26/scripts/Kbuild.include: No such file or directory

### Post by SpaceMaster on 2008-08-18
I haven't had issues following these steps before.  Now, however, I'm trying to install 2.6.26.2.  Everything's fine until
```
make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
```
The machine takes off like a rocket compiling, but after a while crashes with the error message:
```
WARNING: vmlinux.o(.text+0xe907): Section mismatch in reference from the function cpu_exit_clear() to the function .cpuinit.text:cpu_uninit()
The function cpu_exit_clear() references
the function __cpuinit cpu_uninit().
This is often because cpu_exit_clear lacks a __cpuinit 
annotation or the annotation of cpu_uninit is wrong.

The function init_hrtick() references
the variable __cpuinitdata hotplug_hrtick_nb.33447.
This is often because init_hrtick lacks a __cpuinitdata 
annotation or the annotation of hotplug_hrtick_nb.33447 is wrong.

  GEN     .version
  CHK     include/linux/compile.h
  UPD     include/linux/compile.h
  CC      init/version.o
  LD      init/built-in.o

<built-in>:0: fatal error: when writing output to /tmp/ccicJJVi.s: No space left on device
compilation terminated.
make: *** [.tmp_kallsyms1.o] Error 1

```

My system is a Pentium 4 HT with 2 processors, and an ATI Raedon graphics card, and 1GB RAM, on a 60GB HDD (10GB OS partition).  I'm selecting "Pentium 4" in xconfig, as well as the 2 processor scheduler.  There's 3.9GB free on the OS partition, with 2.5GB free at the time the compilation crashes.  I don't have any relics of previous 2.6.26 kernels floating about, either.

Any ideas on what I magically managed to break?

My system is a Pentium 4 HT with 2 processors, and an ATI Raedon graphics card, and 1GB RAM, on a 60GB HDD (10GB OS partition).  I'm selecting "Pentium 4" in xconfig, as well as the 2 processor scheduler.  There's 3.9GB free on the OS partition, with 2.5GB free at the time the compilation crashes.  I don't have any relics of previous 2.6.26 kernels floating about, either.

Any ideas on what I magically managed to break?
From what it looks like, you're out of disk space; however, according to your information, this may not be the case. Just to be sure, try freeing up some disk space and set the permissions on /tmp to 777. Use the following command to compile the kernel (it does not require as much space):

```
INSTALL_MOD_STRIP=1 CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=4 make-kpkg --initrd kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
```

### Post by mahuyar on 2008-08-24
2 questions:

1)  After reading the man page about make-kpkg, I still don't understand the modules_image.  I thought the kernel_image already includes the selected modules.

2)  The option kernel_headers:  Things work fine for me without the kernel headers.  What would be the reason to include it?

Thanks

---

### Post by glennric on 2008-08-26
> **mahuyar said:**
> 2 questions:

1)  After reading the man page about make-kpkg, I still don't understand the modules_image.  I thought the kernel_image already includes the selected modules.

2)  The option kernel_headers:  Things work fine for me without the kernel headers.  What would be the reason to include it?

Thanks

The modules_image option will compile modules that are in /usr/src/modules.  You probably don't need that option, unless you have something in that directory that you want compiled.

The kernel_headers options will compile the kernel headers for the kernel that you are compiling.  This is needed if you compile something that depends on the functions built into the kernel, and don't have the original kernel source.  For instance if you want to use VirtualBox you will need the headers (or the compiled source) so that the virtual box setup can build its kernel modules.  I usually clean the source after compilation as the compiled kernel takes up quit a bit of disk space, so I always build and install the headers.

---

### Post by jvincent08 on 2008-08-30
Several pages back, someone asked about setting the -j option for make. I'm not sure if it was covered, but if anyone else is wondering, you can put MAKEFLAGS="CONCURRENCEY_LEVEL=X" before make-kpkg, so it looks like this:
```

MAKEFLAGS="CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=X" make-kpkg --initrd --revision=whateveryouwant kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image

```
..where X is the number of jobs you want the process to make (>= the number of cores on your system to take full advantage of all of them).

I recommend doing this to anyone who has a multi-core system, as it speeds up compile time considerably.

Maybe something to do with frame-buffer. As I have an intregrated video. Also the sound doesn't work. I thought copying the .configfile with this would copy my options:

That config file didn't have ALSA turned on.

Edit2: I got the sound to work, but still no graphics text, frame buffering, using vga=773.

Had the same problem with vga= not working, I got it working again by changing the Kernel configuration


Device Drivers ---> Graphics support ---> Support for frame buffer devices ---> VESA VGA graphics support
Set to [*] built-in

Device Drivers --> Graphics support ---> Console display driver support ---> Framebuffer Console Support
Set to <*> built-in

Device Drivers --> Graphics support ---> Console display driver support ---> Select compiled-in fonts :
Set to [*] built-in

Read a few people were ending up with a huge kernel, turn off kernel debugging, having it on makes the kernel huge;
Kernel Hacking --->
Turn off Kernel debugging

### Post by plowna on 2008-09-19
An update: If I got rid of the quiet and splash boot parameters, it would panic and drop to a busybox terminal with ALERT! /dev/sda1 blah blah blah.

Basically it detects my drives as /dev/hda, /dev/hdb etc now. I'm pretty sure I compiled in SATA and SCSI support, yet it still sees them as /dev/hd*, how do I change it back?

### Post by tormod on 2008-09-20
> **plowna said:**
> An update: If I got rid of the quiet and splash boot parameters, it would panic and drop to a busybox terminal with ALERT! /dev/sda1 blah blah blah.

Basically it detects my drives as /dev/hda, /dev/hdb etc now. I'm pretty sure I compiled in SATA and SCSI support, yet it still sees them as /dev/hd*, how do I change it back?

You should not specify the root device using the low level device names, but use UUID which is independent of the driver and device name. See **ls -l /dev/disk/by-uuid** for your UUIDs. Example from /boot/grub/menu.lst:
```
kernel  /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-2-generic root=UUID=4039194d-732b3782 ro quiet splash
```

---

### Post by plowna on 2008-09-24
> **tormod said:**
> You should not specify the root device using the low level device names, but use UUID which is independent of the driver and device name. See **ls -l /dev/disk/by-uuid** for your UUIDs. Example from /boot/grub/menu.lst:
```
kernel  /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.27-2-generic root=UUID=4039194d-732b3782 ro quiet splash
```

I haven't tried this, but I have recompiled the kernel in various ways. Eventually I got it to work (may need to play with the video settings but at least its working) I've been using the kernelcheck hopestar that is recommended in the OP.

What makes it frustrating is when the kernel detects the drive as /dev/hd* is that things like fstab are written for /dev/sd*. 

I figure that the kernel is likely either loading or detecting my drives as IDE first and SCSI/SATA second.

My system is LinuxMint Elyssa R1, which as I understand it is based upon ubuntu.

So I don't know for sure that its just LinuxMint that prefers to use the /dev/ format for declaring the root partition.

If indeed it is better to specify by UUID, why doesn't kernelcheck and/or dpkg use this? What about ubuntu? Does ubuntu do this by default?

### Post by unutbu on 2008-09-24
plowna, here are a few thoughts on the issues you raised. Hope they help.
[list]
[*]Imagine a computer with two external USB drives. One might be named /dev/sda and the other /dev/sdb. The naming might change from boot to boot depending on which port they are plugged in to, or the order in which they are plugged in. Imagine hot-swapping drives... I'm not sure, but I think they might change just due to the hard drive controller's fickleness (based on which drive gets recognized first at boot time). Using UUIDs to specify partitions is a way to avoid the ambiguity that arises from the situations mentioned above.

It is for this reason that specifying UUIDs are considered better than specifying device names.


[*]For the past few years Linux has been in the process of moving to libata as the new IDE driver. See [http://lwn.net/Articles/198344/](http://lwn.net/Articles/198344/). When the machine uses the libata driver it names all drives /dev/sd*, never /dev/hd*. So if you are seeing your drives named /dev/hd* it means you are not using the libata driver. At least this is my understanding. As always, I could be wrong and welcome corrections.

Since the libata driver is the default Ubuntu driver and the only one with active development, you might want to check that you are compiling libata into your kernel. 

If you type "lsmod" you can see what modules are being used by your kernel, and if you type 
```

modinfo MODULENAME
```
you can get a short description of the purpose of the module. 

Putting the two together:```


for mod in $(lsmod | awk '{print $1}'); do echo "MODINFO $mod"; modinfo $mod; echo ""; done
```
This command will list short descriptions of every module your kernel has loaded.

By scanning through the output you should be able to find what modules are controlling your hard drive(s).
 
[*]I'm not sure why kernelcheck inserted a kernel line in your menu.lst that used the device name instead of UUID. When I used the CLI method the the first page of this thread, the kernel line that I got used UUIDs.
[/list]

---

### Post by tormod on 2008-09-24
> **plowna said:**
> What makes it frustrating is when the kernel detects the drive as /dev/hd* is that things like fstab are written for /dev/sd*.
You should use UUID in /etc/fstab as well.
> 
If indeed it is better to specify by UUID, why doesn't kernelcheck and/or dpkg use this? What about ubuntu? Does ubuntu do this by default?
Ubuntu does this by default since Ubuntu 6.10. When upgrading to 6.10 or newer, older systems are automatically migrated from device names to UUID, both in /etc/fstab and /boot/grub/menu.lst.

---

> Hello, i'm a novice on forum and Linux. For some reason I need to compile a newer kernel (my setup is Ubuntu 8.04.1 x64)with large pages support. Any help appreciated.

Download all of the packages that the first page said to install with "sudo apt-get install" from [packages.ubuntu.com]("http://packages.ubuntu.com/"). Then copy them over to your linux box and install them all at once with ```
sudo dpkg -i [package names]
```
After that, just continue with step 2 in the guide. Anytime apt-get comes up, just download it from packages.ubuntu.com and install them manually. Or, in the case of wget, the full URL is given, so you can download the file from said URL on your Windows box and copy it over.

### Post by Dark_X on 2008-10-08
I was wondering if there are any files I can delete after compiling the kernel.  I moved the debian packages and source archive to a thumb drive but I am still missing a lot of space.  What can I do?

---

### Post by unutbu on 2008-10-08
```
make-kpkg clean          # Cleans  the kernel source directory of all files created by target build, and runs a make distclean.
make-kpkg modules_clean  # This cleans all packages  under  MODULE_LOC
```
(See man make-kpkg.)

If this does not free up enough space, it is also possible to remove the entire /usr/src/linux directory, though of course this will remove your ability to compile kernels.

.103 is the main machine (localhost) with one core and should be able to do 4 tasks as indicated.

When restricting work to the dual core machine I get 8 tasks running, when restricting work to the main machine I get 4 tasks running. Seems ok, but when both are allowed to work only 4 tasks per host are issued.

What am I missing?

Another thing: I get a lot of ```
(dcc_mkdir) ERROR: mkdir //.distcc failed: Permission denied
```messages during compilation but the final compilation is fine. Any ideas?

### Post by sephiroth135 on 2008-10-28
> **jvincent08 said:**
> I'm trying to compile 2.6.27.2 on Intrepid Beta, and I'm running into errors (for the first time following this guide) starting at make-kpkg clean:
-------
Any ideas?

This happened to me and I solved it by disabling "Paravirtualized guest support" in the configuration. After doing this, it started compiling as a x86_64 (what I wanted) instead of Xen (what I did not want).

Hope this helps.

---

### Post by jvincent08 on 2008-10-28
> **sephiroth135 said:**
> This happened to me and I solved it by disabling "Paravirtualized guest support" in the configuration. After doing this, it started compiling as a x86_64 (what I wanted) instead of Xen (what I did not want).

Hope this helps.

I've since removed Intrepid from my system, but thanks for the reply :) If I get this error again when using the final release, I'll give this a try.

### Post by ph3ar on 2008-11-04
Hello, really nice post... :D

I'm trying to figure out how to install the latest fglrx driver (module) for ati 9700 mobile graphic card. 

Do i also need to compile also linux-headers?

---

### Post by jvincent08 on 2008-11-05
> **ph3ar said:**
> Hello, really nice post... :D

I'm trying to figure out how to install the latest fglrx driver (module) for ati 9700 mobile graphic card. 

Do i also need to compile also linux-headers?

Linux-headers should be compiled already if you followed the steps in the first post. You can download ATI drivers from their website:
[http://ati.amd.com/support/driver.HTML](http://ati.amd.com/support/driver.HTML)

You may also be able to download drivers from Ubuntu's repositories. Go to System > Administration > Hardware Drivers to see if your card is listed. I should probably recommend doing this first, since thats what everyone else does, but I personally have never had any luck doing that with my nVidia card.

### Post by jvincent08 on 2008-11-18
> **josedb said:**
> i mean. the smaller kernel you have the faster it boots. so, if iam compiling the kernel my self, i need to know what stuff is not needed to me. for example: i dont need to use modem, so telephony support can be supressed. but there are a lot of things that i dont have any idea what are the for. like NETFILTER Xtables support, is it really needed? or its specific? RAM/ROM/FLASH chip driver? etc

Usually, in the description for obscure options, it'll say "if you don't know whether or not you need this, select [yes or no]". If it doesn't, just google it to find out what it does :)

---

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2008-11-18
> **josedb said:**
> i mean. the smaller kernel you have the faster it boots. so, if iam compiling the kernel my self, i need to know what stuff is not needed to me. for example: i dont need to use modem, so telephony support can be supressed. but there are a lot of things that i dont have any idea what are the for. like NETFILTER Xtables support, is it really needed? or its specific? RAM/ROM/FLASH chip driver? etc
There is a help option for most items. In general, compiling something as a (M)odule will not make your kernel bigger if you don't have the hardware that matches that module (it will just make compile time a bit longer, but chances are you'll be doing something else while the kernel is compiling).

There is a certain amount of trial and error with rolling your own kernel. Do your best, and if it doesn't boot.. back to the drawing board.

---

### Post by MianoSM on 2008-12-03
I recently built my own 2.6.27.5 kernel, and immediately went to building a .7 patched kernel.

On the dpkg -i step you will have issues with the .5 kernel installed, with one of the /lib/fw files already being installed, and it will error out on you trying to install .7.

In order to avoid it - I actually removed the 2.6.27.5 dpkg, and then installed the .7 package.

Not exactly the most comfortable way to install a new kernel, but it does work - and it's not so uncomfortable if you have more then one kernel already installed.

### Post by tormod on 2008-12-12
> **questioning said:**
> i would love to streamline my kernel some more so i would like to just kick out all the drivers i will never need
I have asked earlier in this thread about any measured performance gain by compiling your own kernels, but no one has given any compelling numbers. I stopped compiling my own kernels many years ago. If the stock kernel has performance issues, please identify the problem and file a bug so that it will gain everybody. I hope you can use all your energy and CPU time to help fixing real bugs instead, thus making Linux and Ubuntu better for other people also.
> is my assumption correct that all my needed drivers are listed when i execute "lsmod" ? or do i forget some "hidden drivers" ?
Some drivers are compiled "built-in", others as modules. Usually only the modules that you need are loaded anyway, so filtering out modules that are not needed will not gain much, only shorten your compile time. You can save some kernel boot time by opting out the built-in drivers, but bugs aside, only a few seconds.

Some disadvantages of compiling your own kernel:
- wasting time and energy
- you'll miss security patches in the official kernel
- you have to do it again if you get a new piece of hardware

I know I get very popular posting this in this crowd, but please complement any "but I need to compile because" remarks with links to relevant bug reports.

---

Now that's an excellent reason.
> 
and btw, if i compile my own kernel i can actually optimize it for my processor i really use.
This comes up again all the time, but the Ubuntu kernel actually does run-time optimizations depending on the processor. So again, hard facts abouts how much speed you gain would be interesting. The Ubuntu kernel team asks themselves this from time to time, but tests have shown little impact. It used to be the kernel algorithms were chosen at compile time, but now they are roughly all compiled and included, and the code path is selected when the kernel boots.

---

theres not much you need to know about dkms. if you install new kernel it compiles your drivers for it.. finito, thats all there is to it.

### Post by graysky on 2008-12-28
I compiled my 2.6.27.10 kernel using the /boot/config-2.6.27-generic file as a starting point (just added a few minor tweaks including my processor type which is Newer Core2/Xeon and upping the timer frequency from 250 to 300 Hz).  I also disabled Xen support because it gives errors otherwise.  The main reason I recompiled was so that my cpu frequency scaler would work which is doesn't with the stock Ubuntu kernel.

ANYWAY, here is my problem: my linux-image-2.6.27.10-custom_amd64.deb is enormous at 236 M.  This is in stark contrast to the standard kernel-image.deb which is 22 M.  Also, when I compile kernels under Debian/Lenny using the Debian config, they are also around 22 M.  Here is the .config I used.  Please let me know what I'm doing wrong.

### Post by huntzire on 2008-12-28
> **graysky said:**
> I compiled my 2.6.27.10 kernel using the /boot/config-2.6.27-generic file as a starting point (just added a few minor tweaks including my processor type which is Newer Core2/Xeon and upping the timer frequency from 250 to 300 Hz).  I also disabled Xen support because it gives errors otherwise.  The main reason I recompiled was so that my cpu frequency scaler would work which is doesn't with the stock Ubuntu kernel.

ANYWAY, here is my problem: my linux-image-2.6.27.10-custom_amd64.deb is enormous at 236 M.  This is in stark contrast to the standard kernel-image.deb which is 22 M.  Also, when I compile kernels under Debian/Lenny using the Debian config, they are also around 22 M.  Here is the .config I used.  Please let me know what I'm doing wrong.

Thanks!

```
#
# Automatically generated make config: don't edit
# Linux kernel version: 2.6.27.10-xeon64
# Sun Dec 28 11:25:50 2008
#

#

CONFIG_ARCH_SPARSEMEM_DEFAULT=y
CONFIG_ARCH_SPARSEMEM_ENABLE=y
CONFIG_ARCH_SELECT_MEMORY_MODEL=y

CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_MANUAL=y

# CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_STATIC is not set
CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_EXTREME=y
CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP_ENABLE=y
CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP=y

#
# Memory hotplug is currently incompatible with Software Suspend
#
CONFIG_PAGEFLAGS_EXTENDED=y
CONFIG_SPLIT_PTLOCK_CPUS=4
CONFIG_MIGRATION=y
CONFIG_RESOURCES_64BIT=y
CONFIG_ZONE_DMA_FLAG=1
CONFIG_BOUNCE=y
CONFIG_VIRT_TO_BUS=y
CONFIG_MMU_NOTIFIER=y
CONFIG_X86_RESERVE_LOW_64K=y
CONFIG_MTRR=y
CONFIG_MTRR_SANITIZER=y
CONFIG_MTRR_SANITIZER_ENABLE_DEFAULT=0
CONFIG_MTRR_SANITIZER_SPARE_REG_NR_DEFAULT=1

CONFIG_EFI=y
CONFIG_SECCOMP=y
# CONFIG_HZ_100 is not set
# CONFIG_HZ_250 is not set
CONFIG_HZ_300=y

CONFIG_SCHED_HRTICK=y
CONFIG_KEXEC=y
CONFIG_CRASH_DUMP=y
CONFIG_PHYSICAL_START=0x200000
CONFIG_RELOCATABLE=y
CONFIG_PHYSICAL_ALIGN=0x200000
CONFIG_HOTPLUG_CPU=y

```


I'm not directly a expert but Id use make xconfig and simply cut out the fat by compling less modules. You see when you compile modules like the default config has you do, it is included in the kernel deb, and generic kernels include alot of support for various hardware, even hardware you dont use. Id use make xconfig and specify processor, and such, and also cut out the obivous like telephahy, ISDN equipment drivers, Dallas 1 wire etc things like that.

once you cut down on the drivers you should be fine, also if you have your system setup for LVN or Raid support dont disable that in the kernel config.

---

### Post by graysky on 2008-12-28
...but this is more or less the same config file that the stock unbuntu kernel comes with.. I shouldn't have to switch off modules.  There has to be some option that controls this.

---

### Post by huntzire on 2008-12-28
> **graysky said:**
> ...but this is more or less the same config file that the stock unbuntu kernel comes with.. I shouldn't have to switch off modules.  There has to be some option that controls this.


The stock ubuntu kernel has to be quite big, after all its made to seamlessly support alot of different kinds of hardware, all i know is when I selectively compile only the modules I needed and configured my kernel with options appliable, I got my deb file to 16.3 megs

---
