CONFIG_SECURITY_FILE_CAPABILITIES=y
CONFIG_SECURITY_DEFAULT_MMAP_MIN_ADDR=0
CONFIG_SECURITY_SMACK=y
CONFIG_XOR_BLOCKS=m
CONFIG_ASYNC_CORE=m
CONFIG_ASYNC_MEMCPY=m
CONFIG_ASYNC_XOR=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO=y

#
# Crypto core or helper
#
# CONFIG_CRYPTO_FIPS is not set
CONFIG_CRYPTO_ALGAPI=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_ALGAPI2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_AEAD=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_AEAD2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_BLKCIPHER=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_BLKCIPHER2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_HASH=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_HASH2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RNG=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RNG2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_MANAGER=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_MANAGER2=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_GF128MUL=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_NULL=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CRYPTD=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_AUTHENC=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TEST=m

#
# Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CCM=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_GCM=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SEQIV=m

#
# Block modes
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CBC=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CTR=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CTS=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_ECB=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_LRW=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_PCBC=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_XTS=m

#
# Hash modes
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_HMAC=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_XCBC=m

#
# Digest
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CRC32C=m
# CONFIG_CRYPTO_CRC32C_INTEL is not set
CONFIG_CRYPTO_MD4=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_MD5=y
CONFIG_CRYPTO_MICHAEL_MIC=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RMD128=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RMD160=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RMD256=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_RMD320=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SHA1=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SHA256=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SHA512=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TGR192=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_WP512=m

#
# Ciphers
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_AES=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_AES_X86_64=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_ANUBIS=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_ARC4=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_BLOWFISH=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CAMELLIA=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CAST5=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_CAST6=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_DES=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_FCRYPT=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_KHAZAD=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SALSA20=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SALSA20_X86_64=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SEED=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_SERPENT=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TEA=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TWOFISH=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TWOFISH_COMMON=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_TWOFISH_X86_64=m

#
# Compression
#
CONFIG_CRYPTO_DEFLATE=m
CONFIG_CRYPTO_LZO=m

#
# Random Number Generation
#
# CONFIG_CRYPTO_ANSI_CPRNG is not set
# CONFIG_CRYPTO_HW is not set
CONFIG_HAVE_KVM=y
# CONFIG_VIRTUALIZATION is not set

#
# Library routines
#
CONFIG_BITREVERSE=y
CONFIG_GENERIC_FIND_FIRST_BIT=y
CONFIG_GENERIC_FIND_NEXT_BIT=y
CONFIG_CRC_CCITT=m
CONFIG_CRC16=m
CONFIG_CRC_T10DIF=m
CONFIG_CRC_ITU_T=m
CONFIG_CRC32=y
CONFIG_CRC7=m
CONFIG_LIBCRC32C=m
CONFIG_ZLIB_INFLATE=y
CONFIG_ZLIB_DEFLATE=m
CONFIG_LZO_COMPRESS=m
CONFIG_LZO_DECOMPRESS=m
CONFIG_REED_SOLOMON=m
CONFIG_REED_SOLOMON_DEC16=y
CONFIG_TEXTSEARCH=y
CONFIG_TEXTSEARCH_KMP=m
CONFIG_TEXTSEARCH_BM=m
CONFIG_TEXTSEARCH_FSM=m
CONFIG_PLIST=y
CONFIG_HAS_IOMEM=y
CONFIG_HAS_IOPORT=y
CONFIG_HAS_DMA=y
CONFIG_CHECK_SIGNATURE=y

```

Hope it helps.

### Post by graysky on 2008-12-28
Thanks... man you really did strip it down to bare bones didn't you :)

EDIT: Fixed!

I went though line-by-line with the Debian kernel config and indeed, the Ubuntu config had a bunch of debugging options enabled under Kernel and elsewhere.  Just got the kernel built and it's the right size after disabling these debugging options.

```
6.5M 2008-12-28 16:36 linux-headers-2.6.27.10-ubuntu64_1_amd64.deb
22M 2008-12-28 16:36 linux-image-2.6.27.10-ubuntu64_1_amd64.deb
```

Now the real question is why doesn't the kernel-image in the repo have the same huge size?  After all, it is built from the same config file as the one I copied over from the /boot isn't it :(

> **ThunderRd said:**

### Post by /carlito on 2009-01-09
> **ThunderRd said:**
> Do you mean the linux-image source file?  Or something else?

If you look at the output you can see the script dies when executing nvidia-common.

```
run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20
```

Move this file to /tmp and try to install the kernel-image again. Afterwards you can put the file back to its location. Use this one liner to achieve all this. 

Replace **_your-kernel-image.deb**_ with your appropriate package name!!

```
$ sudo mv /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common /tmp && sudo dpkg -i your-kernel-image.deb && sudo mv /tmp/nvidia-common /etc/kernel/postinst.d/
```

---

### Post by /carlito on 2009-01-09
> **ThunderRd said:**
> Any other suggestions? ;) and thanks...

 Try to "apt-get --purge linux-image-2.6.28" and reinstalling the kernel image. Looks like something went wrong.

I had the same problem as you had (with the nvidia-common) and didn't place the file back to its location. Maybe that is what is causing the problem on your box?

My advice would be to apt-get --purge your kernel image, mv the nvidia-common to /tmp and dpkg -i your kernel image. Post back here when you've booted into your fresh and steamy new kernel!

---

### Post by ThunderRd on 2009-01-09
Yup.  That did it. ;)

After purging the image it allowed dpkg to complete the installation.  It booted, but there are some minor problems in the boot; I'll run menuconfig and recompile tomorrow.  I prolly missed a setting or two, but I can't be too far from getting it right.  It's a bit of a trial and error until I get my .config file just right.

It also broke the nVidia driver and Compiz, but I expected that.  Also not a big problem.

I'm satisfied that I should be able to get it running properly after I recompile; it may take a couple of tries but that's OK.  I just want to cut out the fat from the Ubuntu kernel.  I'll remember to purge if I get the same problem installing the .debs.

Thanks a lot m'man.  I'll post back here if I have another problem. ;)

### Post by /carlito on 2009-01-09
> **ThunderRd said:**
> It also broke the nVidia driver and Compiz, but I expected that.  

You need to get the 180 beta driver for your nvidia card (I presume it's a recent card) . 

Leave the ubuntu version installed an run the binary from nvidia in the console, rmmod and modprobe nvidia, /etc/init.d/gdm start and you're good 2 go.

One more thing though, is the only reason you're doing this to cut some "fat" from the kernel? If so I wouldn't advise it because the performace difference is minimal and you risk on breaking things with an upgrade!

### Post by dk75 on 2009-01-17
```
Reversed (or previously applied) patch detected! Assume -R? [n]
```
it means that patch was already applied and you can remove it from source if you agree to it ("y").

### Post by Yellow Pasque on 2009-01-19
> Support for ScaleMP vSMP (X86_VSMP)
vSMP is a virtualization technology for server clusters. ( [http://www.techworld.com/opsys/news/index.cfm?newsID=11850&pagtype=all](http://www.techworld.com/opsys/news/index.cfm?newsID=11850&pagtype=all) ) Unless you're using that virtualization technology, don't enable this option, even if you do have an Intel CPU. The only reason it mentions EM64T is because Intel is the official supplier for these types of clusters.

> Symmetric multi-processing support (SMP)
Keep this enabled on multi-core/processor systems. Make sure you have a sufficient number of cores set. Default is 8, and I would recommend most people leave it there unless they're hurting for RAM and want to save a few KB of RAM.

---

...
test ! -f .config || cp -pf .config config.precious
test ! -e stamp-building || rm -f stamp-building
test ! -f Makefile || \
            make    **ARCH=xen** distclean
...
ABORT: stage5 returned exit status 2
It thinks your architecture is 'xen' as in a xen server. The problem is that xen is a sub-architecture. I got this error too at one time, but using Ubuntu Jaunty and latest Ubuntu kernel-source package seems to have fixed it.

If you're still getting the error, try manually specifying your archictecture, e.g.:
```
make-kpkg --arch=x86 clean
```
You can see a list of valid architectures with:
```
ls <path to your kernel source>/arch
```

---

> It thinks your architecture is 'xen' as in a xen server. The problem is that xen is a sub-architecture. I got this error too at one time, but using Ubuntu Jaunty and latest Ubuntu kernel-source package seems to have fixed it.[/code]

Thanks for the tip. I updated to latest kernel source and now I am rolling.

### Post by Praxicoide on 2009-01-30
Hello. As I wrote [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=618563&page=34) I tried to compile a kernel for an old Sony Vaio without success using both KernelCheck and the instructions presented here. Since that post, I have tried it again, this time, thinking that it could be a patching error, using only the 2.6.28 kernel. Unfortunately I got the same &#8220;non-literal&#8221; error (I don't recall exactly what they say, since the output is huge. Is this saved anyplace so I can grep it or can it be done somehow, using yes for example?) .
 
The reason I insist on compiling is that 1.- The computer's specs leave a lot to be desired. In fact, I probably should be using a leaner distro than Xubuntu, but I've really come to like Ubuntu, and actually, once you get rid of the gnome dependencies, Xubuntu is  pretty fast (currently only Synaptic is a peg to load, but I try to avoid using it as much as possible). And 2.- The wireless card I have is Atheros 802.11n based. I could only get it to work with the 2.6.27.11 kernel and the backports modules, and that was only until yesterday. For some reason the thing is no longer working properly (very low signal, doesn't connect). 

So, in order to get wireless working again, I tried to install the compat-wireless modules available at [http://wireless.kernel.org/](http://wireless.kernel.org/) I got them to download and everything, but when it came to building them, lo and behold, I got the same &#8220;non-literal argument, no arguments to build&#8221; error (and of course the wireless broke down, I'll now try with Wicd). So it would seem that the problem is either in build-essential or some other tool. 

ANYWAYS, based on this, what I will try to do now, is to build the kernel using another computer (a Dell Inspiron 700m with Ubuntu 8.10) and then move the .deb to the intended one to install. Adapting the instructions by Master Kernel, I guess I would have to perform the following. Please someone correct this if I'm mistaken, I'm not very experienced:

1.- On the auxiliary computer (not the one to be updated), download the kernel and unpack it

```
wget -c http://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/linux-2.6.28.tar.bz2 && tar -xvjf linux-2.6.28.tar.bz2
```

2.- Move into it and download the patch

```
cd ~/linux-2.6.28 &&  wget -c http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/patch-2.6.28.2.bz2
```

4.- Apply the patch 

```
bzcat patch-2.6.28.2.bz2| patch -p1
```

5.- Exit the folder and pack it

```
 cd .. && tar -c ~/linux-2.6.28
```

NOTE: I'm sure there's an option that could be used to create the tar file quietly, but I don't know it.

7.- Put the tarfile in an USB or use some other method to get it on the target computer

8.- Move to the target computer and gain root access

```
sudo -s
```

9.Copy the tarfile from the CD, USB, or wherever to the /usr/src directory

10.- Move into the /usr/src directory and unpack the kernel

```
cd /usr/src &&  tar -xvjf linux-2.6.28.tar.bz2
```

11.- Remove the linux directory, make a symbolic link to the new kernel and move into it

```
rm -rf linux && ln -s /usr/src/linux-2.6.28 linux && cd /usr/src/linux
```

12.- Import configuration and get kernel options

```
cp /boot/config-$(uname -r) .config && yes "" | make oldconfig
```

NOTE: Is this necessary? Can't I get copy the config file and take it to the other computer instead? 

13.- Exit the folder and pack it again

```
 cd .. && tar -c /usr/src/linux-2.6.28
```

14.- Take the tarfile to the auxiliary computer again so it can be compiled. I'll do it on the home folder

15.- Unpack the tarfile and move into this directory

```
 tar -xvjf linux-2.6.28.tar.bz2 && cd ~/linux-2.6.28
```

16.- Choose the appropriate kernel options

```
make xconfig
```

17 .- Build kernel

```
make-kpkg clean
```

18.- Make debs 

```
CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 make-kpkg --initrd --revision=386 kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
```

NOTE: Do I need to use sudo here?

19- Move the two .debs to the target computer's /usr/src directories.

20.-  On the target computer, delete the linux and linux-2.6.28 directories, move in the linux-2.6.28 directory again from the auxiliary computer and create a symbolic link between a new linux directory and that directory.

21.- Install the kernel on the target computer (assuming you are still in /usr/src here with root access)

```
dpkg -i linux*2.6.28*.deb
```

Would this work? Or do you need to be in the /usr/src/linux directory in order to build the kernel? I would appretiate if somebody could let me know.


[size=3]EDIT:[/size] Doesn't work. Upon building (step 17 here) the program complains about missing architecture settings (and that's even with the --arch=x86 option) and proceeds to erase the config file. 

Oh, well. I'll try to see why the other computer can't build correctly.

### Post by The_Rebel52 on 2009-02-05
Whats the point of doing this when the default Ubuntu kernel actually has more hardware support (thanks to the inclusion of various patches/modules added by the Ubuntu devs)

End results i got from this:

A kernel that actually took longer to load and had less hardware support.

Also ran some benchmarks, no real improvements in disk, network or memory I/O.

### Post by toopi on 2009-02-09
> **The_Rebel52 said:**
> Whats the point of doing this when the default Ubuntu kernel actually has more hardware support (thanks to the inclusion of various patches/modules added by the Ubuntu devs)

End results i got from this:

A kernel that actually took longer to load and had less hardware support.

Also ran some benchmarks, no real improvements in disk, network or memory I/O.

Because we can???  :P

Joking aside, some people feel that they have to run the latest (and greatest?) vanilla kernel from kernel.org, and it really pleases them, including me of course.

Sometimes you need those latest additions of modules.  (Yes, I'm aware that you can sometimes grab them and compile them into your own kernel.)

Sometimes you feel like, "Oh! I gotta try this new SLUB allocator thingy...", after reading about it on kernelnewbies.org.

Most of the times, at least for me, it's the curiosity...  and that pleasant feeling after successfully booting into your newest kernel...  :)

### Post by KuroYoma on 2009-02-19
Ok. The installation process was going to the /etc/kernel/postinst.d/ folder and going through the files there before installation. So I removed nvidia-common from the folder and the installation went great. I don't have anything nvidia in my laptop so I know I don't need it. For now its only 3 seconds but I shaved that much off my boot up. Next I am going to implement ext4fs and see if that does anything.

Hi

I have installed but the touchpad does not work.

The package xserver-xorg-input-synaptics is installed.


For **cd .. && dpkg -i linux*2.6.28*.deb**
i get
> run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20
Failed to process /etc/kernel/postinst.d at /var/lib/dpkg/info/linux-image-2.6.28.8.postinst line 1181.
dpkg*: erreur de traitement de linux-image-2.6.28.8 (--install)*:

Des erreurs ont été rencontrées pendant l'exécution*:
 linux-image-2.6.28.8

For fixing this
> aptitude purge nvidia-common
cd .. && dpkg -i linux*2.6.28*.deb
aptitude install nvidia-common

### Post by deepspring on 2009-03-28
> **BBBolded said:**
> Examining /etc/kernel/postinst.d.
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/dkms
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common
run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20
Failed to process /etc/kernel/postinst.d at /var/lib/dpkg/info/linux-image-2.6.29-ultimate.postinst line 1181.
dpkg: error processing linux-image-2.6.29-ultimate (--configure):
 subprocess post-installation script returned error exit status 2
Errors were encountered while processing:
 linux-image-2.6.29-ultimate
E: Sub-process /usr/bin/dpkg returned an error code (1)

Don't worry the kernel installed properly, the error message is a left over for the DKMS (?) functionality.

This usually works for the above error, simply remove the nvidia-common package like so:

```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-common
```The kernel should install and configure itself automatically on completion of this command. Once its finished, just run the following to install nvidia-common again.

```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-common
```You will probably want to re-install the nvidia drivers just to be on safe side.

### Post by dk75 on 2009-04-22
> **wrt54gl said:**
> I can't seem to find anything on the first page that deals with that. I do not have a config file to import.

Ubuntu without installed config file?
```
ls -lA /boot/*config*
```


> **Temüjin said:**
> You need to do a menuconfig and that will generate one for you.

Generating config from scratch isn't a good idea.

### Post by deepspring on 2009-04-22
> **boylan said:**
> OK, I tried to add a custom patch to my kernel because I was having some I/O problems with one of my SATA drives and found a kernel patch to fix it.  However, during the process, I accidentally turned off the power strip while the kernelcheck was still going at it.  Now, I can't update anything - the kernel or other packages - I keep getting this error:

```
E: The package linux-image-2.6.28.8-ultimate needs to be reinstalled, but I can't find an archive for it.
```My attempts to fix this problem have been fruitless and searches came up with solutions that didn't help.  Any suggestions?  Thanks.

Try:
```

sudo apt-get remove linux-image-2.6.28.8-ultimate linux-headers-2.6.28.8-ultimate

```

If you followed the guide exactly, there should be a pair of deb files in "/usr/src". So if the above fails...

Try:
```
dpkg -r linux-*-2.6.28.8-ultimate*
```

### Post by toopi on 2009-04-27
> **loomsen said:**
> It's been ever since. Doesn't have to do anything to do with any _ctl_ which _somehow_ doesn't work.

...

If you wanna get rid of it you gotta recompile your kernel.

UPDATE:  The following method does not work.  Please refer to the next post.

Can't we use the "blacklist" to disable modules from auto-loading here?

e.g.  Inside /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

blacklist ipv6
blacklist ip6_tables

```

There may be more modules to be blacklisted though.

---

### Post by dkruidhof on 2009-04-28
> **toopi said:**
> Can't we use the "blacklist" to disable modules from auto-loading here?

e.g.  Inside /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

blacklist ipv6
blacklist ip6_tables

```

There may be more modules to be blacklisted though.

You can use blacklist to disable modules. However, in Jaunty, ipv6 is not a module, as has been stated a few times now, so that is irrelevant. a custom kernel must be recompiled if ipv6 is causing you problems.

However, this is getting rather off-topic. Sorry I mentioned it in my post.

To answer my own question that started all this mess, you can delete that huge directory (linux-source-*)you create while compiling so long as you don't need it to recompile with a different config soon. Just make sure you don't delete the new folder that contains the installed kernel (linux-headers-*).

---

### Post by Praxicoide on 2009-04-28
I compiled the .30-rc3 kernel using this guide for my Dell inspiron 700m. Wow! The boot-up just flies. I already compiled the .28.1 kernel, and even though I noticed a boost then, it was nothing compared to this one.

Unfortunately, boot-up is as far as I got. Ubuntu couldn't mount my ext4 /home partition (even though / is also ext4) saying that it needed the CONFIG_LBD module (I guess I disabled too much stuff). Meaning that I have to compile it again (I already compiled it once before, because put the driver for the ipw2200 wireless in the kernel, instead of a module as you're supposed to do) (whoops!).

I'll perform this again and try it out. But I have a question: the kernel failed to load Apparmor. I'm a home user on a laptop, so I'm not extremely worried, but I'm wondering if Ubuntu uses it for anything by default (wireless or ethernet, for example).

Another question is that when the final .30 kernel comes out, can erase this rc folder after compiling the new one. I'm asking because it's such a huge directory.  When I uninstalled the failed kernel last time, I rebooted under .28 and then tried to delete the .30-rc3 directory and the computer immediately crashed.

EDIT:

From Ubuntu Wiki:

"Kernel upgrade / apparmor-module-source upgrade
When a new kernel is installed or when a new version of apparmor-module-source is installed, the apparmor module has to be recompiled :

```
sudo m-a -v -t -f build apparmor-modules
sudo m-a -v -t install apparmor-modules
```"

I don't see myself using Apparmor at all, so first I have to wonder if I need to bother (i.e. is there any risk of breakage), and next, if I do, if I need to download this module or what, before carrying out these commands. I don't even know what "m-a" does, I can't find any info on it.

### Post by snowx1000 on 2009-05-22
Just an FYI for people wondering why their kernels are huge (mine were hundreds of megs), remove everything related to debugging (in make xconfig search for debug). My kernel is now 17MB. Also, to keep the kernel source down to a minimum run a 'make clean' after the debs are created.

---

linux-backports-modules & linux-restricted-modules

### Post by stoner_di on 2009-05-24
&#1052;ethod of compilation in the beginning is wonderful:KS.

My problems:

1.I want to ask someone tried to incorporate the bootup logo (in a mini tux top left of the display to load the system).
Perform all necessary for this:
- when compiling the kernel, I select this:

Support for frame buffer devices FB->VESA VGA graphics support FB_VESA

Select all in the:
 
Console display driver support ->

selct all in the:

Bootup logo LOGO->

-in the GRUB, in the file menu.lst fix this row:

kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.29.3 root=UUID=d5b52903-5.... video=vesafb vga=0x0323 ro quiet  

-in the file /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-framebuffer.conf, comment this row:

#blacklist vesafb

-in the file /etc/initramfs-tools/modules, add:

fbcon
vesafb 

-in the end update initramfs this command:

update-initramfs -u

In all my UBUNTU not load boot logo!!!!!!!!

2. I can not find a method to patching the kernel with AppArmor!!!!

In all this, my kernel is compiled successfully and when I optimized for my hardware, is about 12 MB

### Post by _Q_ on 2009-06-07
Hey everyone :)
I just compiled the new 2.6.29.4 using procedure in this guide, but did some of my own configuration..

I noticed some differences in syslog with the new and with the old kernel (from ubutnu repos) and I hope someone could comment with some explanations. :)
Some lines show only in new, and some only in the old syslog...

Problem with the map file (/usr/src/linux/System.map file exists!)

New kernel syslog
```
Inspecting /boot/System.map-2.6.29.4
Inspecting /usr/src/linux/System.map
Cannot find map file.
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Inspecting /boot/System.map-2.6.24-24-generic
Loaded 27917 symbols from /boot/System.map-2.6.24-24-generic.
Symbols match kernel version 2.6.24.
```

Some extra lines in the new syslog...?
```
Initializing cgroup subsys cpuset
KERNEL supported cpus:
  Intel GenuineIntel
last_pfn = 0x7ffb0 max_arch_pfn = 0x100000
kernel direct mapping tables up to 377fe000 @ 7000-c000
RAMDISK: 37a5e000 - 37fef492
Allocated new RAMDISK: 007d4000 - 00d65492
Move RAMDISK from 0000000037a5e000 - 0000000037fef491 to 007d4000 - 00d65491
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Initializing cgroup subsys cpuset
Initializing cgroup subsys cpu
```

Again, some extra lines in the new syslog + different formating..?
```
mapped low ram: 0 - 377fe000
  low ram: 00000000 - 377fe000
  bootmap 00008000 - 0000ef00
(9 early reservations) ==> bootmem [0000000000 - 00377fe000]
  #0 [0000000000 - 0000001000]   BIOS data page ==> [0000000000 - 0000001000]
  #1 [0000001000 - 0000002000]    EX TRAMPOLINE ==> [0000001000 - 0000002000]
  #2 [0000006000 - 0000007000]       TRAMPOLINE ==> [0000006000 - 0000007000]
  #3 [0000100000 - 00007d0554]    TEXT DATA BSS ==> [0000100000 - 00007d0554]
  #4 [00007d1000 - 00007d4000]    INIT_PG_TABLE ==> [00007d1000 - 00007d4000]
  #5 [000009fc00 - 0000100000]    BIOS reserved ==> [000009fc00 - 0000100000]
  #6 [0000007000 - 0000008000]          PGTABLE ==> [0000007000 - 0000008000]
  #7 [00007d4000 - 0000d65492]      NEW RAMDISK ==> [00007d4000 - 0000d65492]
  #8 [0000008000 - 000000f000]          BOOTMAP ==> [0000008000 - 000000f000]
Zone PFN ranges:
  DMA      0x00000000 -> 0x00001000
  Normal   0x00001000 -> 0x000377fe
  HighMem  0x000377fe -> 0x0007ffb0
Movable zone start PFN for each node
early_node_map[2] active PFN ranges
    0: 0x00000000 -> 0x0000009f
    0: 0x00000100 -> 0x0007ffb0
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Entering add_active_range(0, 0, 524208) 0 entries of 256 used
Zone PFN ranges:
DMA             0 ->     4096
Normal       4096 ->   229376
HighMem    229376 ->   524208
Movable zone start PFN for each node
early_node_map[1] active PFN ranges
0:        0 ->   524208
```

These lines only appear in the old syslog?
```
Movable zone: 0 pages used for memmap
ACPI: RSDP signature @ 0xC00FC600 checksum 0
ACPI: DMI detected: Hewlett-Packard
```

Again, different formating of registering nosave memory + some extra lines in the new kernel syslog
```
SMP: Allowing 2 CPUs, 0 hotplug CPUs
nr_irqs_gsi: 24
PM: Registered nosave memory: 000000000009f000 - 00000000000a0000
PM: Registered nosave memory: 00000000000a0000 - 00000000000e0000
PM: Registered nosave memory: 00000000000e0000 - 0000000000100000
....
Allocating PCI resources starting at 88000000 (gap: 80000000:7ec00000)
NR_CPUS:2 nr_cpumask_bits:2 nr_cpu_ids:2 nr_node_ids:1
PERCPU: Allocating 36864 bytes of per cpu data
....
Extended CMOS year: 2000
Fast TSC calibration using PIT
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Allocating PCI resources starting at 88000000 (gap: 80000000:7ec00000)
swsusp: Registered nosave memory region: 000000000009f000 - 00000000000a0000
swsusp: Registered nosave memory region: 00000000000a0000 - 00000000000e0000
swsusp: Registered nosave memory region: 00000000000e0000 - 0000000000100000
```

Could this be called a good improvement? (New kernel syslog)
```
Memory: 2065460k/2096832k available (4192k kernel code, 30024k reserved, 1710k data, 520k init, 1187528k highmem)
....
virtual kernel memory layout:
    fixmap  : 0xfff9e000 - 0xfffff000   ( 388 kB)
    pkmap   : 0xff800000 - 0xffc00000   (4096 kB)
    vmalloc : 0xf7ffe000 - 0xff7fe000   ( 120 MB)
    lowmem  : 0xc0000000 - 0xf77fe000   ( 887 MB)
      .init : 0xc06cb000 - 0xc074d000   ( 520 kB)
      .data : 0xc0518094 - 0xc06c39b8   (1710 kB)
      .text : 0xc0100000 - 0xc0518094   (4192 kB)
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Memory: 2065912k/2096832k available (2179k kernel code, 29540k reserved, 1008k data, 368k init, 1179328k highmem)
....
virtual kernel memory layout:
    fixmap  : 0xfff4b000 - 0xfffff000   ( 720 kB)
    pkmap   : 0xff800000 - 0xffc00000   (4096 kB)
    vmalloc : 0xf8800000 - 0xff7fe000   ( 111 MB)
    lowmem  : 0xc0000000 - 0xf8000000   ( 896 MB)
      .init : 0xc0423000 - 0xc047f000   ( 368 kB)
      .data : 0xc0320d78 - 0xc041cdc4   (1008 kB)
      .text : 0xc0100000 - 0xc0320d78   (2179 kB)
```

Again, some differences in the numbers/formating + extra lines in the old kernel syslog...
New kernel syslog
```
Checking if this processor honours the WP bit even in supervisor mode...Ok.
SLUB: Genslabs=12, HWalign=64, Order=0-3, MinObjects=0, CPUs=2, Nodes=1
hpet clockevent registered
HPET: 3 timers in total, 0 timers will be used for per-cpu timer
Calibrating delay loop (skipped), value calculated using timer frequency.. 3990.54 BogoMIPS (lpj=1995274)
```
Old kernel syslog
```
Checking if this processor honours the WP bit even in supervisor mode... Ok.
SLUB: Genslabs=11, HWalign=64, Order=0-1, MinObjects=4, CPUs=2, Nodes=1
hpet clockevent registered
Calibrating delay using timer specific routine.. 3994.59 BogoMIPS (lpj=7989194)
....
SMP alternatives: switching to UP code
Early unpacking initramfs... done
```

Yet again... with some extra lines in old kernel syslog
New kernel syslog
```
ACPI: Core revision 20081204
....
```
Old kernel syslog
```
ACPI: Core revision 20070126
ACPI: Looking for DSDT in initramfs... error, file /DSDT.aml not found.
....
SMP alternatives: switching to SMP code
....
Calibrating delay using timer specific routine.. 3990.00 BogoMIPS (lpj=7980011)
monitor/mwait feature present.
....
ENABLING IO-APIC IRQs
```

These lines show only in the old kernel syslog...
```
CPU0 attaching sched-domain:
domain 0: span 03
groups: 01 02
CPU1 attaching sched-domain:
domain 0: span 03
groups: 02 01
```

And, finnaly... net_namespace difference...(is it good or bad?)
New kernel syslog
```
net_namespace: 960 bytes
```
Old kernel syslog
```

```

### Post by deepspring on 2009-06-10
> **WIGGMPk said:**

Firstly, don't do bump posts, it only annoys people. If someone has a fix for you to try, they will post it IN THEIR OWN TIME.

You can try:
```
sudo apt-get install --reinstall ucf
```Then try installing the new kernel and headers again.

Edit 2: Out of curiosity, do you have a /boot partition, if so how big and is it full?

Please excuse the bump, I am just getting annoyed myself with this.

I do have a /boot partition and it is 512 MB, and there is only 1 kernel image in there so there is more than enough room. It is however worth mentioning that my /boot partition is ext4. Not sure if that would effect the install.

### Post by deepspring on 2009-06-10
> **WIGGMPk said:**
> I do have a /boot partition and it is 512 MB, and there is only 1 kernel image in there so there is more than enough room. It is however worth mentioning that my /boot partition is ext4. Not sure if that would effect the install.

My google search for the error came up with a number of different possible causes, one of them being the /boot partition being full, the other being a missing template file related to the UCF package.

I don't think the boot partition being EXT4 is the problem.

### Post by master_kernel on 2009-06-10
> **jamiyoel said:**
> I'm unable to install libqt3-mt-dev, because it needs libxft-dev. libxft-dev needs libxft2 version 2.4.10-1ubuntu[COLOR=Navy]1.1[/COLOR], but I have libxft2 version 2.1.10-1ubuntu[COLOR=Red]1.3[/COLOR]. If I uninstall libxft2, I get a slew of programs and such that would also be uninstalled.
Any ideas on it?

Well you need a newer version of libxft2 - you highlighted the wrong part: libxft-dev needs libxft2 version 2.[COLOR="Red"]4[/COLOR].10-1ubuntu1.1 and you have libxft2 version 2.[COLOR="Red"]1[/COLOR].10-1ubuntu1.3.

There was a post about that error, I found it after i nuked my system and found a way around it.

Basically if you use ubuntu supplied prioertiy drivers, then your custom kernel will give u errors due to dlkms.

Best thing to do is one of two things.

One: nuke ur system and install the drivers via the offical installer.

Two:find that artcile by punching that into google as I couldnt find the thread by search here :(

### Post by deepspring on 2009-06-13
> **WIGGMPk said:**
> ...

You have a missing "grub.templates" file.

You can probably reinstall grub, see if that fixes it.

```
sudo apt-get install --reinstall grub
```

Or you can try grabbing the attached text file to your home folder, renaming it to "grub.templates" and copying to the "/var/lib/dpkg/info/" folder like so:
```

mv grub.txt grub.templates
sudo cp grub.templates /var/lib/dpkg/info/

```

---

### Post by WIGGMPk on 2009-06-13
> **deepspring said:**
> ...


Thanks mate, that worked perfectly.

Everything installed without issue. I really appreciate your time and effort in solving this.

Since I'm an ignorant git, intimidated by the thought of manually installing a new kernel, I installed the stable version of kernelcheck and ran it. It failed. 

I came here, to the forums, and discovered that the stable version doesn't work with jaunty, so (thanks to the blog - it isn't explained anywhere else - for ignorant gits like myself) I installed bazaar, downloaded and installed the development version, and ran it.

It too, failed at the same place.

Help!

Here is the relevant output (I think) from the kernelcheck.log:

This fix worked fine. I started over with kernelcheck (development) and it seemed to install fine (though being my first kernel I won't really know for a while - I haven't rebooted yet). 

The fix was easy to do, but seemed to beg the problem. I liked what Mike Stroyan said here
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/292606/comments/17](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/292606/comments/17)  about this issue, but I'm so unfamiliar with command structure that I didn't know exactly where or how to place the pipe. I would've like to try it.

$ sudo modprobe -l |grep toshiba
kernel/drivers/acpi/toshiba_acpi.ko

$ sudo modprobe toshiba_acpi
FATAL: Error inserting toshiba_acpi (/lib/modules/2.6.28-11-generic/kernel/drivers/acpi/toshiba_acpi.ko): No such device

Would building a custom kernel help me to get suspend/resume and other acpi functions working ok ?

### Post by loomsen on 2009-06-23
> **edgar83 said:**
> Hi, is there a way to put a "custom" kernel into an installation iso?

I've got a netbook and want to install my home partiotion into an external SD card. The standard kernel don't "see" the card during installation, one custom kernel I'm trying see the card, so I wonder if I could install with that kernel instead of the default one.

thanks!
Angelo

Hi.

You need to generate an initrd image which contains the modules you need.

initrd=Initial RAM Disk
This will be copied to your RAM, and then your kernel will be initialized and populated from there.

Nearly every distro ships with a mkinitrd tool. The options slightly differ, so please use 
mkinitrd --help
mkinitrd --usage

for usage info on ubuntu.

However, you will have to specify the kernel you want to build against, so you need the headers installed. The command should be sth like
mkinitrd -k $(uname -r) -m (modules to include) [-outputfile]

Make sure you rename it appropriately. For instance, if you'd want to include btrfs, name it initrd-custom-btrfs.img
This isn't necessary, but it's easier to link the initrds you want to use rather then overwriting.
When done, copy the initrd to your /boot/ (/*assuming you followed my hint above and placed your destination image in /tmp/ */) link it in and reboot.

If everything works fine, you may remove your old initrd :)

I used mkinitrd to include lvm2, btrfs and the nouveau modules, wasn't 2 hard.

```
lsinitrd /boot/my-initrd.img >> /tmp/myinitrd-img-log
```
Running the above as root will give you a log file of your initrd. Which modules it includes and the nash script used to actually boot your system.

I'll attach my log so you get an idea. It really sounds more complicated than it actually is.

```

/boot/initrd-2.6.29.4-167.fc11.x86_64.img:

========================================================================

-rwxr-xr-x   1 root     root        90040 May 15 16:17 lib64/libply.so.2.0.0
lrwxrwxrwx   1 root     root           13 Jun 23 06:16 lib64/libz.so.1 -> libz.so.1.2.3
-rwxr-xr-x   1 root     root        85992 Mar 18 11:13 lib64/libz.so.1.2.3
-rwx------   1 root     root         2168 Jun 23 06:16 init
========================================================================
#!/bin/nash

mount -t proc /proc /proc
setquiet
echo Mounting proc filesystem
echo Mounting sysfs filesystem
mount -t sysfs /sys /sys
echo Creating /dev
mount -o mode=0755 -t tmpfs /dev /dev
mkdir /dev/pts
mount -t devpts -o gid=5,mode=620 /dev/pts /dev/pts
mkdir /dev/shm
mkdir /dev/mapper
echo Creating initial device nodes
mknod /dev/null c 1 3
mknod /dev/zero c 1 5
mknod /dev/systty c 4 0
mknod /dev/tty c 5 0
mknod /dev/console c 5 1
mknod /dev/ptmx c 5 2
mknod /dev/fb c 29 0
mknod /dev/hvc0 c 229 0
mknod /dev/tty0 c 4 0
mknod /dev/tty1 c 4 1
mknod /dev/tty2 c 4 2
mknod /dev/tty3 c 4 3
mknod /dev/tty4 c 4 4
mknod /dev/tty5 c 4 5
mknod /dev/tty6 c 4 6
mknod /dev/tty7 c 4 7
mknod /dev/tty8 c 4 8
mknod /dev/tty9 c 4 9
mknod /dev/tty10 c 4 10
mknod /dev/tty11 c 4 11
mknod /dev/tty12 c 4 12
mknod /dev/ttyS0 c 4 64
mknod /dev/ttyS1 c 4 65
mknod /dev/ttyS2 c 4 66
mknod /dev/ttyS3 c 4 67
daemonize --ignore-missing /bin/plymouthd
echo "Loading i2c-core module"
modprobe -q i2c-core
echo "Loading i2c-algo-bit module"
modprobe -q i2c-algo-bit
echo "Loading drm module"
modprobe -q drm
echo "Loading nouveau module"
modprobe -q nouveau
echo "Loading nvidia module"
modprobe -q nvidia
/lib/udev/console_init tty0
plymouth --show-splash
echo Setting up hotplug.
hotplug
echo Creating block device nodes.
mkblkdevs
echo Creating character device nodes.
mkchardevs
echo "Loading btrfs module"
modprobe -q btrfs
echo &quot;Loading pata_acpi module"
modprobe -q pata_acpi
echo "Loading ata_generic module"
modprobe -q ata_generic
echo Making device-mapper control node
mkdmnod
modprobe scsi_wait_scan
rmmod scsi_wait_scan
mkblkdevs
echo Scanning logical volumes
lvm vgscan --ignorelockingfailure
echo Activating logical volumes
lvm vgchange -ay --ignorelockingfailure  vg_local
resume /dev/mapper/vg_local-LogVolSwap
echo Creating root device.
mkrootdev -t btrfs -o defaults,ro UUID=c5ca49c2-c607-4be3-834e-32aeef479fa7
echo Mounting root filesystem.
mount /sysroot
cond -ne 0 plymouth --hide-splash
echo Setting up other filesystems.
setuproot
loadpolicy
plymouth --newroot=/sysroot
echo Switching to new root and running init.
switchroot
echo Booting has failed.
sleep -1
========================================================================

```

*edit*
[You may want to take a look here too.](http://forums.fedoraforum.org/showthread.php?p=1223584#post1223584)

A little more detailed, the actual module you need doesn't matter, what matters is including the module in your initrd so your sd card will be recognized and mounted from RAM

### Post by mr_raider on 2009-06-29
> **master_kernel said:**
> Purge nvidia-common before installing the package, then reinstall it when the package installs successfully.

That didn't work. Here's is what I did:

Booted with old kernel. Added X updates PPA

[https://launchpad.net/~ubuntu-x-swat/+archive/x-updates/](https://launchpad.net/~ubuntu-x-swat/+archive/x-updates/)

Installed the nvidia 185 updated driver. Then re-installed the 2.6.30 kernel from the mainline archive. This time DKMS properly rebuilt everything. Reboot into 2.6.30, and now my webcam works!

Purge nvidia-common package like so:
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-common
```

Install the kernel and header packages using dpkg.

Then re-install nvidia-common package like so:
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-common
```

### Post by deepspring on 2009-06-30
> **von Stalhein said:**
> I think this looks like my fix as well - but could you please flesh out the dpkg command too?

Thanks.
Just install kernel packages as you normally would:
```

cd /usr/src
sudo dpkg -i linux-image*2.6.30*.deb
sudo dpkg -i linux-headers*2.6.30*.deb

```Edit: This assumes that you followed this threads first post.

### Post by deepspring on 2009-07-01
> **von Stalhein said:**
> Thanks Deepspring.

I got it installed, just chasing some display issues now, I think due to the reason below.

It's set for a verbose start, and I notice that aside form an AppArmor load fail, the 180.11 nVidia drivers also won't load.

I presume there is an option in the kernel to change this? Is this an option I should have specified when the GUI came up early in the kernel install process?

As it is I can't use the nVidia drivers or enable desktop effects.You will need to re-install the nvidia drivers for them to work.

You can try using the Hardware Drivers applet (Found: System -> Administration -> Hardware Drivers) to install them. 

**OR...**

You can install EnvyNG and try that:
```

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install envyng-core envyng-gtk envyng-qt
envyng -k

```

**OR...**

You can try downloading and installing the latest drivers from the nvidia website, which can be a little scary as you have to leave Gnome/X to do it. There is a good guide here:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1125400](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1125400)

You can skip the "Installing Build Deps" part, as you should have everything you need already installed from following the advice in this thread.

I recommend copying and pasting that how-to into OpenOffice.org Write and printing it if you are going to attempt it. You wont have access to a web browser for much of the process (you'll be using the command line directly).

### Post by loomsen on 2009-07-05
> **peterwil said:**
> 
Now I need to start looking at the acpi code modules to understand the overheating problem.. during compile temps went up to 90C.. is this normal ?


While compiling your CPU is under pretty high load, however,90° are everything but normal (my critical temp is set to 85°, switching off my laptop whenever it is reached)

You're probably missing proper ACPI tables in your BIOS (or at least it is ignored)

Use 
```
dmesg | grep -i osi
```

You should see:
> ACPI: BIOS _OSI(Linux) query honored via cmdline

If your output is
> ACPI: BIOS _OSI(Linux) ignoring

Add acpi_osi="Linux" to your kernel parameters in grub, reboot and grep your dmesg again.

If this doesn't fix your problem at all visit 
[http://www.lesswatts.org/projects/index.php](http://www.lesswatts.org/projects/index.php)
or look for the fixing buggy DSDT howto in this section.

Checking your current bootoptions:
```
cat /proc/cmdline
```

To add the option to your commandline, open up /boot/grub/menu.lst as root and look for the altoptions definitions:
> 
## altoption boot targets option
## multiple altoptions lines are allowed
## e.g. altoptions=(extra menu suffix) extra boot options
##      altoptions=(recovery) single
# altoptions=(recovery mode) single

Modify it to read:
```

## altoption boot targets option
## multiple altoptions lines are allowed
## e.g. altoptions=(extra menu suffix) extra boot options
##      altoptions=(recovery) single
# altoptions=(recovery mode) single
# altoptions=(Set Linux acpi) acpi_osi="Linux"

```

Save and close the file, then run
```

sudo update-grub
```

Grub will populate the proper entry, now reboot and choose your just generated entry to boot.

---

### Post by loomsen on 2009-07-06
> **johndessuza said:**
> hi dear friends,
                        I was able to compile version 2.6.19 on my Dell Dimension at work, but after compiling, installing, and rebooting on my Precision M65 laptop, it just hangs at "Begin: Waiting for root file system.
Please help what are doing?

Mount your partitions using LABELS rather than UUIDs.

Set a label on your root partition, for instance "root" (without quotes)

Then change your /etc/fstab from
UUID=XXXX-random-number-XXXXX / <your_fs> <options>
to
LABEL=root / <your_fs> <options>

So basically only change the way it is mounted from UUID to LABEL, then change your grub entry the same way.

Open up /boot/grub/menu.lst and modify the line where your kernel is specified from root=UUID=XXXXX to root=LABEL=root...

Don't forget to update-grub afterwards, reboot, and see if it works.

### Post by v41 on 2009-08-11
So far I've failed to get wireless working with a custom kernel.  Hence I'm wondering whether there's a straightforward way to activate/compile wireless in a custom kernel, without resorting to ndiswrapper?  There has been some discussion of wireless in this and other threads, but some of it has gone over my head and some of it may not be relevant to my wireless card.

My wireless card is broadcom bcm4328 802.11a/b/g/n (rev 03), and this appears not to be supported by the b43 driver.

When using the stock Jaunty kernel my wireless card  makes use of the linux restricted modules.  In particular, there is a module called wl which does not exist in the custom kernel

The custom kernel [2.6.30.4] includes various modules related to broadcom and wireless,
viz broadcom, b43, b43legacy, lib80211, lib80211_crypt_ccmp, lib80211_crypt_tkip, lib80211_crypt_wep.  However NetworkManager does not recognise the wireless card.

Suggestions gratefully received...:P

### Post by v41 on 2009-08-18

Problem solved :D

The driver from broadcom, 

[http://www.broadcom.com/support/802.11/linux_sta.php](http://www.broadcom.com/support/802.11/linux_sta.php)

works with newer cards that are not supported by the b43 opensource driver in the vanilla/mainline kernel.

### Post by Lockheed on 2009-08-31
I was wondering if there is more up to date **tutorial for optimizing kernel** than this link provided in the first post: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=1174954&postcount=507](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=1174954&postcount=507)

Trying to use it with new kernels can be very confusing.

---

### Post by Exodist on 2009-08-31
> **Lockheed said:**
> I was wondering if there is more up to date **tutorial for optimizing kernel** than this link provided in the first post: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=1174954&postcount=507](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=1174954&postcount=507)

Trying to use it with new kernels can be very confusing.

Well after getting your config file, you will basicly pull all the current settings from your current kernel as your defualt in your new kernel.
What I would basicly do is if you NEED something new, look for that option and unclude it to be built. Then what I do is set your desktop pre-emptive up to the newer lower latency desktop one. Then set your timming to 1000. This will speed it up alot.

Thats basicly it. Newer stuff will be marked as NEW in the kernel menu config. If you need it, get it, else dont bother bloating your kernel.

---

> I have that same issue as well, it only really happens to me when I go to 2.6.31 though.

besides my mobo chipset, I dont have any nvidia crap. These shouldnt be listing.

Now I have found them in "etc/kernel/header_postinst.d and postinst.d

Seriously thinking of just removing them. But wanted someone that builds kernels more often then I do so confirm thats just some rubbish left over from the previous kernels that are either now built in the kernel or no longer used.

### Post by del_diablo on 2009-09-09
> all of the command in this thread are doing by super user
u could read it at "sudo -s " command

make
make menu/xconfig
Does these need sudo? Not at all, just if you are outside of the ~/ dir.

```
dpkg-deb: building package `linux-image-2.6.31-its.m65a.nb.01' in `../linux-image-2.6.31-its.m65a.nb.01_2.6.31-its.m65a.nb.01-10.00.Custom_amd64.deb'.

```

There are no .deb files left behind either.

**EDIT:** Solved this myself.  I noticed the permissions on the /usr/src directory had the s attribute set, so just ran:

```
sudo chmod -R a-s /usr/src
```

Trying a new kernel build again. :)

### Post by TSJason on 2009-09-15
> **colo505 said:**
> Thanks. Can you tell me how to install it. Am on 2.6.31/JauntyAMD64. Thanks

Colo,

If you look at the first page of this thread, with the instructions, step number 7 shows how to apply patches. You would just use the same method there as with the bfs and autoiso patch (but instead of using 'bzcat', just use 'cat' since the bfs files are not compressed).

During the "make menuconfig" you'll want to use CK's recommendations; specifically:

> 
THESE ARE OPTIONAL FOR LOWEST LATENCY. YOU DO NOT NEED THESE!
Configure your kernel with 1000Hz, preempt ON and disable dynamic ticks.

### Post by stevenpusser on 2009-09-17
> **go_beep_yourself said:**
> [LIST=1]
[*]Can I still apply a patch to my wireless driver (I compiled as a module) after I have built the kernel?
[*]Is it safe to disable most options in menuconfig that says Intel If I have an amd processor?
[*]Does anybody really know what all of these options mean? I usually leave them as default if I can't figure it out.
[*]How can I do the equivalent of make -j 4 for a multicore processor when using make-kpg?
[*]It would be nice to see some explanation of specific options that most of us can disable if we meet the criteria. I want an efficient kernel for performance.
[/LIST]

1.  If you have the wireless driver source, go ahead and patch, build, and install it over the kernel version.  Be aware that the drivers are in a constant state of flux, and new or renamed drivers may complicate your update.

2.  You should be able to disable Intel hardware drivers safely.

3. If you aren't sure what the option means after reading the hints, just leave it.

4. For four-core builds, execute

export CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=4

just before the kernel build command, though I have seen some advice to make it (cores + 1)...i.e. 5.

Try 8 if the cores are hyperthreaded.

### Post by dk75 on 2009-09-25
[http://www.feedparser.org/](http://www.feedparser.org/)
Install it ( throught Synaptic - "python-feedparser" ) and it gives you function to parse RSS.

Sample code:
```

#! /usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
##############################################################
# parse RSS feed
# RSS.py rss.url no._of_entry_to_parse
##############################################################

import sys
import feedparser
d = feedparser.parse(sys.argv[1])
print
print d.entries[int(sys.argv[2])].title
print
print d.entries[int(sys.argv[2])].description
print
quit()

```

using:
```

#:~ RSS.py http://www.kernel.org/kdist/rss.xml 0

### Post by Dizzle7677 on 2009-09-26
For those that like doing it the old fashioned way, here's a little script I came across and hacked to do everything KC does.Adjust values accordingly. Make executable & Run from /usr/src. Enjoy.

```

#! /bin/sh

# Build_Kernel Ver.00000000000000001
# Run under /usr/src. Adjust <whatever-kernel>.

#Dependency Check
sudo apt-get install -y --force-yes build-essential bin86 kernel-package libqt3-headers libqt3-mt-dev wget libncurses5 libncurses5-dev
#Wget the kernel
wget http://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v2.6/testing/linux-<whatever-kernel>.tar.bz2
#Untar the kernel
sudo tar -xvjf linux-<whatever-kernel>.tar.bz2
#Symbolic link the kernel directory 
sudo ln -s /usr/src/linux-<whatever-kernel> linux
#Apply any patches here. Repeat a Wget for the patch itself but they offer fully patched kernels now. Sweet.
#sudo cp <whatever-patch>.patch linux
cd linux
#Copy the previously used config for re-use in the new kernel.
sudo cp /boot/config-<whatever-kerenel-ver> .config
#Apply any patches here. 'patch -p1 < <whatever-patch>'
#sudo patch -p1 < <whatever-patch>.patch
#Make the Menu. Adjust options -> Save.
sudo make xconfig
#Place a comment here / Only useful if rebuilding 
#sudo make-kpkg clean
#Build the new kernel.Adjust Concurrency to the CPU cores +1. Install if the build completed successfully.
#Add a break here if the build didn't complete successfully. We can just move out of the directory if the stage is successful.
sudo CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=2 make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-candela kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image && cd ..
#Back out of the linux directory
#cd ..
#Install the image and headers
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
#Remove the Kernel Directory and downloaded bz2s.
sudo rm -rf /usr/src/linux-<whatever-kernel> && sudo rm *.bz2
#Symbolic Link the *new* headers directory to /lib/modules/<whatever-kernel>/build & source
#First remove bad links if there are any.
sudo rm /lib/modules/<whatever-new-kernel>/build && sudo rm /lib/modules/<whatever-new-kernel>/source
#Now synbolic link the new header directory.
sudo ln -s /usr/src/linux-headers-<whatever-new-kernel> /lib/modules/<whatever-kernel>/build && sudo ln -s /usr/src/linux-headers-<whatever-new-kernel> /lib/modules/<whatever-kernel>/source
#Reinstall any video,etc drivers after reboot or add a stage for envyng or something like that yourself.;)

```

You must do "make oldconfig" before "make xconfig". None could make good working config out of the blue. It's better to modify working one than to make it from scratch.

### Post by dk75 on 2009-09-29
No.
In some circumstances such kernel might work without any issues, in other circumstances it will not work at all ( errors during boot ) and in other circumstances it will bootup, it will work but then you will see some strange errors wit your software that you won't recognize as broken kernel.

The thing is that your old kernel might have settings that have been removed from new kernel and new kernel might have new features that without "make oldconfig" won't be set and won't be even shown in "make xconfig" to configure.

### Post by masux594 on 2009-10-07
mmm.. i do the same error.. Well in the guide there isn't the command ```
CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-mk kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
``` <- All in one line

But there's the command```
CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-mk kernel_image 
kernel_headers modules_image
``` with a line return !!

So, when u copy this command, open on editor, delete the spaces between "kernel_image" & "kernel_headers" and then press space between the two words.. Them copy, paste and press return !

Sysc, A

### Post by b4tn on 2009-10-09
> **b4tn said:**
> I almost it all working. The update to the kernel seems good it boots and I can work my way around but for some reason it will not load the firmware for my bnx2 cards so I have no network. I found this article but I get a kernel panic when I try and modify the initrd file adding the bnx2 firmware.
 
[http://www.ducea.com/2009/03/02/debian-lenny-pxe-installation-on-dell-poweredge-19502950-servers-bnx2-annoyances/](http://www.ducea.com/2009/03/02/debian-lenny-pxe-installation-on-dell-poweredge-19502950-servers-bnx2-annoyances/)
 
 
Just incase someone else wants to do this on a Dell PE2950 I want to post the fix. I was getting the following firmware error
 
Cant load firmware file "bnx2/bnx2.mips-06-4.6.16.fw" 
 
It would then boot but with no network cards. I booted into the old kernel and and downloaded the broadcom 5708 drivers from broadcom. I then booted back into the new kernel and and did a make and make install after unzipping the drivers. Once they where installed I switched to /lib/modules/your kernel version/drivers/net and did sudo rmmod bnx2 then sudo insmod bnx2.ko.
 
This started the network drivers so I could do sudo apt-get install firmware-bnx2. after reboot everything loaded just fine.

### Post by sandy8925 on 2009-10-13
I have a question.I was able to compile the 2.6.31 kernel without any problems. It took far less than 4-5 hours. The only problem is the resulting .deb file was around 200 MB ! I was able to install it though and it worked very well. Someone else also said that they got a 400 MB deb package and that it was because debugging was enabled because of the "make oldconfig" step.   The thing is if debugging is there how do I disable it and how do I reduce the size ? 

Also, can anyone tell me how to figure out which modules I need and which I don't ? For example do I need the Amiga/Acorn/BeOS partition format support ?

---

### Post by TSJason on 2009-10-13
> **sandy8925 said:**
> I have a question.I was able to compile the 2.6.31 kernel without any problems. It took far less than 4-5 hours. The only problem is the resulting .deb file was around 200 MB ! I was able to install it though and it worked very well. Someone else also said that they got a 400 MB deb package and that it was because debugging was enabled because of the "make oldconfig" step.   The thing is if debugging is there how do I disable it and how do I reduce the size ? 

Also, can anyone tell me how to figure out which modules I need and which I don't ? For example do I need the Amiga/Acorn/BeOS partition format support ?

Sandy,

During the "make menuconfig" or "make xconfig" step you'll need to review the options which are enabled. If 'debugging' is turned on for any of the modules you will see it marked. Likewise, you'll want to check the "kernel hacking" section and for the "kernel debugging" option. I actually have everything in there turned off (with the exception of "Magic SysRq key") for my main machine since I just use it for work and won't be doing any debugging there. 

You very likely don't need those partition capabilities and if you did you would know. In order to really cut the fat out of your kernel you'll need to be familiar with the exact hardware you have installed and a general idea of your system configuration. I would suggest starting by thinking about your architecture (x86 or x86_64), reviewing the output of "lspci", the type of processor and amount of memory. Go from there and don't hesitate to ask questions. Be sure to check the "Help" option during "make menuconfig" if your curious about a particular module. There is usually a reference there to more info in the kernel documentation.

---

The problem is likely the modules have not been stripped for debug information: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3543026#post3543026](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3543026#post3543026)

### Post by dk75 on 2009-10-27
If you don't have "/usr/src/linux" then you can't delete it. So, whats left is to make it.

---

### Post by MonsterDuc on 2009-10-28
> **dk75 said:**
> If you don't have "/usr/.src/linux" then you can't delete it. So, whats left is to make it.

Understood that can be done, but why?  What does that do, instead of just leaving it with the current name?

---

### Post by dk75 on 2009-10-28
I'm guessing that Makefile inside linux kernel source dirctory have hardcoded path to itself and it could be trouble if "make" cannot find "/usr/src/linux" directory/link.
And symlinking is best/safest thing to do in that case.

As for deleting "/usr/src/linux" in first place... if you have that symlink then it minds that you have old kernel source there from "before". So... you want to compile new kernel not old right?

---

> **MonsterDuc said:**
> What is the difference between the kernel image and the headers explained in a line or two?
There are a lot of things selected in the kernel (i used menuconfig) that 
**Kernel Image** it is a binnary code of kernel and **Kernel Headers** are "C" include text files for using kernel code in external programs ( like nVidia kernel module ) durring compilation.

### Post by 565Customz on 2009-11-02
> **Jordanwb said:**
> Well for me I get two "ata 3.00 soft reset failed" warnings on boot. I can't fix this unless I compile a custom kernel.


well i keep getting an ecc warning and a crash report...is this something i can use to fix that?

this is the bug report that matches up with the kernel crash error im getting

Bug Description

The edac_amd64 module attempts to load on many systems. If the system doesn't have ECC
memory, or it is disabled in the BIOS, then the module prints

EDAC amd64: WARNING: ECC is NOT currently enabled by the BIOS. Module will NOT be loaded.
    Either Enable ECC in the BIOS, or use the 'ecc_enable_override' parameter.
    Might be a BIOS bug, if BIOS says ECC is enabled
    Use of the override can cause unknown side effects.

before failing to load. This won't cause issues for most people that see the message.

kerneloops picks this up as kernel problem as the "WARNING:" looks a lot like what
you get when using WARN().

There are several aspects to this issue:

  * linux
     - something attempts to load the module when perhaps it doesn't need to (unclear at this point)
     - the module looks like it is using WARN() when it isn't, and is arguably more SHOUTY than
       it needs to be.
  * kerneloops
     - picks up this as WARN() when it isn't.
     - Was accidentally left enabled for the stable release, people shouldn't be getting asked to report
        this at this point.
     - Isn't very good at dealing with repeat issues, causing more reports to be sent than are needed.

and possibly others as well.

### Post by TSJason on 2009-11-12
My guess is that:

a) you don't have an nvidia graphics card
and
b) you have an onboard graphics card which is claiming part of your system memory, so that memory isn't available for linux to use - hence the less that 4Gb being reported.

If you're not sure, check the output of "lspci |grep VGA" to see what sort of VGA Adapter you're running. If it's not by nvidia, just delete that postinstall script with "sudo rm /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common", then retry that dpkg command to install your kernel.

---

> My guess is that:

a) you don't have an nvidia graphics card
and
b) you have an onboard graphics card which is claiming part of your system memory, so that memory isn't available for linux to use - hence the less that 4Gb being reported.

If you're not sure, check the output of "lspci |grep VGA" to see what sort of VGA Adapter you're running. If it's not by nvidia, just delete that postinstall script with "sudo rm /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common", then retry that dpkg command to install your kernel.

### Post by TSJason on 2009-12-07
> **smoosh said:**
> 
When I paste in the first command - "sudo ./dsdt-patch", I get an error "sudo: ./dsdt-patch: command not found". Does anyone know either: 


The way this script works is that it applies itself against your current kernel and updates the initramfs image that already exists. So you need to have your newest kernel already built and installed before running this patch. 

You're probably getting the above error because you didn't set the script to be executable after you created it. Try running:

```

chmod u+x dsdt-patch

```

Then try again. HTH.

---

Errors were encountered while processing:
 linux-image-2.6.32-amd64

```

Is there any mention of "run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20" above what you have pasted here? If so, then I believe running this should solve it: "sudo apt-get remove --purge nvidia-common"
that did fix the installation for me, i'll be back when i've rebooted in to the kernel....

edit: worked fine! thanks a lot! saved me a couple of hours of recompiling=)

====== making target debian/stamp/install/linux-image-2.6.33-rc1-core2duo [new prereqs: ]======
This is kernel package version 11.015.

Please correct this.
make[1]: *** [debian/stamp/install/linux-image-2.6.33-rc1-core2duo] Error 2

```
Help please.

You can find an explanation here. [http://groups.google.com/group/linux.debian.bugs.dist/browse_thread/thread/747f97c8d2d116a4?fwc=1](http://groups.google.com/group/linux.debian.bugs.dist/browse_thread/thread/747f97c8d2d116a4?fwc=1)

In commit 92045954058671fdd0ccf031ca06611ce1d929d1¹, compile.h was moved
from include/linux to include/generated. 

You might have to pause before the making the package and move the compile.h to where make-kpkg thinks it should be in include/linux. I'm going to give it a try and see what blows up. :)

::UPDATE FYI::
Copying the compile.h from include/generated to include/linux allows the build to complete normally. I copied all the files but you can just do the compile.h if you're wary. This will do until deb/ubu do an update to address this bug I suppose.

### Post by Milos_SD on 2009-12-18
Thanks Dizzle7677. I'll try that right away.

Edit:

I found another way get around this, and it is a permanent. :)

Edit /usr/share/kernel-package/ruleset/misc/version_vars.mk

find:

```
UTS_RELEASE_HEADER=$(call doit,if [ -f include/linux/utsrelease.h ]; then  \
	                       echo include/linux/utsrelease.h;            \

```

and change it in:

```
UTS_RELEASE_HEADER=$(call doit,if [ -f include/generated/utsrelease.h ]; then  \
	                       echo include/generated/utsrelease.h;            \
```

Enjoy! :)

### Post by piperdown10 on 2010-01-07
I had the same error. It happens when you copy/paste the commands into terminal. When you highlight and copy this line:

```
INSTALL_MOD_STRIP=1 CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-mk 
kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image
```

There's a line break between *kernel_image* and *kernel_headers*. What happens is, when you copy the code it copies the line break and Terminal thinks it's an 'enter' command. So it doesn't create the *kernel_headers* or *modules_image*s.

The only way I've found to fix it is to recompile with the correct commands in place. (You'll need to type that line by hand.)

Hope this helps!

---

```

There's a line break between *kernel_image* and *kernel_headers*. What happens is, when you copy the code it copies the line break and Terminal thinks it's an 'enter' command. So it doesn't create the *kernel_headers* or *modules_image*s.

The only way I've found to fix it is to recompile with the correct commands in place. (You'll need to type that line by hand.)

Hope this helps!

### Post by prasys on 2010-01-19
Here is how to fix the UTS error. Its very simple

I'll write this in very basic terms , giving the fullest explanation on what each step does 

1. Navigate to /home/<username>/ubuntu/linux-2.6/debian/ruleset/misc/ or wherever you've the sources stored

2. Now fire up Terminal and then type 

sudo gedit or sudo kate (if you want and then drag and drop version_vars.mk file or type its path. Its your call or you may issue sudo gedit and open the file via Gedit or via Kate. Few options here 

Look for UTS_RELEASE_HEADER=$( , and change it to something like which I've showed below 
```

UTS_RELEASE_HEADER=$(call doit,if [ -f include/generated/utsrelease.h ]; then  \
	                       echo include/generated/utsrelease.h;            \
	                   else                                            \
                               echo include/generated/utsrelease.h ;              \
	                   fi)
```

The important thing to change is include/linux to include/generated and version.h to utsrelease.h

Thats about it , recompile and thats it. 

Thanks goes out to this lad :- [http://www.mail-archive.com/ubuntu-devel-discuss@lists.ubuntu.com/msg10203.html](http://www.mail-archive.com/ubuntu-devel-discuss@lists.ubuntu.com/msg10203.html) and
[http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=377267](http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=377267)

Cheers !

Basically to sum it up change include/linux to include/generated in version_vars.mk and replace version.h with utsrelease.h

Edit : Didn't realise someone has already posted a solution. This is the noob-friendly version on how to edit things for those who can't figure out on how to edit it.

---

[http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=377267](http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=377267)

Cheers !

Basically to sum it up change include/linux to include/generated in version_vars.mk and replace version.h with utsrelease.h

Edit : Didn't realise someone has already posted a solution. This is the noob-friendly version on how to edit things for those who can't figure out on how to edit it.

The UTS Release version in include/generated/utsrelease.h
       "" 
does not match current version:
       "2.6.32.7-dude" 
```
someone who's got an idea? this has been since kernel 2.6.32-4...

### Post by Klondikes on 2010-02-15
i follow and reference your master kernel thread post frequently. i must say, this post is priceless! thank you!

i was wondering, why not use fakeroot for the make-kpkg command? use of root to compile code is considered unwise, possibly dangerous, etc.

here are some discussions on the use of root to compile code:
[http://www.mail-archive.com/debian-user@lists.debian.org/msg332985.html]("http://www.mail-archive.com/debian-user@lists.debian.org/msg332985.html")
[http://www.owlriver.com/tips/non-root/]("http://www.owlriver.com/tips/non-root/")

by using fakeroot, users can compile the kernel and create the debian packages without root privileges.

one benefit to this is the ability to store the linux source in their home directory. e.g., i now have my source in ~/src.

the changes to the guide would be

1. add **fakeroot** to the build command:
```

INSTALL_MOD_STRIP=1 CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 **fakeroot** make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-mk kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image 
```

2. remove the unnecessary command:
```
sudo -s
```

3. use sudo to install the new kernel packages
```
cd .. && sudo dpkg -i linux*2.6.32*.deb
```

4. since /usr/src is a root owned directory, change appropriate paths to ~/src (or similar)

thoughts?

### Post by Klondikes on 2010-02-17
going on a guess here...

i'm guessing this nvidia-common junk running during "dpkg -i" is the nvidia driver failing to install? :P
> run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20

obviously you're using nvidia drivers; do you use the proprietary drivers Ubuntu distributes? or do you download the drivers from nvidia's website and install manually (my method)?

perhaps if you're using Ubuntu's distributed version, try uninstalling/disabling the nvidia drivers, then installing the kernel debian packages, and finally, reinstalling/enabling the nvidia drivers... ??

however, i recommend installing the nvidia drivers manually, unless you're already doing this :-/

### Post by Klondikes on 2010-02-18
> **Lockheed said:**
> I am using Nvidias drivers installed manually which I need to reinstall for every new kernel. Can you advice something in this case?

Perhaps...

are/were you not using an old version of nvidia drivers (180)?? the latest is 190.x. however, i suppose it may depend upon your graphics card. if i remember correctly, the 180.x drivers failed to install on 9.04. perhaps the problem is nvidia driver residue (see links below)

more interestingly, the output of my kernel installations never mentions anything about nvidia*. DKMS output runs through only the VirtualBox drivers as shown below: 

```
Examining /etc/kernel/postinst.d.
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/dkms
 * Running DKMS auto installation service for kernel 2.6.31-20-core2
 *  vboxdrv (3.1.4)...           vboxdrv (3.1.4): Already installed on this kernel.
                                 [ OK ]
 *  vboxnetadp (3.1.4)...        vboxnetadp (3.1.4): Already installed on this kernel.
                                 [ OK ]
 *  vboxnetflt (3.1.4)...        vboxnetflt (3.1.4): Already installed on this kernel.
                                 [ OK ]

Setting up linux-headers-2.6.31-20 (2.6.31-20.57) ...
Setting up linux-headers-2.6.31-20-core2 (2.6.31-20.57) ...
```

are you positive the nvidia drivers distributed by Ubuntu are uninstalled/deactivated? have you used them in the past? did you perhaps modify something (in the past or recently) to automatically install the nvidia drivers upon kernel installation? are the nvidia drivers properly removed (see links below)?

by manually install nvidia drivers, i mean this:
1. install kernel: dpkg -i ...
2. reboot
3. notice X/kdm/gdm fails to start b/c nvidia drivers are not installed
3. log in CLI mode and run sudo sh ./NVIDIA_drivers_190.53.sh ...
4. reboot
5. ???
6. profit

if you haven't killed google yet, try googleing "DKMS tree must be manually fixed". here are two results that look semi-promising:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-restricted-modules-envy-2.6.24/+bug/261816]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-restricted-modules-envy-2.6.24/+bug/261816")
[http://www.mail-archive.com/ubuntu-bugs@lists.ubuntu.com/msg1011629.html]("http://www.mail-archive.com/ubuntu-bugs@lists.ubuntu.com/msg1011629.html")

another tip: try installing this "problematic" kernel on a separate physical or virtual machine on top of a fresh install of 9.04 (or whatever distro you're working with) to prove the custom kernel is OK.

if all else fails and you do not have a spare machine or VMs are impossible, save a backup copy the kernel you built, reinstall 9.04, install updates, then install the custom kernel.

### Post by dk75 on 2010-02-19
> **Lockheed said:**
> EDIT:
Just thought about something - when I am compiling the kernel, should I disable "Nvidia Framebuffer" since I am going to install new nvidia's drivers manually anyway?

nVidia proprietary drivers are in conflict with nVidia FB driver - nVidia developer stated that when users asked about possibilty of running both drivers at the same time (FB in console, bin in X).
When compiled as module it is a chance that it isn't started by anything so there is no conflict (sheer luck).

### Post by Lockheed on 2010-02-21
> **Lockheed said:**
> Just tried - same story.

Progress!

I manually removed unused directories from dkms folder, installed newest dkms deb and successfully compiled and installed newest kernel.

**HOWEVER**

after installation of the kernel DEB files, the new initrd.img file is not being created! 

**EDIT:**
I managed to manually create the missing file with
```
update-initramfs -c -k 2.6.33-rc8-git5-gym
```
and much to my delight, the kernel boots and is working fine now.

Still, would be good to know why the file was not created automatically.

### Post by Lockheed on 2010-02-27
I managed to fix all problems by replacing /etc/kernel-img.conf
with this one:
[http://paste.ubuntu.com/148419/](http://paste.ubuntu.com/148419/)

However, I still need to manually
```
update-initramfs -c -k 2.6.3......
```
to get the img file.

### Post by Klondikes on 2010-03-09
I would start by uninstalling VirtualBox and then download the latest version of VirtualBox 3.1.4 from [http://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Linux_Downloads]("http://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Linux_Downloads").

---

### Post by Chikitulfo on 2010-03-11
It worked!
I thought I was using the last version...

---

### Post by Klondikes on 2010-03-11
VirtualBox should have added a line to /etc/apt/sources.list such as

```
deb http://download.virtualbox.org/virtualbox/debian karmic non-free #virtualbox
```

With that line in sources.list, you should be notified of any VirtualBox updates when you update your system through apt.

### Post by master_kernel on 2010-03-30
> **Klondikes said:**
> i follow and reference your master kernel thread post frequently. i must say, this post is priceless! thank you!

i was wondering, why not use fakeroot for the make-kpkg command? use of root to compile code is considered unwise, possibly dangerous, etc.

here are some discussions on the use of root to compile code:
[http://www.mail-archive.com/debian-user@lists.debian.org/msg332985.html]("http://www.mail-archive.com/debian-user@lists.debian.org/msg332985.html")
[http://www.owlriver.com/tips/non-root/]("http://www.owlriver.com/tips/non-root/")

by using fakeroot, users can compile the kernel and create the debian packages without root privileges.

one benefit to this is the ability to store the linux source in their home directory. e.g., i now have my source in ~/src.

the changes to the guide would be

1. add **fakeroot** to the build command:
```

INSTALL_MOD_STRIP=1 CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=3 **fakeroot** make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-mk kernel_image kernel_headers modules_image 
```

2. remove the unnecessary command:
```
sudo -s
```

3. use sudo to install the new kernel packages
```
cd .. && sudo dpkg -i linux*2.6.32*.deb
```

4. since /usr/src is a root owned directory, change appropriate paths to ~/src (or similar)

thoughts?
Noted.

### Post by master_kernel on 2010-04-27
Looks like missing SATA drivers in the kernel. You have to recompile with SATA drivers enabled - you probably disabled them by accident.

---

### Post by wbar2 on 2010-04-28
That's odd, because I just tried this again on another machine and I made absolutely sure I changed none of the options. Is my current config file not being copied properly? I'm using KernelCheck.

EDIT: I also tried on my laptop again, making sure to enable SATA options. It booted once, but after I restarted I got locked out with all kinds of errors. I'm missing something here.

EDIT-**_[COLOR="Red"]SOLVED[/COLOR]_**:)!: For some reason when I follow the instructions on this thread, or when I use KernelCheck an initrd image is not generated. Therefore none of my modules would load, including SATA. I ran this in terminal and now 2.6.33.3 is up and running one at least one machine:
```

sudo update-initramfs -c -k 2.6.33.3-mk
```

I found that it may actually be related to a bug? [https://bugs.launchpad.net/initramfs-tools/+bug/220094](https://bugs.launchpad.net/initramfs-tools/+bug/220094)

---

### Post by maulteez on 2010-05-09
I just compiled the latest kernel using Kernelcheck on a new install of lucid and it completed without errors but on reboot I get the error "kernel panic-not syncing:vfs:unable to mount root fs on unknown-block (0,0)". I tried updating initramfs as suggested in post #1449 but still get the same error. Any suggestions?

EDIT: I found the solution to this myself. After making the initramfs as suggested in post #1449 you have to: > sudo /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig
 sudo update-grub
This updates the file grub.cfg

### Post by BT1 on 2010-05-22
Alright, for a while I was unable to compile a kernel and apply it but now I am able to do it, and this is how I am doing it:

1.) I downloaded the program installer found here:
[http://launchpadlibrarian.net/44725226/kernelcheck_1.2.5-4_all.deb](http://launchpadlibrarian.net/44725226/kernelcheck_1.2.5-4_all.deb)

2.) I installed it.

3.) Updated Kernel information (click the button)

4.) Then did the typical install (top option I believe)

5.) Waited for it to get all the packages and stuff.

6.) Picked a couple of options such as processor type, I/O Scheduler

7.) Saved, then quit (this makes the program auto-configure and install the kernel)

8.) This is where most of these installs fail because of the new intramfs(?) tools not automatically updating. So I looked around in these forums and found out that I needed to do these things before restarting my computer:

a.) At the end of the terminal screen when it is done installing, note what the name of the new kernel is. When I compiled it was 2.6.33.4-candela so I ran this command:

```
sudo update-initramfs -c -k 2.6.33.4-candela
```b.) When that was done, I did this:

```
sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postinst.d/initramfs  /etc/kernel/postinst.d/ 
sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postrm.d/initramfs  /etc/kernel/postrm.d/
```c.) Lastly, I ran this code:

```
sudo /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig
 sudo update-grub                      
```After it updated the grub, I restarted my computer and booted into the new kernel, and it works flawlessly. Now I may have done one step extra that is uneeded, but this is the formula that has been working for all 5 of my PC/Laptops, from Linux Mint, to Lubuntu and everything inbetween.

Just thought I'd share.

---
