### Post by skkeeper on 2010-05-30
After compiling and instaling the kernel can I safely delete the /usr/src/linux contents? They just take like gigs and gigs of space on my laptop : |

Thanks for this awesome always up-to-date post

---

### Post by master_kernel on 2010-05-31
> **skkeeper said:**
> After compiling and instaling the kernel can I safely delete the /usr/src/linux contents? They just take like gigs and gigs of space on my laptop : |

Thanks for this awesome always up-to-date post
You can, just make sure you re-link the headers!

(Substitue 2.x.x with your kernel version)
```

ln -s /usr/src/linux-headers-2.x.x /lib/modules/2.x.x/source
ln -s /usr/src/linux-headers-2.x.x /lib/modules/2.x.x/build
```

---

### Post by meklu on 2010-07-02
> **masux594 said:**
> No, it's a common problem.. I'm not sure that  meklu's post is the solution.. someone  else has experienced?

Sysc, A

Well, if that doesn't work for you, try adding "initramfs" after the last slash. (Without the quotes, of course.) In step 6 it is trying to create a file called "postrm.d" in the directory "/etc/kernel/" but it can't take the folder's name because it's already reserved. If you, however place "initramfs" at the line's end, you'll be able to create the file (if it doesn't already exist, which can be overridden with the -f operand).
**EDIT:** Step 6 has a space in the end of the line doing "postinst" stuff, so just adding a space should work in any occasion.

---

### Post by tormod on 2010-07-02
> **BongoBob said:**
> I too am getting a problem on step 6. The first line of step 6 works fine. The second line, however, returns this: ```
sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postrm.d/initramfs  /etc/kernel/postrm.d/
cp: cannot create regular file `/etc/kernel/postrm.d/': Is a directory

```

Any thoughts?

The error message is quite misleading. The command is intended to copy a file to a directory, keeping the same file name. Since the directory does not exist on your system, cp believes your second argument (target or destination) is the new file name. However since the file name ends with / it complains that your "file" name looks like a directory name and it can not create a file with that name.

A more intuitive error message would be "directory does not exist". But cp does not reason like that :) It does not take the trailing slash as a hint that you meant to copy to a directory. But adding the trailing slash to a target directory name is a good habit for us humans, since in the case the directory does not exist, cp will bail out (like here) instead of happily copying the file and give it the name of your "imagined" directory.

Anyway, the easy solution is to just create the missing directory:
```
sudo mkdir /etc/kernel/postrm.d
```

(You could add the trailing slash, would not make a difference). BTW, there is nothing magic about some whitespace at the end of the line.

### Post by masux594 on 2010-07-06
> **thahir1986 said:**
> Is it possible to remove the Old kernel and its device drivers ?

Of course that y can remove the some unused kernels.. You need to remove two packages:

linux-headers-2.6.xxxxx and the linux-image-2.6.xxxxx.. if u want to see what kernel are y currently use y type  ```
uname -r
```.. So y can see the current kernel version [[COLOR=#000000]that y must not remove[/COLOR] of course].. 
the type ```
sudo apt-get remove linux-(+double tab)
``` and the system list all the linux-image and linux-headers that u have currently installed.. So remove the couples 
"linux-headers-2.6.xxxxxxx" "linux-image-2.6.xxxxxxx" with the command ```
 sudo apt-get remove linux-headers-2.6.xxxx linux-image-2.6.xxxx
``` (replace "xx.." with the package listed before)

### Post by jwach on 2010-07-26
> **zackiv31 said:**
> Can someone help me with this error... 10.04 x86_64, following these directions: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild)

After a couple hours after executing step 8, I get the following **not in control info** error

```


install -p    -o root -g root  -m  644 ./debian/templates.master /home/zivester/linux-2.6/debian/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+/DEBIAN/templates
dpkg-gencontrol -DArchitecture=amd64 -isp         \
            -plinux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+ -P/home/zivester/linux-2.6/debian/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+/
dpkg-gencontrol: error: package linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+ not in control info
make[2]: *** [debian/stamp/binary/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+] Error 255
make[2]: Leaving directory `/home/zivester/linux-2.6'
make[1]: *** [debian/stamp/binary/pre-linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+] Error 2
make[1]: Leaving directory `/home/zivester/linux-2.6'
make: *** [kernel_image] Error 2

```

I got the same error. I'm not sure of a cause or a real fix, but it has to do with the plus sign being added to the end of the kernel name. I was able to find a workaround, but it isn't ideal at all, and there is probably a better way.

You have to run make-kpkg again (i.e. repeat step 8 at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild)) and then, **while it is running** (and before it returns to the point of the error), edit the debian config file at ~/linux-2.6/debian/control. In that file, replace every instance of "linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+"; replace every instance of "linux-source-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-source-2.6.35-rc5-custom+"; and replace every instance of "linux-headers-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-headers-2.6.35-rc5-custom+". 

The trouble with this is that make-kpkg will automatically regenerate the debian/control file, so you have to watch and be sure that you edit that file after it does.

Anybody know a better solution?

install -p    -o root -g root  -m  644 ./debian/templates.master /home/zivester/linux-2.6/debian/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+/DEBIAN/templates
dpkg-gencontrol -DArchitecture=amd64 -isp	     \
			-plinux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+ -P/home/zivester/linux-2.6/debian/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+/
dpkg-gencontrol: error: package linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+ not in control info
make[2]: *** [debian/stamp/binary/linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+] Error 255
make[2]: Leaving directory `/home/zivester/linux-2.6'

make[1]: Leaving directory `/home/zivester/linux-2.6'
make: *** [kernel_image] Error 2

```

You might want to omit the plus sign from your command or escape it (put a backslash in front of it, ie. \+). I'm not sure whether or not the latter method works, so try the other first.

### Post by emshains on 2010-08-01
I apologize if I am not the first one to mention this, but the kernel compiles heck of a lot faster if you use make -j 2 (or 4 or whatever number of cores/cpu's you've got/are willing to use). Isn't there a way to do this while making a .deb package ? I hope I made sense.

---

### Post by darkdragn on 2010-08-02
> **emshains said:**
> I apologize if I am not the first one to mention this, but the kernel compiles heck of a lot faster if you use make -j 2 (or 4 or whatever number of cores/cpu's you've got/are willing to use). Isn't there a way to do this while making a .deb package ? I hope I made sense.

export CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=`getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN` 
Will set the compile to use your maximum number of currently available processors, or just 

export CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=$WhateverNumberYouWant #not to exceed your current number of available processors

In the guide it defines this value before doing the compile process, within the same line

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild)
|||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||
\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\//////////////////////////
################################################################
CONCURRENCY_LEVEL=`getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN` fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-custom kernel_image kernel_headers
################################################################

---

### Post by darkdragn on 2010-08-05
> **jwach said:**
> I got the same error. I'm not sure of a cause or a real fix, but it has to do with the plus sign being added to the end of the kernel name. I was able to find a workaround, but it isn't ideal at all, and there is probably a better way.
 
You have to run make-kpkg again (i.e. repeat step 8 at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/GitKernelBuild)) and then, **while it is running** (and before it returns to the point of the error), edit the debian config file at ~/linux-2.6/debian/control. In that file, replace every instance of "linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-image-2.6.35-rc5-custom+"; replace every instance of "linux-source-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-source-2.6.35-rc5-custom+"; and replace every instance of "linux-headers-2.6.35-rc5-custom" with "linux-headers-2.6.35-rc5-custom+". 
 
The trouble with this is that make-kpkg will automatically regenerate the debian/control file, so you have to watch and be sure that you edit that file after it does.
 
Anybody know a better solution?
 
This is going to sound weird... but in newer kernels, when doing the make $AnyOfTheConfigTypes (EX: make menuconfig) you have an option to embed a subversion string, with the Ubuntu kernel compile options, this is not supported. And that's where the error is. If you do use make menuconfig, under general configuration (like the third option down) Blank that out. Instead, in the example:
 
fakeroot make-kpkg --initrd --append-to-version=-some-string-here kernel-image kernel-headers
 
The --append-to-version= is what you will want for the information to show up on the kernel, and --revision= will apply to the .deb revision number. If you blank that string in your make $ConfigType and instead put that string in --append-to-version it will compile without any issues.

kernel includes at /lib/modules/2.6.34-mk/build/include do not match current kernel.
they are versioned as ""
instead of "2.6.34-mk".
you might need to adjust your symlinks:
- /usr/include
- /usr/src/linux

What does this mean? What do I do?

### Post by emshains on 2010-08-11
FYI, if you are trying to compile 2.6.32 with the bfs patch, your wi-fi won't work, compiling 2.6.34 with bfs patch works.

### Post by ThirdKind on 2010-09-11
Hello,

I have been trying to install the kernel mentioned above on 9.04....I run to an error on the line 'sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postinst.d/initramfs /etc/kernel/postinst.d/', because there is no such directory. The file tree terminates at /etc/
with only 5 single files in there, non of them being the target files. Were the files meant to be copied during the previous lines or are the folders naturally in a different location with 9.04?

Would love some help, I have tried a few different approaches. My aim is to get a kernel/kernel tree above 2.6 to allow development of drivers for external hardware. So if anybody can give advice on how to get a development system up and running I would be grateful. 
Thanks.

### Post by LinUxaliVe on 2011-01-11
> **master_kernel said:**
> [SIZE=2]A. Note to everyone: The append-to-version option has absolutely nothing to do with the kernel at all. It is simply a short title for your kernel. 64 bit users can even put --append-to-version=64 or don't even include the append-to-version at all. 64-bit users can enable 64-bit options in the kernel under Processors.[/SIZE]

Does this mean that by selecting the processor type, for say specific/generic 686 support, you get a 686 optimized kernel?

[SIZE=2]Adding this to the make-kpkg command saved me some hassle, 'modules_image'. A lot of other tutorials skip right over this, thanks.
[/SIZE]

### Post by M4570D0N on 2011-03-08
Ok, well I decided to try compiling my first kernel with KernelChecker and I have run into some problems. I'm currently running kernel 2.6.36 on Linux Mint 10 on this partition. I was attempting to compile the 2.6.38rc7 kernel. First, I was able to grab a few warnings I saw during the compiling process. This is incomplete but it was all I was able to copy down. (Note: some may be paraphrased)

```
mkpiggy.c.65: warning ignoring return value of 'fread' with attribut warn_unused_result

Warning modpost: Found 2 section mismatch. see details build kernel with" male CONFIG_DEBUG_SECTION_MISMATCH=y

via-core.c684: warning: assignment discards qualifiers from pointer target type
LD [M] drivers/video/wia/viafb.o
    Building modules, stage 2
    MODPOST 2724 modules
WARNING: modpost: Found 41 section mismatches

```When the process completed I also noticed that it ended with these two lines:
```
cat: /etc/X11/xorg.conf: No such file or directory!
cat: /etc/X11/xorg.conf: No such file or directory!
```I was not sure if that was going to be an issue because it didn't seem to say the compile aborted or failed. 

When I installed the two .deb files I saw this message several times:
```
W: Possible missing firmware /lib/firmware/rtl_nic/rtl8168d-2.fw for module r8169
```But after looking for r8169 in other kernels all I found was this:
```
/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.36-020636-generic/include/config/r8169
/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.36-020636-generic/include/config/r8169.h
/usr/src/linux-headers-2.6.36-020636-generic/include/config/r8169/vlan.h
```Both r8169.h and vlan.h are 0 byte 0 text C headers that, as far as I can tell, are just empty files that don't do anything, so I didn't worry about it. (I know now that it's the ethernet kernel/driver. I never use ethernet though)

I updated initramfs and grub  (received the same firmware warning) and then rebooted.
When I got to the grub screen I did not see it listed, so I booted back into the kernel I was using before. I saw the the linux-headers-2.6.38-rc7-candela directory, and I swear it was completely filled with empty files at first, but looking again several minutes later it appears that everything is there. I also noticed that there was now a directory called "linux-2.6.37" and a "patch-2.6.38-rc7.bz2" archive that appeared from who knows where, in addition to the *.38.rc7 kernel directory and the "Linux" directory. Also, the linux-2.6.37 directory looks like a kernel directory but it has about twice as many items listed within it than any other kernel directory (57 items vs 24-29 items). 

It was about this time that I realized that the Natty Alpha-3 that I installed the other day on another partition overwrote grub and that was why it wasn't showing up in grub. I thought I successfully reverted back to the Mint grub and thought everything was starting to go well until I finally booted the new kernel. After selecting it from the grub menu I had a large plain text dump onto the screen for about 3-5 seconds and then it froze (with my caps lock LED flashing). I could not print screen but I did take a crappy cell phone picture.
[[IMG]http://i596.photobucket.com/albums/tt45/SH00TTH3H0STAG3/th_0308110922-00.jpg[/IMG]]("http://i596.photobucket.com/albums/tt45/SH00TTH3H0STAG3/0308110922-00.jpg")

What is all this jazz? How might I go about correcting this? Any assistance is much appreciated.

more info:

### Post by davidst on 2011-03-25
hi, thanks for the writeup!

i just compiled 2.6.38.1 and here were some problems that happened:

* the kernel didn't want to compile first (edit: nevermind! it was because i was using the "libre" kernel sources from [http://linux-libre.fsfla.org/](http://linux-libre.fsfla.org/))

* once compiled, installing the debs resulted in this error:

```
Setting up linux-image-2.6.38.1-libre (2.6.38.1-libre-10.00.Custom) ...
Running depmod.
Examining /etc/kernel/postinst.d.
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/dkms 2.6.38.1-libre /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38.1-libre
 * dkms: running auto installation service for kernel 2.6.38.1-libre

 *       vboxhost (4.0.4)...
   ...done.
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/initramfs 2.6.38.1-libre /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38.1-libre
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common 2.6.38.1-libre /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38.1-libre
run-parts: /etc/kernel/postinst.d/nvidia-common exited with return code 20
Failed to process /etc/kernel/postinst.d at /var/lib/dpkg/info/linux-image-2.6.38.1-libre.postinst line 341.
dpkg: error processing linux-image-2.6.38.1-libre (--configure):
 subprocess installed post-installation script returned error exit status 2
Errors were encountered while processing:
 linux-image-2.6.38.1-libre
Press return to continue.
```after purging the nvidia-common package it worked.

* CONFIG_SCHED_AUTOGROUP is off by default. maybe this could be mentioned in the post.

Begin: Waiting for root file system...
```

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-06-03
Hi. I've never done this before but I have 10.04 and a Sandy bridge mobo so thought I'd try my luck at upgrading to 2.6.38

All went according to plan except that I had to purge nvidia-common and I got a few "kernel crash notifications" along the way.
Then I still got these warnings:
```
Setting up linux-image-2.6.38-mk (2.6.38-mk-10.00.Custom) ...
Running depmod.
Examining /etc/kernel/postinst.d.
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/dkms 2.6.38-mk /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-mk
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/initramfs 2.6.38-mk /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-mk
W: Possible missing firmware /lib/firmware/rtl_nic/rtl8168d-2.fw for module r8169
W: Possible missing firmware /lib/firmware/rtl_nic/rtl8168d-1.fw for module r8169
run-parts: executing /etc/kernel/postinst.d/pm-utils 2.6.38-mk /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-mk
Running postinst hook script update-grub.
Generating grub.cfg ...
Found background image: gnome-pca.png
Found linux image: /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-mk
Found initrd image: /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-mk
Found linux image: /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-32-generic
Found initrd image: /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-32-generic
Found memtest86+ image: /memtest86+.bin
Found Windows 7 (loader) on /dev/sda1
done
```
Are they important?

Then when I rebooted it dropped to a text prompt, complaining it couldn't find the nvidia drivers.

> IMPORTANT: IF YOU HAVE AN NVIDIA OR ATI GRAPHICS CARD, YOU MAY HAVE TO REINSTALL THE DRIVERS FOR IT.

I see. Unfortunately, I don't know how to action this.

[update]
I got it working. It was a roller-coaster ride and I actually stopped my previous kernel from booting at one point...it produced the Ubuntu splash screen then went black and became completely unresponsive. Meanwhile, 2.6.38 would not run gdm at all. I did a lot of Googling, installed nvidia drivers from their site, had a lot of kernel crash reports, then uninstalled nvidia and to cut a long story short I managed to purge nvidia from the system and now both boot properly. As soon as I have the courage to reinstall nvidia drivers I'll post the result.

I don't know what I am doing here, and I am in no position to advise anything. But my advice to similar ignoramuses is to uninstall all nvidia drivers before you carry out the new kernel procedure. 

I haven't resolved those warnings, but I gather they are something to do with Realtek. I haven't noticed any ill effects so far.

So I am running 10.04 with 2.6.38! What fun. :)

Note to OP: one thing I forgot that failed in your instructions, the second line of
```
sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postinst.d/initramfs  /etc/kernel/postinst.d/ 
sudo cp /usr/share/kernel-package/examples/etc/kernel/postrm.d/initramfs  /etc/kernel/postrm.d/
```
failed because the /postrm.d directory did not exist. I created it.

[update]
Running the new kernel I used the System/Admin/HWdrivers utility to reinstall the nvidia recommended drivers. This failed to complete and I got several kernel crash notifications. Something is very broken now. I ended up doing 'sudo apt-get -f install' to identify the 3 broken nvidia packages and I removed them. Then I booted in rescue mode and ran the driver install shell script that I downloaded from nvidia's website. It warned me that my distro config didn't want it to install but offered me the option to install anyway, so I did (that'll show it!). It then complained about the "nouveau" module and offered me a nouveau module disabling module and I accepted. It then installed and all has been well in 2.6.38. Actually, the nvidia drivers are better than the stock Ubuntu ones that I used before as I get a control panel customized to my graphics card.

But my old kernel, 2.6.32 complains on boot about the drivers and only offers low graphics mode.

Note: upgrading to the new kernel and/or the nvidia problems broke my VirtualBox. It would not launch. So I had to remove it and reinstall it. Now ok.

I'm using the new kernel now and it seems stable enough. Music works, video works, RAID works.

**I would be grateful for anyone's comments regarding gotchas of running an old distro (10.04) with a new kernel (2.6.38 ). Is this generally considered an ok thing to do?**

---

### Post by dk75 on 2011-08-01
As for nVidia, you must login to new kernel and only ther you must/could install nVidia kernel - from now on you must install nVidia *.run script from nVidia site and none other.

Or ditch KernelCheck and search for new kernel at PPA - this kernel works with drivers from Ubuntu and you don't need to install manually anything.

As for UbuntuTweak - it could show only kernel that was developed by Ubuntu team.

yes, you must compile everything again
coretemp, gfx driver, wifi drivers (if not from kernel itself)
